topics.title: Clostridioides Difficile Infection Cdi
topics.html:
Related: [[Antibiotic-Associated Diarrhoea]], [[Sepsis and Septic Shock]], [[Inflammatory Bowel Disease]], [[Antimicrobial Stewardship]]

[!important]
CDI = toxin-mediated diarrhoea/colitis from C. difficile overgrowth after antibiotic disruption of normal flora. Diagnosis: toxin EIA/GDH + PCR or NAAT (2-step algorithm). Severity: non-severe, severe, fulminant. Treatment: Vancomycin 125mg PO 6h ×10d (1st line); Fidaxomicin 200mg PO 12h ×10d (if recurrence risk); Metronidazole ONLY if no access to vancomycin/fidaxomicin (non-severe only). Recurrence: 15–30%; treat with fidaxomicin or vancomycin taper/pulse. FMT for multiple recurrences. IPC: contact precautions, sporicidal cleaning, antibiotic stewardship.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Define CDI diagnostic criteria (clinical + laboratory)
  • Apply 2-step testing algorithm (GDH/EIA → PCR/NAAT)
  • Assess severity (non-severe, severe, fulminant)
  • Select first-line treatment by severity
  • Manage first recurrence and multiple recurrences
  • Understand faecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) indications
  • Implement infection prevention and control measures
  • Recognise fulminant CDI requiring surgery

2. Pathogenesis & Risk Factors

FactorMechanism
Antibiotics (clindamycin, fluoroquinolones, cephalosporins, penicillins, carbapenems)Disrupt normal anaerobic flora → C. difficile overgrowth
Age ≥65Immunosenescence, comorbidity, healthcare exposure
Hospitalisation / LTCFAcquisition pressure, environmental spores
PPI useGastric acid barrier loss (controversial, modest risk)
ImmunosuppressionChemo, steroids, biologics, transplant, HIV
GI surgery / IleusStasis, loss of colonisation resistance
Renal failureAltered microbiome, healthcare exposure
Prior CDIRecurrence risk 15–30% after 1st episode
[!tip]
Highest risk antibiotics: clindamycin > fluoroquinolones > cephalosporins > penicillins. Ribotype 027/NAP1/BI = hypervirulent, increased toxin production, fluoroquinolone resistance, higher severity/recurrence.

3. Clinical Features

PresentationFeatures
Mild/Non-severeWatery diarrhoea ≥3/day, mild abdominal cramps, no systemic toxicity
SevereDiarrhoea ≥3/day + WCC >15×10⁹/L OR creatinine >1.5×baseline OR temp ≥38.5°C
FulminantHypotension/shock, ileus, toxic megacolon, perforation, lactate ↑, WCC >35 or <2, lactate >5
Asymptomatic carriagePositive test, no diarrhoea — DO NOT TREAT
[!warning]
Ileus = diarrhoea may be ABSENT in fulminant CDI (toxic megacolon). Abdominal distension, pain, tachycardia, leukopenia/leukocytosis, lactate ↑ = surgical emergency.

4. Diagnosis — 2-Step Algorithm (Recommended)

Step 1: Screen (High Sensitivity)

TestTargetSensitivitySpecificityTurnaround
GDH EIAGlutamate dehydrogenase (enzyme produced by all C. difficile)High (~95%)Moderate1–2h
Toxin A/B EIAToxins A and BLow (~70–80%)High (>95%)1–2h
Combined GDH + Toxin EIABothModerateHigh1–2h

Step 2: Confirm (If Step 1 Discordant or Positive)

TestTargetSensitivitySpecificity
NAAT / PCRtcdB (toxin B gene)Very high (>95%)High (>95%)
Toxigenic cultureViable toxin-producing organismGold standardGold standard

Algorithm

Diarrhoea (≥3 loose stools/24h) + clinical suspicion
         ↓
   Step 1: GDH EIA + Toxin A/B EIA
         ↓
   GDH− / Toxin−       → CDI EXCLUDED (NPV >99%)
   GDH+ / Toxin+       → CDI CONFIRMED (PPV >95%)
   GDH+ / Toxin−       → **Step 2: NAAT/PCR**
         ↓
   NAAT+               → CDI CONFIRMED
   NAAT−               → CDI EXCLUDED
[!key]
NAAT alone NOT recommended as stand-alone (low PPV in low prevalence = overdiagnosis). Toxigenic culture = reference standard but slow (48–72h). Test only if ≥3 unformed stools/24h.

5. Severity Assessment

CategoryCriteria
Non-severeWCC ≤15, Creatinine ≤1.5×baseline, Temp <38.5°C, No organ failure
SevereWCC >15×10⁹/L OR Creatinine >1.5×baseline OR Temp ≥38.5°C
FulminantHypotension/shock, Ileus, Toxic megacolon, Perforation, Lactate >5, WCC >35 or <2

6. Treatment by Severity

First Episode

SeverityPreferredAlternativeNotes
Non-severeVancomycin 125mg PO 6h ×10dFidaxomicin 200mg PO 12h ×10dMetronidazole NO LONGER 1st line (inferior cure, ↑ recurrence)
SevereVancomycin 125mg PO 6h ×10dFidaxomicin 200mg PO 12h ×10dHigh-dose vancomycin 500mg 6h NOT superior
FulminantVancomycin 500mg PO/NG 6h + Metronidazole 500mg IV 8hFidaxomicin + Metronidazole IVSurgical consult early; consider IV tigecycline if ileus
[!critical]
Oral vancomycin NOT absorbed systemically — achieves high colonic concentrations. IV vancomycin does NOT treat CDI. Fidaxomicin: narrower spectrum, less microbiome disruption, lower recurrence (if available). Metronidazole: only if vancomycin/fidaxomicin unavailable AND non-severe.

First Recurrence

OptionRegimen
PreferredFidaxomicin 200mg PO 12h ×10d (lowest recurrence)
AlternativeVancomycin taper/pulse: 125mg 6h ×10d → 125mg 12h ×7d → 125mg 24h ×7d → 125mg every 48h ×7d → 125mg every 72h ×7d

Second/Subsequent Recurrence (≥2 recurrences)

OptionRegimen
PreferredFaecal Microbiota Transplantation (FMT)
AlternativeVancomycin taper/pulse +/− Rifaximin 400mg 12h ×20d (chaser)
AlternativeFidaxomicin ×10d + Bezlotoxumab (monoclonal anti-toxin B, single IV dose with antibiotics)
[!tip]
FMT: cure rate 85–95% for recurrent CDI. Delivery: colonoscopy (preferred), enema, capsules, nasogastric. Donor screening mandatory. Bezlotoxumab: reduces recurrence in high-risk (age>65, immunocompromised, severe CDI, prior CDI) — give with standard antibiotics.

7. Fulminant CDI — Surgical Indications

IndicationAction
Toxic megacolon (colonic dilatation >6cm + systemic toxicity)Urgent subtotal colectomy + end ileostomy
PerforationUrgent laparotomy
Refractory shockSurgery if medical management failing
Lactate >5, WCC >35 or <2, rising lactateEarly surgical consult
[!warning]
Diverting loop ileostomy + antegrade vancomycin flushes = alternative to colectomy in selected centres.

8. Infection Prevention & Control (IPC)

MeasureDetail
Contact precautionsPrivate room or cohort; gown/gloves for ALL contact
Duration≥48h after diarrhoea resolves (some guidelines: until discharge)
Hand hygieneSoap and water (alcohol gel NOT sporicidal)
Environmental cleaningSporicidal agent: hypochlorite 1000ppm OR peracetic acid OR hydrogen peroxide vapour
EquipmentDedicated or sporicidal wipe between patients
Antibiotic stewardshipRestrict clindamycin, fluoroquinolones, cephalosporins; review at 48–72h
Diagnostic stewardshipTest only ≥3 unformed stools/24h; no test of cure

9. Special Situations

SituationManagement
PregnancyVancomycin PO safe (not absorbed); fidaxomicin limited data; metronidazole avoid 1st trimester
ImmunosuppressedHigher risk severe/fulminant; lower threshold for fidaxomicin/FMT
IBD flare vs CDITest for CDI in ALL IBD flares (co-infection common); treat CDI first
IleusVancomycin 500mg PR q6h + Metronidazole 500mg IV q8h; consider fidaxomicin oral if some motility
PaediatricMetronidazole 1st line (mild); Vancomycin PO (severe); FMT established
Test of cureNOT RECOMMENDED (colonisation persists weeks after cure)

10. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • CDI: toxin-mediated diarrhoea post-antibiotics
  • Risk: antibiotics (clindamycin > fluoroquinolones > cephalosporins), age>65, hospital/LTCF, PPI, immunosuppression
  • Diagnosis: 2-step algorithm (GDH/Toxin EIA → NAAT if discordant); test only ≥3 loose stools/day
  • Severity: non-severe, severe (WCC>15 or Cr>1.5×baseline or temp≥38.5), fulminant (shock, ileus, toxic megacolon)
  • 1st line ALL severity: Vancomycin 125mg PO 6h ×10d (fidaxomicin alternative)
  • Metronidazole: ONLY if vancomycin/fidaxomicin unavailable AND non-severe
  • Fulminant: Vancomycin 500mg PO/NG 6h + Metronidazole 500mg IV 8h + SURGICAL CONSULT
  • Recurrence: 1st → Fidaxomicin or Vancomycin taper/pulse; ≥2 → FMT (85–95% cure)
  • Bezlotoxumab: anti-toxin B monoclonal, single IV dose with antibiotics for high-risk recurrence
  • IPC: Contact precautions, soap/water hand hygiene, hypochlorite 1000ppm cleaning, antibiotic stewardship
  • No test of cure; asymptomatic carriage not treated

11. Common Viva Questions

  1. What is the recommended diagnostic algorithm for CDI? 2-step: GDH + Toxin EIA → if discordant (GDH+/Toxin−), confirm with NAAT/PCR.
  2. When do you test for CDI? ≥3 unformed stools/24h with clinical suspicion. No test of cure.
  3. First-line treatment for non-severe CDI? Vancomycin 125mg PO 6h ×10d (fidaxomicin alternative). Metronidazole no longer 1st line.
  4. First-line treatment for severe CDI? Vancomycin 125mg PO 6h ×10d (same as non-severe). Fidaxomicin alternative.
  5. Fulminant CDI treatment? Vancomycin 500mg PO/NG 6h + Metronidazole 500mg IV 8h + urgent surgical consult.
  6. First recurrence management? Fidaxomicin 200mg 12h ×10d preferred OR Vancomycin taper/pulse.
  7. Multiple recurrences (≥2) management? Faecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) preferred (85–95% cure).
  8. Bezlotoxumab indication? High-risk recurrence (age>65, immunocompromised, severe CDI, prior CDI) — single IV dose with standard antibiotics.
  9. IPC measures for CDI? Contact precautions, soap/water hand hygiene, hypochlorite 1000ppm cleaning, antibiotic stewardship.
  10. Why is IV vancomycin NOT used for CDI? Not absorbed orally → no colonic concentration. Oral vancomycin achieves high colonic levels.

12. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
Metronidazole 1st line for all CDIVancomycin 1st line for ALL severity; Metronidazole only if no access to vancomycin/fidaxomicin AND non-severe
IV vancomycin for CDIIV vancomycin does not reach colon; MUST be oral/NG/PR
NAAT alone sufficientNAAT alone = overdiagnosis (low PPV); 2-step algorithm recommended
Test of cure neededNo test of cure; colonisation persists weeks after clinical cure
Asymptomatic carrier = treatAsymptomatic carriage: DO NOT TREAT
Fidaxomicin for all = cost-effectiveFidaxomicin preferred for recurrence prevention; cost may limit 1st line use
FMT only for 3+ recurrencesFMT for ≥2 recurrences (some guidelines: after 2nd recurrence)
Alcohol gel kills sporesAlcohol gel NOT sporicidal; SOAP AND WATER required
Hypochlorite 100ppm sufficientHypochlorite 1000ppm (0.1%) required for sporicidal activity
Diarrhoea always present in fulminantIleus/toxic megacolon = diarrhoea may be ABSENT; distension, pain, lactate ↑

13. Mnemonics

  • CDI RISK: Clindamycin, Fluoroquinolones, Cephalosporins, PPI, Age>65, Hospital, Immunosuppression
  • 2-STEP ALGORITHM: GDH + Toxin EIA → Discordant → NAAT
  • SEVERITY: Non-severe (none), Severe (WCC>15 or Cr↑ or Temp≥38.5), Fulminant (Shock, Ileus, Megacolon, Perforation)
  • TREATMENT: Vanco 125mg PO 6h ×10d (ALL); Fulminant = Vanco 500mg + Metro IV + Surgery
  • RECURRENCE: 1st → Fidaxomicin or Vanco taper; ≥2 → FMT
  • IPC: Contact, Soap/Water, Hypochlorite 1000ppm, Abx stewardship
  • BEZLOTOXUMAB: Bezlo = Recurrence reduction in High-Risk (single IV with abx)

14. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Clostridioides difficile Infection))
    Pathogenesis
      Antibiotic disruption → overgrowth → toxins A/B
      Ribotype 027 = hypervirulent
    Risk Factors
      Antibiotics (Clinda > FQ > Ceph)
      Age >65, Hospital/LTCF, PPI, Immunosuppression
    Diagnosis
      2-step: GDH/Toxin EIA → NAAT if discordant
      Test only ≥3 loose stools/day
      No test of cure

*...continued (truncated to 200 lines for renderer)*

topics.title: Community Acquired Pneumonia Cap
topics.html:
Related: [[Hospital-Acquired and Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia]], [[Aspiration Pneumonia]], [[Sepsis and Septic Shock]], [[Tuberculosis (Pulmonary and Extrapulmonary)]], [[Fungal Pneumonias]], [[Viral Pneumonias]]

[!important]
CAP = pneumonia acquired outside hospital (≥48h after admission or <48h if no healthcare contact). Severity assessment: CURB-65 (outpatient) / PSI (inpatient) / IDSA/ATS minor/major criteria (ICU). Empirical antibiotics by severity and risk factors. Legionella urinary antigen + sputum culture + blood cultures before antibiotics. Corticosteroids in severe CAP (if no contraindication). Switch to oral at clinical stability (usually day 3–5). Total 5–7 days treatment.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Define CAP and distinguish from HAP/VAP/HCAP
  • Apply severity scores: CURB-65, PSI, IDSA/ATS minor/major criteria
  • Select empirical antibiotics by severity, risk factors, and local epidemiology
  • Order appropriate investigations (Legionella UA, sputum Cx, blood Cx, CXR, lactate)
  • Determine site of care (outpatient, ward, ICU)
  • Manage complications: empyema, parapneumonic effusion, lung abscess, septic shock, ARDS
  • Apply switch criteria (IV to oral) and treatment duration
  • Recognise atypical pathogens and when to test/treat

2. Definitions

TermDefinition
CAPAcute lower respiratory tract infection acquired in community (≥48h after admission if no healthcare contact)
HAPPneumonia ≥48h after hospital admission, not incubating at admission
VAPPneumonia ≥48h after endotracheal intubation
HCAP (retired concept)Healthcare-associated pneumonia (nursing home, dialysis, recent hospitalisation) — now assessed by risk factors within CAP

3. Aetiology by Setting

SettingTypicalAtypicalViralOther
Outpatient CAPS. pneumoniae (most common), H. influenzae, M. catarrhalisM. pneumoniae, C. pneumoniae, LegionellaInfluenza, RSV, SARS-CoV-2
Inpatient Ward CAPS. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, S. aureus, EnterobacteriaceaeLegionella, M. pneumoniae, C. pneumoniaeInfluenza, RSV, SARS-CoV-2
ICU CAPS. pneumoniae, S. aureus (MSSA/MRSA), Pseudomonas, EnterobacteriaceaeLegionellaInfluenza, SARS-CoV-2Acinetobacter (tropical)
[!tip]
S. pneumoniae = #1 cause overall. Legionella → severe CAP, summer/autumn, GI symptoms, hyponatraemia. M. pneumoniae → young adults, gradual onset, extrapulmonary. C. pneumoniae → mild, persistent cough. Viral co-infection common (influenza + bacterial).

4. Clinical Features

FeatureTypical (Pneumococcal)Atypical (Mycoplasma/Chlamydia/Legionella)
OnsetAcute (hours)Subacute (days)
FeverHigh, rigorsLow-grade to high (Legionella high)
CoughProductive, rusty sputumDry, non-productive (Legionella may become productive)
Chest painPleuriticLess common
DyspnoeaCommon if extensiveVariable
ExtrapulmonaryRareHeadache, myalgia, GI (Legionella), rash (Mycoplasma), haemolysis (Mycoplasma)
ExamConsolidation signsOften minimal signs vs CXR

5. Severity Assessment — Site of Care Decisions

CURB-65 (Outpatient / Ward Triage)

ComponentPoints
Confusion (AMT ≤8 / new disorientation)1
Urea >7 mmol/L (BUN >19 mg/dL)1
Respiratory rate ≥30/min1
BP: SBP <90 or DBP ≤60 mmHg1
Age ≥65 years1
ScoreMortalitySite of Care
0–1<3%Outpatient
2~9%Hospital admission (ward)
3–515–40%ICU consideration (assess IDSA/ATS criteria)

IDSA/ATS Minor Criteria (for ICU Admission — ≥3 = Severe CAP)

CriterionThreshold
Respiratory rate≥30/min
PaO₂/FiO₂≤250
Multilobar infiltratesYes
Confusion / disorientationYes
Uremia (BUN)≥20 mg/dL (7 mmol/L)
LeukopeniaWCC <4×10⁹/L
ThrombocytopeniaPlatelets <100×10⁹/L
HypothermiaCore temp <36°C
Hypotension requiring fluid resuscitationYes

IDSA/ATS Major Criteria (Either = Severe CAP / ICU)

  • Invasive mechanical ventilation
  • Septic shock requiring vasopressors
[!critical]
ICU admission if: 1 Major OR ≥3 Minor criteria. CURB-65 3–5 = consider ICU but use IDSA/ATS criteria for final decision.

6. Investigations

TestIndication
CXRAll suspected CAP (confirmation, multilobar, effusion, cavitation)
Blood culturesInpatient (ward + ICU) — before antibiotics
Sputum Gram stain + cultureInpatient (ward + ICU) — before antibiotics; expectorated > induced
Legionella urinary antigenAll inpatients (serogroup 1 only; rapid, specific)
Pneumococcal urinary antigenInpatients (optional; less specific if colonised)
Respiratory viral PCR panelInfluenza season, ICU, immunocompromised
ABGIf SpO₂ <94%, RR ≥30, confusion, lactate ↑
LactateIf sepsis suspected / CURB-65 ≥2
PCT / CRPSupport diagnosis, guide antibiotic duration (PCT-guided de-escalation)
HIV testAll adults with CAP (opt-out)
CT chestIf CXR unclear, suspected abscess/empyema, immunocompromised
[!tip]
Legionella UA detects serogroup 1 only (80–90% of cases); negative doesn't exclude. Sputum culture yield higher if good sample (<10 squamous epithelial cells/LPF).

7. Empirical Antibiotic Therapy

Outpatient (CURB-65 0–1, No Comorbidities)

RegimenDoseDuration
Amoxicillin1g PO 8h5 days
Doxycycline (if penicillin allergy)200mg PO stat then 100mg 12h5 days
Clarithromycin (if atypical suspected)500mg PO 12h5 days

Outpatient WITH Comorbidities (COPD, Heart Failure, Diabetes, Liver Disease, Alcohol, Steroids, Asplenia) OR Recent Antibiotics

RegimenDoseDuration
Amoxicillin + Clarithromycin1g 8h + 500mg 12h5–7 days
OR Doxycycline monotherapy200mg stat then 100mg 12h5–7 days
OR Levofloxacin500mg PO OD5–7 days

Inpatient Ward (Non-ICU, CURB-65 ≥2 or Severity Indicators)

RegimenDoseDuration
Ceftriaxone 2g IV 12h + Clarithromycin 500mg IV/PO 12hStandard5–7 days (switch oral day 3–5)
OR Levofloxacin 500mg IV/PO ODMonotherapy (covers typical + atypical)5–7 days
Penicillin allergy (anaphylaxis)Levofloxacin 500mg IV/PO OD5–7 days

ICU (Severe CAP — IDSA/ATS Criteria)

RegimenDoseDuration
Ceftriaxone 2g IV 12h + Azithromycin 500mg IV/PO ODPreferred (macrolide immunomodulation)5–7 days
OR Ceftriaxone 2g 12h + Levofloxacin 750mg IV ODIf macrolide contraindicated5–7 days
PLUS MRSA risk (prior MRSA, necrotising, influenza)+ Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg IV 6h
PLUS Pseudomonas risk (bronchiectasis, steroids >10mg, broad abx recent)Replace Ceftriaxone → Pip-tazo 4.5g 6h OR Meropenem 1g 8h OR Ceftazidime 2g 8h
[!key]
ICU: β-lactam + macrolide (ceftriaxone + azithromycin) = BEST OUTCOME. Add vancomycin/linezolid if MRSA risk. Use antipseudomonal β-lactam if Pseudomonas risk.

Special Additions

Risk FactorAdd
Legionella suspected (severe, GI symptoms, hyponatraemia, summer/autumn, travel)Azithromycin 500mg IV/PO OD (or Levofloxacin) — macrolide/fluoroquinolone essential
Influenza co-infectionOseltamivir 75mg PO 12h ×5d (start ASAP, even >48h if severe)
AspirationAdd Metronidazole 500mg IV 8h (anaerobes)
Cavitation / Lung abscessProlonged course 2–4 weeks + Metronidazole/Clindamycin

8. IV to Oral Switch Criteria (Clinical Stability)

CriteriaThreshold
Temperature≤37.8°C for 24h
Heart rate≤100/min
Respiratory rate≤24/min
SBP≥90 mmHg
SpO₂≥90% on room air
Mental statusOriented, no new confusion
Ability to tolerate oralYes
[!tip]
Usually achieved by day 3–5. Switch to same oral counterparts (ceftriaxone → amoxicillin/co-amoxiclav OR levofloxacin PO). Discharge when stable + oral tolerated.

9. Treatment Duration

ScenarioDuration
Uncomplicated CAP5 days (if clinically stable at day 3–5)
Severe CAP / ICU5–7 days (extend if slow response)
MRSA / Pseudomonas7 days
Lung abscess / Cavitation2–4 weeks
Empyema2–4 weeks post-drainage
[!important]
Shorter courses (5 days) non-inferior to 7–10 days in mild-moderate CAP. PCT-guided: stop if PCT <0.25 or >80% drop.

10. Corticosteroids in Severe CAP

IndicationSevere CAP (ICU) without contraindication
RegimenDexamethasone 5mg IV 6h ×5 days (or Hydrocortisone 50mg 6h)
EvidenceReduces mortality, ARDS, time to clinical stability (CAPE COD, DEXA-CAP trials)
ContraindicationsActive GI bleed, uncontrolled diabetes, immunosuppression, fungal/viral pneumonia (except influenza)

11. Complications

ComplicationFeaturesManagement
Parapneumonic effusionExudative, neutrophilic, pH >7.2, glucose >3.4, LDH >1000Antibiotics alone if uncomplicated
EmpyemaPus in pleural space, pH <7.2, glucose <3.4, LDH >1000Chest drain + antibiotics ± fibrinolytics (tPA/DNase) ± surgery (VATS/decortication)
Lung abscessCavitation with air-fluid level, foul sputumProlonged antibiotics 2–4w (clindamycin or metronidazole + β-lactam) ± drainage
Septic shockSee Sepsis noteSepsis bundle
ARDSPaO₂/FiO₂ <300, bilateral infiltratesLow Vt, PEEP, prone, dexamethasone
Necrotising pneumoniaMultiple cavities, rapid progressionBroad antibiotics ± surgery

12. Pathogen-Specific Pearls

PathogenCluesSpecific Rx
S. pneumoniaeRusty sputum, lobar consolidation, high CURB-65Penicillin/Amoxicillin if susceptible; Ceftriaxone if resistant
LegionellaSevere CAP, GI symptoms (diarrhoea), hyponatraemia, elevated LFTs, summer/autumn, travel, Legionella UA +veAzithromycin 500mg OD OR Levofloxacin 750mg OD (macrolide/fluoroquinolone MANDATORY)
M. pneumoniaeYoung adults, gradual, dry cough, headache, rash (erythema multiforme), cold agglutininsDoxycycline OR Macrolide (resistance ↑)
C. pneumoniaeMild, persistent cough, wheezeDoxycycline OR Macrolide
S. aureus (post-influenza)Rapid progression, cavitation, empyema, haemoptysisFlucloxacillin/Cefazolin (MSSA) + Clindamycin (toxin); Vancomycin/Linezolid (MRSA)
PseudomonasBronchiectasis, steroids, recent broad abx, structural lung diseasePip-tazo/Meropenem/Ceftazidime + Fluoroquinolone
KlebsiellaAlcoholics, diabetics, "currant jelly" sputum, cavitationCeftriaxone/Cefepime/Meropenem
InfluenzaSeasonal, myalgia, headache, dry cough, bilateral infiltratesOseltamivir 75mg 12h ×5d (start ASAP) + bacterial cover

13. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • CAP = community-acquired; HAP ≥48h admission; VAP ≥48h intubation
  • CURB-65: 0–1 outpatient, 2 ward, 3–5 consider ICU
  • ICU: 1 Major (ventilation/vasopressors) OR ≥3 Minor criteria (IDSA/ATS)
  • Investigations: CXR all; blood cx + sputum cx + Legionella UA + PCT inpatients; viral PCR seasonal
  • Empirical antibiotics by severity:

...continued (truncated to 200 lines for renderer)

Topic Overview

1. Learning Objectives
2. Definitions
3. Aetiology by Setting
4. Clinical Features
5. Severity Assessment — Site of Care Decisions
CURB-65 (Outpatient / Ward Triage)
IDSA/ATS Minor Criteria (for ICU Admission — ≥3 = Severe CAP)
IDSA/ATS Major Criteria (Either = Severe CAP / ICU)
6. Investigations
7. Empirical Antibiotic Therapy
Outpatient (CURB-65 0–1, No Comorbidities)
Outpatient WITH Comorbidities (COPD, Heart Failure, Diabetes, Liver Disease, ...
Inpatient Ward (Non-ICU, CURB-65 ≥2 or Severity Indicators)
ICU (Severe CAP — IDSA/ATS Criteria)
Special Additions
8. IV to Oral Switch Criteria (Clinical Stability)
9. Treatment Duration
10. Corticosteroids in Severe CAP
11. Complications
12. Pathogen-Specific Pearls
13. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

topics.title: Cryptococcal Meningitis
topics.html:
[!info] Davidson Ch 11 Alignment: Infectious Disease → Specific Organism Groups → Fungi → Cryptococcus
FCPS/MRCP Focus: HIV/AIDS association, induction/consolidation/maintenance phases, amphotericin B + flucytosine, fluconazole, raised ICP management, IRIS

1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Recognise Clinical Presentation: Subacute Meningitis (Weeks), Headache, Fever, Confusion, Cranial Nerve Palsies, Visual Loss
  • [ ] Identify Risk Factors: HIV/AIDS (CD4<100), Immunosuppression, Transplant, Steroids, Sarcoidosis
  • [ ] Diagnose: India Ink (Rapid), CrAg (LFA/ELISA) — Serum/CSF (High Sens/Spec), Culture (Gold Standard), PCR
  • [ ] Manage 3-Phase Therapy: Induction (Amphotericin B + Flucytosine), Consolidation (Fluconazole), Maintenance (Fluconazole)
  • [ ] Manage Raised ICP: Serial LPs, Acetazolamide, VP Shunt, Avoid Steroids
  • [ ] Recognise IRIS: Paradoxical Worsening on ART Initiation, Continue Antifungals + ART, Steroids if Severe
  • [ ] Apply Prophylaxis: Fluconazole 200mg OD (CD4<100), Discontinue if CD4>200 + VL Suppressed

2. 📖 Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetails
Causative AgentCryptococcus neoformans (Var. grubii — Serotype A, Global); C. gattii (Var. gattii — Tropical/Subtropical, Immunocompetent)
Environmental ReservoirBird Droppings (Pigeon Guano), Soil, Eucalyptus Trees (C. gattii)
TransmissionInhalation of Spores → Primary Pulmonary → Haematogenous Dissemination → CNS
Risk GroupsHIV/AIDS (CD4<100) — 80-90% of Cases; Transplant, Corticosteroids, Cirrhosis, Sarcoidosis, Immunocompetent (C. gattii)
IncubationWeeks to Months (Variable, Often Subacute)
Global Burden~200,000 Cases/Year, ~150,000 Deaths (Mostly Sub-Saharan Africa, HIV-Associated)
[!tip] Cryptococcal Meningitis = #1 Cause of Fungal Meningitis in HIV/AIDS. CD4<100 = Major Risk. Subacute Meningitis (Weeks) = Typical Presentation.

3. 📖 Clinical Presentation

Typical Presentation (Subacute Meningitis)

FeatureFrequency
Headache>90% (Progressive, Worsening, Morning Worse)
Fever50-70% (Often Low-Grade / Absent in HIV)
Confusion / Altered Mental Status30-50%
Cranial Nerve PalsiesCN II, III, IV, VI (Visual Loss, Diplopia, Ptosis)
Nausea/VomitingCommon (Raised ICP)
Neck StiffnessVariable (Often Absent in HIV)
Visual LossPapilloedema, Optic Atrophy (Raised ICP, Optic Nerve Compression)

HIV vs Non-HIV Presentation

FeatureHIV (CD4<100)Immunocompetent / Non-HIV
FeverOften Absent / Low-GradePresent (High Fever)
Neck StiffnessOften AbsentPresent (Classic Meningism)
HeadacheProgressive, SubacuteAcute/Subacute, Severe
CT HeadOften Normal / Mild AtrophyHydrocephalus, Cryptococcomas
CSF Opening PressureMarkedly Elevated (>30 cm H₂O)Markedly Elevated
Mortality (High-Income)15-25% (With Optimal Rx)Lower (If Immunocompetent)
[!tip] Cryptococcal Meningitis = Subacute Meningitis + HIV (CD4<100) + Raised ICP + India Ink+/CrAg+. Raised ICP = Major Cause of Morbidity/Mortality.

4. 🔬 Diagnostic Workup

flowchart TD
    A[Subacute Meningitis + HIV/CD4<100 / Immunocompromised] --> B[**LP: Opening Pressure (Mandatory)**]
    B --> C[**CSF: India Ink** (Rapid, Sens 50-80%)]
    C --> D[**CrAg LFA/ELISA (CSF/Serum)**]
    D --> E{**CrAg Positive?**}
    E -->|Yes| F[**Diagnosis Confirmed**]
    E -->|No| G[**Culture (Gold Standard)** / **PCR**]
    G --> H[**Repeat LP if High Suspicion**]
    H --> I[**Cryptococcomas?** — MRI Brain]

CSF Findings

ParameterClassic Cryptococcal MeningitisHIV-Associated
Opening PressureMarkedly Elevated (>30 cm H₂O)Markedly Elevated (>30 cm H₂O)
WBCLymphocytic Pleocytosis (50-500/µL)Normal / Mild ↑ (Often <20/µL in HIV)
ProteinElevated (100-500 mg/dL)Normal / Mild ↑
GlucoseLow (<40% Serum)Normal / Mildly Low
India InkPositive (Encapsulated Yeasts, Capsule)Positive (Sensitivity 50-80%)
CrAg (LFA/ELISA)Positive (Sens >95%)Positive (Sens >95%)
CulturePositive (Gold Standard)Positive (Slower Growth)
CRAG LFA (Serum/CSF)Positive (High Sens/Spec)Highly Sensitive/Specific
[!tip] Opening Pressure >30 cm H₂O = Key Feature in Both HIV & Non-HIV. CSF Lymphocytes May Be Normal in HIV (Immunosuppression). CrAg = Rapid, Highly Sensitive/Specific.

5. 💊 Management — 3-Phase Therapy (WHO/IDSA Guidelines)

Phase 1: Induction (Weeks 1-2)

RegimenDoseDurationNotes
Amphotericin B Deoxycholate + FlucytosineAmB 0.7-1.0 mg/kg/day IV + Flucytosine 100 mg/kg/day PO (QID)14 DaysGold Standard (Survival Benefit)
Liposomal Amphotericin B (LAmB) + FlucytosineLAmB 3-5 mg/kg/day IV + Flucytosine 100 mg/kg/day PO14 DaysPreferred if Renal Impairment / High Mortality Risk
Flucytosine Dose100 mg/kg/day (25mg 1-2-1-2 OR QID)14 DaysMonitor: CBC, Renal, LFT q48h (Bone Marrow / Renal / Hepatic Toxicity)
[!warning] Flucytosine: Dose Adjustment for Renal Impairment (CrCl<50 → Dose Reduce); Monitor CBC, Renal, LFT q48h (Bone Marrow Suppression, Nephrotoxicity, Hepatotoxicity).
[!warning] Amphotericin B: Infusion-Related Reactions (Fever, Chills, Hypotension) → Pre-medicate (Paracetamol, Hydrocortisone, Antihistamine). Renal Toxicity → Hydration, Electrolyte Replacement (K+, Mg2+).

Phase 2: Consolidation (Weeks 3-8)

AgentDoseDuration
Fluconazole800 mg PO/IV Daily (12 mg/kg/day)8 Weeks (Total 10 Weeks Induction+Consolidation)
AlternativeVoriconazole 200mg BD / Itraconazole 200mg BDIf Fluconazole Intolerance
[!tip] Fluconazole 800mg Daily = High Dose for CNS Penetration. Duration 8 Weeks (Total 10 Weeks Induction+Consolidation).

Phase 3: Maintenance (Secondary Prophylaxis)

AgentDoseDurationDiscontinuation Criteria
Fluconazole200 mg PO Daily≥1 YearImmune Reconstitution: CD4>100-200 for ≥6 Months on ART + Viral Suppression
AlternativeItraconazole 200mg BD / Voriconazole 200mg BDIf Fluconazole Intolerance

6. 🩺 Raised ICP Management (Critical)

InterventionIndication / Details
Therapeutic LP (Daily)Opening Pressure >25-30 cm H₂O → Remove CSF to ≤20 cm H₂O (or 50% Reduction); Daily Until Stable
Acetazolamide250mg QID PO/IV (Adjunct, Carbonic Anhydrase Inhibitor → ↓ CSF Production)
VP ShuntRefractory Raised ICP (Failed Serial LPs + Acetazolamide), Hydrocephalus, Visual Loss
CorticosteroidsCONTRAINDICATED (↑ Mortality, ↑ Fungal Burden)
Mannitol / Hypertonic SalineAcute Herniation (Emergency Temporising)
[!warning] Corticosteroids CONTRAINDICATED in Cryptococcal Meningitis (↑ Mortality, ↑ Fungal Burden). Raised ICP = Daily Therapeutic LPs + Acetazolamide → VP Shunt if Refractory.

7. 🔄 HIV-Associated Specifics

ART Timing (IRIS Risk)

CD4 CountART Initiation
CD4 <50Delay ART 4-6 Weeks (After Induction) — Reduce IRIS Risk
CD4 50-100Start ART 2-4 Weeks (After Induction Started)
CD4 >100Standard ART Timing
[!warning] Cryptococcal IRIS = Paradoxical Worsening on ART Initiation (Worsening Meningitis, New Lesions, Rising CrAg). Management: Continue Antifungals + ART, Add Steroids (Prednisolone 1mg/kg) if Severe, Therapeutic LPs, Do Not Stop ART/Antifungals.

Discontinuation of Maintenance Therapy

CriteriaDuration
CD4 >200 for ≥6 Months on ARTAt Least 12 Months Total Therapy
Viral Suppression (VL <50)Sustained
No Active Cryptococcal DiseaseClinical + Radiological + CrAg Negative

8. 🔬 Laboratory Findings

TestFindings
CSF Opening Pressure>30 cm H₂O (Markedly Elevated)
CSF WBCLymphocytic (50-500/µL); Normal/Mild ↑ in HIV
CSF ProteinElevated (100-500 mg/dL)
CSF GlucoseLow (<40% Serum)
India InkPositive (Encapsulated Yeasts, Halo = Capsule); Sens 50-80%
CrAg LFA (CSF/Serum)Positive (Sens >95%, Spec >95%)
CultureGold Standard (4-7 Days)
Serum CrAgScreening (High Sensitivity)

9. 🔄 Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDifferentiating Features
TB Meningitis (TBM)Subacute (Weeks), CSF: Lymphocytic, Low Glucose, High Protein, Acid-Fast Bacilli/PCR/Xpert MTB/RIF, Exudates on Imaging, Basal Exudates
Viral Encephalitis/MeningitisAcute Onset, CSF Lymphocytic, Viral PCR Panel+, Normal Pressure
Bacterial MeningitisAcute, Neutrophilic CSF, Low Glucose, Gram Stain+, Culture+
TB Meningitis vs CryptoTBM: Basal Exudates, Hydrocephalus, Cranial Nerve Palsies, Crypto: Raised ICP, CrAg+, India Ink+
CNS Lymphoma (HIV)Ring-Enhancing Lesions, CSF Cytology+, EBV PCR+
Toxoplasma EncephalitisRing-Enhancing Lesions, IgG+, PCR+, Seizures Common

10. 💡 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
PopulationHIV/AIDS (CD4<100) = 80-90%; Immunocompromised / C. gattii (Immunocompetent)
PresentationSubacute Meningitis (Weeks): Headache, Fever, Confusion, CN Palsies, Visual Loss
CSFOpening Pressure >30 cm H₂O, Lymphocytic Pleocytosis, Low Glucose, High Protein
DiagnosisCrAg LFA (CSF/Serum) >95% Sens/Spec, India Ink (Rapid), Culture (Gold Standard)
Induction (2 Weeks)Amphotericin B + Flucytosine (Gold Standard); LAmB Preferred if Renal Impairment
Consolidation (8 Weeks)Fluconazole 800mg Daily
MaintenanceFluconazole 200mg Daily → Stop: CD4>200 for 6mo on ART + VL Suppressed
Raised ICPDaily Therapeutic LPs (Target <25 cm H₂O), Acetazolamide, VP Shunt (Refractory), NO Steroids
ART TimingCD4<50: Delay 4-6 Weeks; CD4 50-100: Delay 2-4 Weeks; IRIS Risk
IRISContinue Antifungals + ART, Steroids if Severe

11. ❓ Viva Questions

  1. What is the most common cause of fungal meningitis in HIV/AIDS?
  • Cryptococcus neoformans (Cryptococcal Meningitis).
  1. What is the classic CSF opening pressure in Cryptococcal Meningitis?
  • >30 cm H₂O (Markedly Elevated).
  1. What is the induction regimen for Cryptococcal Meningitis?
  • Amphotericin B (0.7-1 mg/kg/day IV) + Flucytosine 100 mg/kg/day PO (QID) × 14 Days.
  1. Why is Flucytosine combined with Amphotericin B?
  • Synergistic Fungicidal Activity; Reduces Duration & Mortality; Prevents Amphotericin Resistance.
  1. What is the target CSF opening pressure during therapeutic LP?
  • ≤25 cm H₂O (or 50% Reduction from Baseline).
  1. Why are corticosteroids contraindicated in Cryptococcal Meningitis?
  • Increase Mortality & Fungal Burden; Worsen Outcome (Clinical Trial Evidence).
  1. When should ART be started in HIV+ Cryptococcal Meningitis?
  • CD4<50: Delay 4-6 Weeks; CD4 50-100: Delay 2-4 Weeks; CD4>100: Standard Timing.
  1. What is Cryptococcal IRIS and how is it managed?
  • Paradoxical Worsening on ART → Continue Antifungals + ART, Add Prednisolone 1mg/kg if Severe, Therapeutic LPs.
  1. When can maintenance fluconazole be stopped?
  • CD4>200 for ≥6 Months on ART + Viral Suppression + No Active Disease + ≥12 Months Total Therapy.
  1. What is the role of Flucytosine in Induction?
  • Synergistic with Amphotericin B; Rapid Fungicidal Activity; Reduces Mortality & Duration; Prevents Resistance.
  1. What is the target CSF opening pressure during therapeutic LP?
  • ≤25 cm H₂O (or 50% Reduction from Baseline).
  1. Why is Amphotericin B + Flucytosine preferred over Fluconazole monotherapy for Induction?
  • Fungicidal (vs Fungistatic), Superior Survival (RCT Evidence), Rapid Sterilisation of CSF, Prevents Resistance.

12. 🧠 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Induction vs ConsolidationInduction = AmB + 5-FC (2 Weeks); Consolidation = Fluconazole 800mg (8 Weeks)
Fluconazole 800mg vs 200mg800mg = Consolidation (Induction Dose); 200mg = Maintenance
ART TimingCD4<50 = Delay 4-6w; CD4 50-100 = Delay 2-4w; CD4>100 = Standard
IRIS vs Treatment FailureIRIS = Paradoxical Worsening on ART (CrAg↑, Symptoms↑); Failure = No Improvement on Optimal Rx
Steroids in CryptoCONTRAINDICATED (↑ Mortality); IRIS = Steroids OK
MnemonicMeaning
"Induction = AmB + 5FC (2w), Consolidation = Flu 800mg (8w), Maint = Flu 200mg (≥1yr)"3-Phase Therapy
"ICP >30 = Daily LP + Acetazolamide"Raised ICP Management
"No Steroids in Crypto"Contraindicated
"ART Delay: <50 = 4-6w, 50-100 = 2-4w"ART Timing
"IRIS = Worsening on ART + Continue Abx + Steroids"IRIS Management
"CrAg = Rapid, >95% Sens/Spec"Diagnosis

13. 🗺️ Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Cryptococcal Meningitis))
    Risk Factors
      HIV/AIDS CD4<100 (80-90%)
      Transplant, Steroids, Sarcoidosis

14. 📋 One-Page Revision Card

CRYPTOCOCCAL MENINGITIS – FCPS/MRCP REVISION CARD
Population: HIV CD4<100 (80-90%), Immunocompromised, C. gattii (Immunocompetent)
Presentation: Subacute Meningitis (Weeks), Headache, Fever, Confusion, CN Palsies, Visual Loss
CSF: Opening Pressure >30 cm H₂O, Lymphocytic, Low Glucose, High Protein
Diagnosis: CrAg LFA (>95% Sens/Spec), India Ink, Culture (Gold)
Induction (2w): AmB + Flucytosine (100mg/kg/d QID) — Gold Standard
Consolidation (8w): Fluconazole 800mg OD
Maintenance: Fluconazole 200mg OD → Stop: CD4>200 ×6m + VL Suppressed
ICP Management: Daily LP → Target <25 cm H₂O, Acetazolamide, NO Steroids, VP Shunt if Refractory
ART Timing: CD4<50: Delay 4-6w; 50-100: 2-4w; >100: Standard
IRIS: Worsening on ART → Continue Antifungals + ART + Steroids
Maintenance Stop: CD4>200 ×6m + VL Suppressed + ≥1yr Total Therapy

topics.title: Cytomegalovirus Cmv Disease
topics.html:
Related: [[Infection in Immunocompromised Host (Transplant, Biologics)]], [[HIV Infection and AIDS]], [[Infection in Pregnancy and Neonates]], [[Fever in Returned Traveller and FUO]], [[Pneumocystis Pneumonia]], [[Viral Encephalitis and Meningitis]]

[!important]
CMV = β-herpesvirus; ubiquitous (seroprevalence 50–90%). Primary infection usually asymptomatic; reactivation in immunocompromised = major morbidity. Transplant: D+/R− highest risk; peak 1–4 months. Diagnosis: quantitative PCR (blood); pp65 antigenemia. Treatment: Ganciclovir/Valganciclovir 1st-line; Foscarnet/Cidofovir if resistant. Prophylaxis: Valganciclovir (high-risk); Pre-emptive therapy (PCR-guided). Congenital CMV = most common congenital infection; hearing loss, microcephaly.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Understand CMV epidemiology, transmission, and pathogenesis
  • Identify clinical syndromes in immunocompetent vs immunocompromised hosts
  • Apply quantitative PCR and pp65 antigenemia for diagnosis and monitoring
  • Select and monitor antiviral therapy (ganciclovir, valganciclovir, foscarnet, cidofovir, maribavir)
  • Implement prophylaxis vs pre-emptive strategies in transplant
  • Manage congenital CMV and CMV in pregnancy

2. Epidemiology & Transmission

AspectDetails
VirusHuman Cytomegalovirus (CMV/HHV-5) — β-herpesvirus, double-stranded DNA
Seroprevalence50–90% (increases with age, lower SES, developing countries)
TransmissionBody fluids: saliva, urine, blood, semen, cervical secretions, breast milk, tears; Vertical (transplacental, intrapartum, breast milk); Horizontal (close contact, sexual, transfusion, transplant)
LatencyLifelong in myeloid progenitors, endothelial cells; reactivation with immunosuppression
ReinfectionPossible with different strains (superinfection)

3. Clinical Syndromes

Host / SyndromeFeatures
Immunocompetent PrimaryAsymptomatic (90%); Mononucleosis-like (fever, fatigue, pharyngitis, cervical lymphadenopathy, mild hepatosplenomegaly, atypical lymphocytosis); resolves 2–6w
Immunocompromised (Transplant, HIV, Biologics)Primary or Reactivation; Viraemia → Syndrome → Tissue-invasive disease
CMV SyndromeFever ≥38°C ±24h + Viraemia (PCR/antigenemia) + ≥2: leucopenia, thrombocytopenia, ↑ ALT/AST, atypical lymphs
Tissue-Invasive DiseasePneumonitis (dyspnoea, hypoxia, bilateral interstitial infiltrates); Colitis (diarrhoea, pain, bleeding); Retinitis (floaters, vision loss); Hepatitis; Encephalitis; Nephritis
Congenital CMVMost common congenital infection (0.5–1% births); 10–15% symptomatic at birth; Asymptomatic → 10–15% late sequelae (hearing loss, developmental delay); Symptomatic: microcephaly, IUGR, petechiae, hepatosplenomegaly, thrombocytopenia, periventricular calcifications, chorioretinitis, SNHL
CMV in PregnancyPrimary infection 30–40% transmission; 1st trimester = highest risk severe congenital disease; 3rd trimester = highest transmission rate; Perinatal (breast milk) → usually mild in term, severe in preterm

4. Diagnosis

TestSampleUtilityTiming
Quantitative PCR (Blood/Plasma)BloodGold standard; quantifies viral load; guides pre-emptive therapy; monitors responseAny time (detects viraemia before symptoms)
pp65 AntigenemiaBlood (PMNs)Rapid; correlates with viral load; lower sensitivity than PCREarly diagnosis; monitoring
Viral CultureUrine, saliva, blood, CSF, tissueDefinitive; slow (2–4w); low sensitivityConfirmation; resistance testing
Serology (IgM/IgG)SerumPast vs recent infection; IgM = recent/acute; IgG avidity = primary vs pastNot for acute management in immunocompromised
IgG Avidity IndexSerumLow = recent primary (<3–4m); High = pastPregnancy/congenital
Histopathology (Biopsy)Tissue (lung, gut, retina)Inclusion cells (owl's eye intranuclear inclusions)Tissue-invasive disease confirmation
[!key]
Quantitative PCR = gold standard for diagnosis, monitoring, pre-emptive therapy. pp65 antigenemia = rapid alternative. IgG avidity = distinguishes primary vs past infection (critical in pregnancy). Viraemia + symptoms = CMV syndrome; add tissue involvement = tissue-invasive disease.

5. Definitions (Transplant Context)

TermDefinition
CMV InfectionDetection of CMV (PCR, antigenemia, culture) regardless of symptoms
CMV SyndromeFever ≥38°C ±24h + viraemia + ≥2: leucopenia, thrombocytopenia, ↑ ALT/AST, atypical lymphocytosis
Tissue-Invasive CMV DiseaseCMV syndrome + organ-specific signs + histopathology (inclusion cells) or PCR in tissue/fluid

6. Treatment

Antivirals

DrugDose (Adult)IndicationKey Adverse Effects
Valganciclovir900mg PO BD (induction) → 900mg PO OD (maintenance)1st-line oral; equivalent to IV ganciclovirNeutropenia (20–40%), thrombocytopenia, GI, renal adjust
Ganciclovir (IV)5mg/kg IV 12h (induction) → 5mg/kg IV OD (maintenance)1st-line IV (severe, unable oral)Neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, renal adjust, teratogenic
Foscarnet90mg/kg IV 12h (induction) → 90–120mg/kg IV OD (maintenance)Resistant CMV (UL97/UL54 mut); ganciclovir intoleranceNephrotoxicity, electrolytes (Ca, Mg, K, Phos), QTc, seizures
Cidofovir5mg/kg IV weekly (induction 2w) → 5mg/kg IV q2w (maintenance)Resistant CMV; foscarnet intoleranceNephrotoxicity (probenecid + hydration mandatory); ocular
Maribavir400mg PO BDRefractory/resistant (UL97 mut); post-transplantTaste disturbance, nausea, ↑ tacrolimus levels
Leflunomide20mg PO OD (load 100mg day 1)Adjunct/salvage (BK virus + CMV)Hepatotoxicity, diarrhoea, alopecia
[!critical]
Valganciclovir 1st-line oral (bioavailability 60% = IV ganciclovir). Foscarnet/Cidofovir/Maribavir for resistant CMV (UL97/UL54 mutations). Monitor: CBC 2×/w ×2w, then weekly; renal function; CMV PCR weekly ×2w, then q1–2w.

Treatment Regimens by Syndrome

SyndromeInductionMaintenanceDuration
CMV Syndrome / Mild DiseaseValganciclovir 900mg PO BDValganciclovir 900mg PO OD2–3 weeks (until viraemia cleared ×2)
Tissue-Invasive Disease (Pneumonitis, Colitis, Retinitis)Ganciclovir 5mg/kg IV 12h OR Valganciclovir 900mg PO BDValganciclovir 900mg PO OD3–6 weeks (until clinical resolution + viraemia cleared)
Congenital CMV (Symptomatic)Ganciclovir 6mg/kg IV 12h OR Valganciclovir 16mg/kg PO BD—6 weeks (improves hearing/developmental outcomes)
Resistant CMV (UL97 mut)Maribavir 400mg PO BD OR Foscarnet—3–6 weeks
Resistant CMV (UL54 mut)Foscarnet 90mg/kg IV 12h—3–6 weeks

7. CMV in Transplant

Risk Stratification (D/R Serostatus)

D/R StatusRiskStrategy
D+/R−Highest (primary infection)Prophylaxis (Valganciclovir 900mg OD ×3–6m) OR Pre-emptive (weekly PCR ×3–6m)
D+/R+Intermediate (reactivation + reinfection)Pre-emptive (weekly PCR ×3–6m)
D−/R+Intermediate (reactivation)Pre-emptive (weekly PCR ×3–6m)
D−/R−Low (community acquisition)Pre-emptive surveillance
[!key]
Prophylaxis (Valganciclovir 900mg OD) for D+/R− high-risk (lung, heart, intestine, HSCT) ×3–6m. Pre-emptive = weekly PCR ×3–6m; treat if viral load > threshold (e.g., >1000 IU/mL or rising). Prophylaxis delays but does not eliminate CMV.

Pre-emptive Therapy Thresholds

ParameterThreshold
Viral Load>1000–2000 IU/mL (or >1000 copies/mL)
Rising Trend>1 log increase in 1 week
Antigenemia>10–20 pp65+ cells/200,000 PMNs

8. CMV in Pregnancy & Congenital CMV

AspectDetails
Primary Infection Rate1–4% pregnancies
Vertical TransmissionPrimary: 30–40% (1st tri ≈30%, 2nd ≈40%, 3rd ≈70%); Recurrent: 1–2%
Congenital CMV SequelaeSymptomatic (10–15%): microcephaly, IUGR, petechiae, hepatosplenomegaly, thrombocytopenia, periventricular calcifications, chorioretinitis, SNHL (most common); Asymptomatic (85–90%): 10–15% late SNHL, developmental delay
Diagnosis in NeonateUrine/Saliva PCR (birth–3w) — gold standard; IgM low sensitivity
Treatment (Symptomatic Neonate)Ganciclovir 6mg/kg IV 12h ×6w OR Valganciclovir 16mg/kg PO BD ×6w → improves hearing/neurodevelopment
Primary Infection in PregnancyIgG avidity low = recent primary; amniocentesis PCR (amniotic fluid) for fetal infection; consider valganciclovir (experimental)
[!critical]
Congenital CMV = most common congenital infection. Urine PCR at birth = gold standard. Ganciclovir/Valganciclovir ×6w for symptomatic neonates improves hearing/neurodevelopment. IgG avidity distinguishes primary vs past infection in pregnancy.

9. Resistance

MutationMechanismCross-ResistanceTreatment
UL97 (Phosphotransferase)Impaired ganciclovir phosphorylationGanciclovir/ValganciclovirMaribavir (UL97 inhibitor) or Foscarnet/Cidofovir
UL54 (DNA Polymerase)Impaired drug bindingGanciclovir, Foscarnet, CidofovirMaribavir (UL97 target intact) OR Leflunomide (adjunct)

10. Prophylaxis vs Pre-emptive in Transplant

StrategyProsConsPreferred For
Prophylaxis (Valganciclovir 900mg OD ×3–6m)Prevents early CMV; simpleDelayed onset after stop; drug toxicity; cost; resistance if breakthroughD+/R− high-risk (lung, heart, intestine, HSCT)
Pre-emptive (Weekly PCR ×3–6m, treat if >threshold)Avoids unnecessary drug exposure; targets only those needing itRequires infrastructure; missed cases if PCR missed; labour-intensiveStandard-risk (D+/R+, D−/R+, D−/R−); D+/R− low-risk

11. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • CMV = β-herpesvirus; ubiquitous; latency in myeloid cells; reactivation in immunocompromised
  • Transplant D+/R− = highest risk; peak 1–4m; prophylaxis (valganciclovir) OR pre-emptive (PCR-guided)
  • CMV Syndrome = fever + viraemia + (leucopenia/thrombocytopenia/↑ALT/atypical lymphs)
  • Tissue-invasive = pneumonitis, colitis, retinitis, hepatitis, encephalitis
  • Diagnosis: Quantitative PCR (gold standard), pp65 antigenemia, IgG avidity (pregnancy)
  • Treatment: Valganciclovir 1st-line (oral); Ganciclovir IV (severe); Foscarnet/Cidofovir/Maribavir for resistant
  • CMV Syndrome: Valganciclovir 900mg BD ×2–3w; Tissue-invasive: IV ganciclovir/Valganciclovir 3–6w
  • Resistance: UL97 mut = ganciclovir resistance → maribavir/foscarnet; UL54 mut = foscarnet/cidofovir resistance
  • Transplant D+/R−: Prophylaxis (valganciclovir 900mg OD ×3–6m) OR Pre-emptive (weekly PCR)
  • Congenital CMV: most common congenital infection; urine PCR at birth; ganciclovir/valganciclovir ×6w for symptomatic
  • IgG avidity low = recent primary infection (pregnancy); high = past

12. Common Viva Questions

  1. Highest risk transplant for CMV? D+/R− (donor positive, recipient negative).
  2. CMV Syndrome definition? Fever + viraemia + ≥2 of: leucopenia, thrombocytopenia, ↑ALT/AST, atypical lymphs.
  3. First-line treatment for CMV? Valganciclovir 900mg PO BD (induction) → OD (maintenance).
  4. When to use foscarnet? Resistant CMV (UL97/UL54 mutations) or ganciclovir intolerance.
  5. Congenital CMV diagnosis? Urine/saliva PCR at birth (gold standard).
  6. Congenital CMV treatment? Ganciclovir 6mg/kg IV 12h OR Valganciclovir 16mg/kg BD ×6w for symptomatic.
  7. Prophylaxis vs Pre-emptive in transplant? D+/R− = prophylaxis OR pre-emptive; others = pre-emptive.
  8. IgG avidity in pregnancy? Low = recent primary (<3–4m); high = past infection.
  9. UL97 mutation? Ganciclovir resistance → use maribavir or foscarnet.
  10. CMV retinitis treatment? Ganciclovir IV + intravitreal ganciclovir/foscarnet; valganciclovir alternative.
  11. CMV pneumonitis treatment? Ganciclovir IV 5mg/kg 12h ×3–6w +/− IVIG.

13. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
CMV IgM = acute infection in transplantIgM unreliable in immunocompromised; use PCR/viral load
Valganciclovir = ganciclovir prodrug, same dose900mg valganciclovir PO ≈ 5mg/kg IV ganciclovir
CMV IgM = acute infection in immunocompetentIgG avidity needed to distinguish primary vs past
Prophylaxis eliminates CMVDelays only; breakthrough occurs after stop; pre-emptive still needed
All CMV infections need treatmentAsymptomatic viraemia in immunocompetent = no treatment
Foscarnet = 2nd line for allOnly for resistant (UL97/UL54) or ganciclovir intolerance
CMV retinitis = only in AIDSAlso in transplant, steroids; treat with intravitreal + systemic
Congenital CMV = always symptomatic85–90% asymptomatic at birth; 10–15% late SNHL
CMV prophylaxis prevents all CMVDelays only; pre-emptive still needed after stop
Valganciclovir = same dose IV/POPO 900mg BD (induction) / OD (maintenance); IV ganciclovir 5mg/kg 12h/OD

14. Mnemonics

  • CMV SYNDROME: Fever + Viraemia + Leucopenia/Thrombocytopenia/ALT↑/Lymphs = FVL-TAL
  • CMV RISK: D+/R− = Highest; D+/R+ = Intermediate; D−/R+ = Intermediate; D−/R− = Low
  • TREATMENT: Valganciclovir 1st oral; Ganciclovir IV severe; Foscarnet/Cidofovir/Maribavir Resistant
  • RESISTANCE: UL97 = Ganciclovir Resistant → Maribavir/Foscarnet; UL54 = Foscarnet/Cidofovir Resistant
  • TRANSPLANT: D+/R− = Prophylaxis OR Pre-emptive; Others = Pre-emptive
  • CONGENITAL: Urine PCR birth = Dx; Ganciclovir/Valganciclovir 6w symptomatic
  • PREGNANCY: IgG Avidity Low = Recent primary; High = Past

15. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((CMV Disease))
    Epidemiology
      Ubiquitous (50-90% seroprevalence)
      Latency in myeloid cells
      Reactivation with immunosuppression
    Clinical
      Immunocompetent: Mono-like (asymptomatic 90%)
      Immunocompromised: Syndrome vs Tissue-invasive
      Syndrome: Fever + Viraemia + 2 of (Leucopenia, Thrombocytopenia, ALT↑, Atypical lymphs)

*...continued (truncated to 200 lines for renderer)*

topics.title: Endemic Mycoses
topics.html:
[!info] Davidson Ch 11 Alignment: Infectious Disease → Specific Organism Groups → Fungi → Endemic Mycoses (Dimorphic Fungi)
FCPS/MRCP Focus: Histoplasmosis, Coccidioidomycosis, Blastomycosis, Paracoccidioidomycosis, Talaromycosis, endemic geography, diagnosis, treatment

1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Identify Dimorphic Fungi: Mould at 25°C / Yeast at 37°C — Histoplasma, Coccidioides, Blastomyces, Paracoccidioides, Talaromyces (Penicillium)
  • [ ] Recognise Endemic Geography: Histoplasma (Ohio/Mississippi River Valleys), Coccidioides (SW USA, N Mexico), Blastomyces (Great Lakes, SE USA), Paracoccidioides (Latin America), Talaromyces (SE Asia)
  • [ ] Recognise Clinical Presentations: Acute/Subacute Pneumonia, Chronic Pulmonary, Disseminated (Skin, Bone, CNS, Adrenal), Immunocompromised
  • [ ] Diagnose: Culture (Gold Standard), Antigen Detection (Urine/Blood/CSF), Serology (CF, ID, EIA), PCR, Histopathology (Yeast in Macrophages)
  • [ ] Manage: Itraconazole (Mild-Mod), Amphotericin B (Severe), Lifelong Suppression (Immunosuppressed), Azoles (Itraconazole, Voriconazole, Posaconazole)

2. 📖 General Features of Dimorphic Fungi

FeatureDetails
DimorphismMould at 25°C (Environment) → Yeast at 37°C (Host) — Thermal Dimorphism
Infection RouteInhalation of Conidia → Primary Pulmonary → Haematogenous Dissemination
Risk FactorsImmunocompromise (HIV/CD4<150, Transplant, TNF-α Inhibitors, Steroids), Occupational/Recreational Exposure (Caves, Soil, Construction)
EndemicityGeographically Restricted (See Table Below)
Incubation1-3 Weeks (Acute), Months-Years (Reactivation)
PathogenEndemic RegionVector/Source
Histoplasma capsulatumOhio/Mississippi River Valleys (USA), Central/South America, Africa, SE AsiaBird/Bat Guano (Caves, Chicken Coops, Old Buildings)
Coccidioides immitis/posadasiiSW USA (CA, AZ, NV, NM, TX), N MexicoSoil (Desert, Dust Storms, Construction, Archaeology)
Blastomyces dermatitidisGreat Lakes, SE USA, Ohio/Mississippi River Valleys, Canada (Ontario, Manitoba)Moist Soil, Decaying Wood (Riverbanks, Lakes, Excavation)
Paracoccidioides brasiliensis/lutziiLatin America (Brazil, Colombia, Venezuela, Argentina)Soil (Rural, Agricultural)
Talaromyces (Penicillium) marneffeiSE Asia (Thailand, Vietnam, Hong Kong, Taiwan, NE India)Bamboo Rats, Soil (Immunocompromised Hosts)
[!tip] Endemic Mycoses = Dimorphic Fungi (Mould 25°C → Yeast 37°C). Inhalation → Primary Pulmonary → Dissemination. Geography = Diagnosis Clue.

3. 📖 Specific Endemic Mycoses

1. Histoplasmosis (Histoplasma capsulatum)

AspectDetails
Endemic AreasOhio/Mississippi River Valleys (USA), Central/South America, Africa, SE Asia
ExposureBird/Bat Guano (Caves, Chicken Coops, Old Buildings, Bridge Demolition)
Clinical FormsAcute Pulmonary (Self-Limited), Chronic Pulmonary (Cavitary, COPD-like), Disseminated (Immunocompromised — Adrenal, Skin, CNS, Mucosal), Mediastinal Fibrosis (Broncholithiasis, SVCO)
Acute PulmonaryFlu-like, Cough, Fever, Chest Pain, Hilar Lymphadenopathy, Self-Limited
Chronic PulmonaryCavitary Apollo Upper Lobes, Cough, Haemoptysis, Weight Loss, Mimics TB
DisseminatedHIV/CD4<150, Adrenal Insufficiency (Addison's), Oral/NG Ulcers, Hepatosplenomegaly, CNS

Diagnosis

TestSensitivity/SpecificityRole
Urine/Serum Histoplasma AntigenHigh Sens/Spec (90%+)Rapid, First-Line
Culture (Gold Standard)DefinitiveSlow (2-4 Weeks), BSL-3
Serology (CF, ID, EIA)Antibody DetectionSupportive
HistopathologyYeast in Macrophages (Small, Oval, Intracellular)Diagnostic (Biopsy)
PCRRapidEmerging

Treatment

Syndrome1st LineDuration
Acute Pulmonary (Mild)Observe (Self-Limited) / Itraconazole 200mg BD if Prolonged >1 Month6-12 Weeks
Chronic PulmonaryItraconazole 200mg BD12-24 Months
Disseminated / CNSLAmB 3-5mg/kg/day IV ×2w → Itraconazole 200mg BD12 Months (Lifelong if Immunosuppressed)
Mediastinal FibrosisNo Antifungal → Surgery/Stents if SVCO—

2. Coccidioidomycosis (Valley Fever)

FeatureDetails
AgentCoccidioides immitis (CA), C. posadasii (AZ, MX, Rest)
EndemicSW USA (CA, AZ, NV, NM, UT, TX), N Mexico
ExposureSoil Dust (Construction, Archaeology, Dust Storms, Military, Agriculture)
Clinical60% Asymptomatic; Acute Pneumonia (Valley Fever): Fever, Cough, Fatigue, Erythema Nodosum (Erythema Multiforme), Arthralgia ("Desert Rheumatism"), Chronic Cavitary, Disseminated (Meningitis, Skin, Bone, Joints)
Risk FactorsImmunocompromised, Pregnancy (3rd Trimester), African/Filipino Ancestry (↑ Dissemination)

Diagnosis & Treatment

TestUtility
Serology (CF, ID, EIA)IgM (Early), IgG (Late), CF Titer (Prognosis)
CultureDefinitive (BSL-3)
Antigen (Urine/Serum)Available
Syndrome1st LineDuration
Primary Pulmonary (Mild)Observe / Fluconazole 400mg OD3-6 Months
Chronic Pulmonary / DisseminatedFluconazole 400-800mg OD / Itraconazole 200mg BD12+ Months
MeningitisFluconazole 800-1200mg OD + Intrathecal Amphotericin (Refractory)Lifelong
[!warning] Coccidioidal Meningitis = Lifelong Suppression (Fluconazole 800-1200mg OD). Relapse Common if Stopped.

3. Blastomycosis

FeatureDetails
AgentBlastomyces dermatitidis
EndemicGreat Lakes, SE USA, Ohio/Mississippi River Valleys, Canada (ON, MB)
ExposureMoist Soil, Decaying Wood (Riverbanks, Lakes, Excavation, Construction)
ClinicalAcute Pneumonia (Flu-like), Chronic Pneumonia (Cavitary, Mimics TB), Disseminated (Skin: Verrucous Ulcers, Bone (Osteomyelitis), Prostatitis, CNS)
Skin LesionsVerrucous, Ulcerative, Crusted (Face, Extremities) — "Blastomycotic Dermatitis"

Diagnosis & Treatment

TestUtility
CultureGold Standard (Yeast at 37°C, Broad-Based Budding)
Antigen (Urine/Serum)High Sens/Spec (Cross-Reacts with Histoplasma)
KOH/HistologyBroad-Based Budding Yeast (8-15µm), Thick Wall
Syndrome1st LineDuration
Pulmonary (Mild/Moderate)Itraconazole 200mg BD6-12 Months
Severe / CNS / DisseminatedLAmB 3-5mg/kg/day ×2w → Itraconazole 200mg BD6-12 Months
CNSLAmB 5mg/kg/day ×4-6w → Fluconazole 800mg ODLifelong
[!warning] Blastomycosis = Broad-Based Budding Yeast (8-15µm) on KOH/Histology. Skin Lesions = Verrucous Ulcers. Prostatitis Common in Men.

4. Paracoccidioidomycosis

FeatureDetails
AgentParacoccidioides brasiliensis / P. lutzii
EndemicLatin America (Brazil, Colombia, Venezuela, Argentina, Ecuador)
ClinicalAcute/Subacute (Juvenile): Fever, Lymphadenopathy, Hepatosplenomegaly; Chronic Adult (Adult): Pulmonary (Cavitary), Mucosal (Oral/Pharyngeal — "Mulberry" Ulcers), Adrenal Insufficiency, Lymphadenopathy
Microscopy"Pilot's Wheel" / "Mariner's Wheel" Yeast (Multiple Budding)
SyndromeTreatment
Acute/SubacuteAmphotericin B → Itraconazole 200mg BD × 6-12 Months
ChronicItraconazole 200mg BD × 6-18 Months

5. Talaromycosis (Penicilliosis marneffei)

FeatureDetails
AgentTalaromyces (Penicillium) marneffei
EndemicSE Asia (Thailand, Vietnam, Hong Kong, Taiwan, NE India)
HostHIV/AIDS (CD4<100) — AIDS-Defining Illness
ClinicalFever, Weight Loss, Anaemia, Skin Lesions (Umbilicated Papules, Central Necrosis), Lymphadenopathy, Hepatosplenomegaly, Respiratory, GI
DiagnosisCulture (Red Colony, Soluble Red Pigment), PCR, Histology (Yeast in Macrophages, Transverse Septa)
TreatmentLAmB 3-5mg/kg/day IV ×2w → Itraconazole 200mg BD ×10 Weeks (Secondary Prophylaxis: Itraconazole 200mg OD until Immune Reconstitution)

4. 💡 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DimorphismMould at 25°C → Yeast at 37°C (Thermal Dimorphism)
HistoplasmaOhio/Mississippi Valleys, Bat/Bird Guano, Urine/Serum Ag (Gold Standard), Itraconazole
CoccidioidesSW USA (Valley Fever), Erythema Nodosum, Arthralgia, Fluconazole, Meningitis = Lifelong Suppression
BlastomycesGreat Lakes/SE USA, Broad-Based Budding Yeast, Itraconazole, Skin Lesions (Verrucous), Prostatitis
ParacoccidioidesLatin America, "Pilot's Wheel" Yeast, Mucosal Ulcers ("Mulberry"), Itraconazole
Talaromyces (Penicillium) marneffeiSE Asia, HIV/AIDS, Umbilicated Skin Papules, Red Pigment Culture, LAmB → Itraconazole
Treatment PrinciplesMild: Itraconazole; Severe/CNS: LAmB → Itraconazole; Long Duration (6-24 Months)
DiagnosisAntigen (Urine/Serum) → Rapid, Culture (Gold Standard), PCR
MorphologyHistoplasma: Small Yeast in Macrophage, Blastomyces: Broad-Based Budding, Paracoccidioides: Pilot's Wheel

5. ❓ Viva Questions

  1. What is the endemic region for Histoplasmosis?
  • Ohio and Mississippi River Valleys (USA), also Central/South America, Africa, SE Asia.
  1. What is the classic radiological finding in Chronic Pulmonary Histoplasmosis?
  • Cavitary Lesions in Upper Lobes (Mimics TB).
  1. What is the diagnostic test of choice for Histoplasmosis?
  • Urine/Serum Histoplasma Antigen (High Sens/Spec, Rapid); Culture = Gold Standard.
  1. What is the endemic region for Coccidioidomycosis?
  • SW USA (CA, AZ, NV, NM, UT, TX) + Northern Mexico.
  1. What is the classic triad of Coccidioidomycosis ("Valley Fever")?
  • Fever, Cough, Erythema Nodosum (Plus Arthralgia = "Desert Rheumatism").
  1. What is the treatment for Coccidioidal Meningitis?
  • Fluconazole 800-1200mg OD Lifelong (Relapse Common if Stopped).
  1. What is the characteristic microscopic appearance of Blastomyces?
  • Broad-Based Budding Yeast (8-15µm), Thick Double Wall ("Broad-Based Budding").
  1. What is the endemic area for Blastomycosis?
  • Great Lakes, SE USA, Ohio/Mississippi River Valleys, Canada (Ontario, Manitoba).
  1. What is the characteristic skin lesion of Blastomycosis?
  • Verrucous, Ulcerated, Crusted ("Blastomycotic Dermatitis").
  1. Describe Paracoccidioidomycosis microscopy.
  • "Pilot's Wheel" / "Mariner's Wheel" Yeast (Multiple Budding Daughter Cells Around Mother Cell).
  1. What is the treatment for Paracoccidioidomycosis?
  • Itraconazole 200mg BD × 6-18 Months (Chronic); Amphotericin B → Itraconazole (Acute).
  1. Where is Talaromyces marneffei endemic and who does it affect?
  • SE Asia (Thailand, Vietnam, Hong Kong, Taiwan, NE India); HIV/AIDS (CD4<100) — AIDS-Defining.
  1. Differentiate Histoplasmosis from Blastomycosis on microscopy.
  • Histoplasma: Small Yeast (2-4µm) Inside Macrophages; Blastomyces: Large Yeast (8-15µm), Broad-Based Budding, Thick Wall, Extracellular.

6. 🧠 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Histoplasma vs BlastomycesHistoplasma: Small Intracellular Yeast (2-4µm) in Macrophages; Blastomyces: Large Extracellular Yeast (8-15µm), Broad-Based Budding
Coccidioides vs HistoplasmaCocci: SW USA, Erythema Nodosum, Arthralgia, CF Serology; Histo: Ohio/Mississippi Valleys, Bat Guano, Urine Antigen
Blastomyces vs ParacoccidioidesBlasto: Broad-Based Budding Yeast (8-15µm); Paracoccidioides: "Pilot's Wheel" Multiple Budding
Talaromyces vs PenicilliumT. marneffei = Former Penicillium marneffei; Red Pigment, Transverse Septa, SE Asia + HIV
Itraconazole vs FluconazoleItraconazole = 1st Line for Most Endemic Mycoses; Fluconazole = Coccidioidal Meningitis, Maintenance
MnemonicMeaning
"Histo = Ohio/Mississippi + Bat Guano + Urine Antigen"Histoplasma
"Cocci = SW USA + Valley Fever + Erythema Nodosum + Fluconazole"Coccidioides
"Blasto = Great Lakes + Broad-Based Budding + Skin Ulcers"Blastomyces
"Paracoccidioides = Latin America + Pilot's Wheel + Mucosal Ulcers"Paracoccidioides
"Talaromyces = SE Asia + HIV + Red Pigment + Umbilicated Papules"Talaromycosis
"Dimorphic = Mould 25°C → Yeast 37°C"Thermal Dimorphism

7. 🗺️ Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Endemic Mycoses))
    Dimorphic Fungi
      Mould 25°C → Yeast 37°C
      Inhalation → Primary Pulmonary
    Histoplasma
      Ohio/Mississippi Valleys
      Bat/Bird Guano

    Coccidioides

      Meningitis = Fluconazole Lifelong

    Blastomyces
      Great Lakes / SE USA
      Broad-Based Budding Yeast
      Skin Ulcers / Prostatitis
      Itraconazole
    Paracoccidioides

      Pilot's Wheel Yeast
      Mucosal Ulcers (Mulberry)
      Itraconazole
    Talaromyces (Penicillium) marneffei
      SE Asia

      LAmB → Itraconazole
    Treatment
      Mild: Itraconazole 200mg BD
      Severe/CNS: LAmB → Itraconazole
      Duration: 6-24 Months (Lifelong Meningitis)

8. 📋 One-Page Revision Card

ENDEMIC MYCOSES – FCPS/MRCP REVISION CARD
Histoplasma: Ohio/Mississippi Valleys, Bat Guano, Urine/Serum Antigen (Gold), Itraconazole
Coccidioides: SW USA, Valley Fever, Erythema Nodosum, Arthralgia, Meningitis = Fluconazole Lifelong
Blastomyces: Great Lakes/SE USA, Broad-Based Budding Yeast, Skin Ulcers, Prostatitis, Itraconazole
Paracoccidioides: Latin America, Pilot's Wheel Yeast, Mucosal "Mulberry" Ulcers, Itraconazole
Talaromyces (Penicillium) marneffei: SE Asia, HIV/AIDS, Red Pigment, Umbilicated Papules, LAmB → Itraconazole
All Dimorphic: Mould 25°C → Yeast 37°C, Inhalation, Itraconazole 1st Line
Severe/CNS: LAmB 3-5mg/kg ×2w → Itraconazole 200mg BD
Diagnosis: Antigen (Urine/Serum) Rapid, Culture Gold Standard, PCR, Microscopy (Yeast Morphology)

9. 📅 Spaced Repetition Tracker

ReviewDateScore (1-5)Next Review

10. 🎯 Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know
