topics.title: Fungal Pneumonias
topics.html:
Related: [[Community-Acquired Pneumonia (CAP)]], [[Hospital-Acquired and Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia (HAP-VAP)]], [[Neutropenic Sepsis]], [[Post-Transplant Infections]], [[HIV-Associated Opportunistic Infections]]

[!important]
Fungal pneumonias are opportunistic — they target immunocompromised hosts (neutropenia, transplant, HIV, steroids, biologics). Early diagnosis requires low threshold for invasive diagnostics (BAL, biopsy) and rapid antigen/PCR testing. Empirical therapy often needed before confirmation.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Classify fungal pneumonias by host immune deficit and geographic epidemiology
  • Recognise clinical-radiological syndromes: angioinvasive, airway-invasive, chronic, endemic mycoses
  • Select appropriate diagnostics: galactomannan, β-D-glucan, PCR, culture, histopathology
  • Initiate empirical and targeted antifungal therapy (azole, echinocandin, polyene)
  • Understand prophylaxis strategies in high-risk groups

2. Definition

Fungal pneumonia = lower respiratory tract infection caused by pathogenic or opportunistic fungi. Classified by:

  • Host immune status: neutropenic vs non-neutropenic immunocompromised vs immunocompetent
  • Pathogenesis: angioinvasive (Aspergillus, Mucorales), airway-invasive (Aspergillus tracheobronchitis), chronic (CPA, ABPA), endemic dimorphic (Histoplasma, Coccidioides, Blastomyces)
  • Acquisition: inhalation of conidia/spores (moulds), reactivation (endemic), aspiration (Candida)

3. Core Microbiology — Key Pathogens by Host Context

Host ContextPrimary PathogensKey Features
Prolonged neutropenia (AML, HSCT)Aspergillus fumigatus (80%), A. flavus, A. niger, A. terreus (azole resistance)Angioinvasive, hemorrhagic infarcts, halo sign → air-crescent
Mucorales (Rhizopus, Mucor, Lichtheimia)Ribbon-like aseptate hyphae, 90° branching, vascular invasion, palate/sinus involvement
Fusarium, Scedosporium, LomentosporaResistant moulds; fusarium = bloodstream invasion
Non-neutropenic immunocompromised (steroids, COPD, transplant, biologics, CGD)Aspergillus (chronic, allergic, invasive)CPA, ABPA, invasive aspergillosis (IA)
Pneumocystis jiroveciiHIV CD4<200, transplant, steroids; ground-glass, ↑β-D-glucan
Candida (pneumonia rare)Usually haematogenous dissemination; consider in VAP
Cryptococcus neoformans/gattiiHIV, transplant; nodular/miliary, meningoencephalitis common
Nocardia (bacterium, mimics fungus)Subacute, cavitating, immunocompromised; sulfa treatment
Endemic mycoses (immunocompetent or immunocompromised)Histoplasma capsulatumOhio/Mississippi valleys; bat/bird droppings; intracellular yeast
Coccidioides immitis/posadasiiSW USA, CA, Mexico; "Valley fever"; spherules in tissue
Blastomyces dermatitidisGreat Lakes, SE USA; broad-based budding yeast
Paracoccidioides, Talaromyces (Penicillium) marneffeiLatin America, SE Asia; HIV-associated
EmergomycesEmerging, HIV, Africa/Asia

4. Normal Values / Important Cut-offs

BiomarkerThresholdInterpretation
Serum galactomannan (GM)Index ≥0.5 (or ≥1.0 for specificity)IA in neutropenic; false +ve: π-lactams, Candida, food
BAL galactomannanIndex ≥1.0Higher specificity for pulmonary aspergillosis
Serum β-D-glucan (BDG)≥80 pg/mL (varies by assay)Pan-fungal (Aspergillus, Candida, Pneumocystis, Histoplasma); not Mucorales, Cryptococcus
Aspergillus PCR (blood/BAL)Positive (validated assay)Emerging; not yet standard
Histoplasma antigen (urine/serum)PositiveHigh sensitivity in disseminated/histoplasmosis; cross-reacts Blastomyces
Coccidioides serology (IgM/IgG)PositiveIgM = acute; IgG = past/complement fixation titre for severity
Cryptococcal antigen (CrAg)Positive (LFA, serum/CSF)High sensitivity/specificity; titre correlates with burden
[!tip]
Galactomannan: False positives with piperacillin-tazobactam (cross-reactivity), certain foods, gut translocation. β-D-glucan: False positives with haemodialysis (cellulose membranes), IVIG, gauze, bacteraemia. Cryptococcal Ag: Not detected by BDG (capsular polysaccharide).

5. Clinical Syndromes and Radiology

SyndromeTypical HostRadiologyKey Features
Invasive pulmonary aspergillosis (IPA)Neutropenic, HSCT, steroidsHalo sign (early, CT) → air-crescent sign (recovery), nodules, cavitationFever refractory, pleuritic pain, haemoptysis; angioinvasion → thrombosis, infarction
Chronic pulmonary aspergillosis (CPA)Pre-existing lung disease (TB, COPD, sarcoid)Cavities ± fungal ball (aspergilloma), pleural thickening≥3 months; weight loss, cough, haemoptysis
Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA)Asthma, CFCentral bronchiectasis (glove-finger), mucoid impaction, fleeting infiltratesHigh IgE, Aspergillus-specific IgE/IgG, eosinophilia
Aspergillus tracheobronchitisLung transplant, airway stENTSUlcerative/pseudomembranous/obstructing tracheobronchitisCough, dyspnoea, wheeze; bronchoscopy diagnostic
Pneumocystis pneumonia (PCP)HIV CD4<200, transplant, steroidsBilateral perihilar ground-glass (classic), cysts, pneumothoraxSubacute dyspnoea, dry cough, fever; LDH ↑, SpO₂ ↓ on exertion
MucormycosisDKA, neutropenia, deferoxamine, transplantReverse halo sign, sinus/orbital/palate invasion, vascular thrombosisRapid progression; KOH: ribbon-like aseptate hyphae 90° branching
Disseminated histoplasmosisHIV CD4<150, endemic areaMiliary, mediastinal LAD, hepatosplenomegalyFever, weight loss, pancytopenia; urine Ag +ve
Coccidioidal pneumoniaEndemic area exposureLobar/complex pneumonia, hilar LAD, eosinophilia"Valley fever"; erythema nodosum, arthralgia; spherules on histology
BlastomycosisEndemic area, outdoor exposureMass-like pneumonia, skin lesions, osteoarticular, GUBroad-based budding yeast on KOH/histology

6. Approach / Algorithm

flowchart TD
  A[Suspected fungal pneumonia] --> B{Host immune status}
  B -->|Neutropenic / HSCT| C[High suspicion IPA / Mucorales]
  C --> D[CT chest (halo/air-crescent/reverse halo)]
  D --> E[Serum GM + BDG]
  E --> F[BAL for GM, PCR, culture, histopathology]
  F --> G[Empirical voriconazole / isavuconazole
+ liposomal ampho B if Mucorales suspected]
  B -->|Non-neutropenic immunocompromised| H[Consider IPA, CPA, PCP, Cryptococcus, endemic]
  H --> I[CT chest + serum GM/BDG/CrAg/Histoplasma Ag]
  I --> J[BAL if PCP/IPA suspected
Bronchoscopy if airway disease]
  J --> K[Targeted therapy based on pathogen]
  B -->|Immunocompetent / traveller| L[Endemic mycoses: Histoplasma, Coccidioides, Blastomyces]
  L --> M[Serology, urine Ag, culture, histology]
  M --> N[Itraconazole for mild-moderate; ampho B for severe]

7. Investigations

TestSamplePathogen(s)Utility
CT chest (HRCT)—AllHalo sign, air-crescent, reverse halo, tree-in-bud, cavities, GGO
Serum galactomannanBloodAspergillusScreening in neutropenic; serial monitoring
BAL galactomannanBALAspergillusHigher specificity for IPA
Serum β-D-glucanBloodPan-fungal (not Mucorales, Cryptococcus)Rule-out; monitor response
Aspergillus PCRBlood/BALAspergillusAdjunct; not standalone
Histoplasma antigenUrine/serum/BALHistoplasmaHigh sensitivity disseminated; cross-reacts Blastomyces
Coccidioides serology (IgM/IgG, CF titre)BloodCoccidioidesDiagnosis + severity monitoring
Blastomyces antigenUrine/serumBlastomycesEmerging
Cryptococcal antigen (CrAg LFA)Blood/CSFCryptococcusRapid, high sensitivity
Pneumocystis PCR / DFABAL / induced sputumP. jiroveciiGold standard for PCP
KOH / Calcofluor whiteBAL / sputum / tissueMoulds, yeastsRapid morphology
Fungal cultureBAL / blood / tissueAllSpecies ID, susceptibility; slow (days-weeks)
Histopathology (H&E, PAS, GMS)Tissue (biopsy)AllGold standard for invasive moulds; hyphal morphology

8. Antifungal Therapy

PathogenFirst-LineAlternative / SalvageDuration / Notes
Invasive aspergillosisVoriconazole 6mg/kg IV BD ×2d → 4mg/kg IV/PO BD (TDM target 1–5.5 mg/L)Isavuconazole 372mg (200mg ×3) IV/PO BD ×2d → 372mg OD (no TDM needed); L-AmB 3–5mg/kg IV OD≥6–12 weeks; until immune recovery + clinical/radiological resolution; surgery for solitary lesions
MucormycosisLiposomal amphotericin B 5–10mg/kg IV ODIsavuconazole (secondary); posaconazole delayed-release 300mg PO BDSurgical debridement mandatory; continue until resolution + immune recovery
Chronic pulmonary aspergillosis (CPA)Itraconazole 200mg BD (TDM target 1–2 mg/L) or VoriconazolePosaconazole DR 300mg OD; isavuconazoleMonths to years; monitor IgE, imaging, symptoms
ABPAPrednisolone 0.5mg/kg OD ×2w → taper + itraconazole 200mg BD ×16w (TDM)Voriconazole; omalizumab (refractory)Monitor IgE, CXR, spirometry
Pneumocystis (PCP)TMP-SMX 15–20mg/kg TMP IV/PO TID (HIV: 21d; non-HIV: 14d)Clindamycin + primaquine; pentamidine IV; atovaquone; dapsone + TMPAdjunctive steroids if PaO₂<70 or A-a gradient≥35 (HIV)
Cryptococcal pneumoniaL-AmB 3–5mg/kg + flucytosine 100mg/kg ×2w → fluconazole 400mg OD ×8wFluconazole monotherapy if mildScreen for meningitis (LP); manage raised ICP
Histoplasmosis (moderate-severe/disseminated)L-AmB 3mg/kg IV OD ×1–2w → Itraconazole 200mg BD ×12mFluconazole if itraconazole intolerantMonitor urine Ag clearance; chronic cavitary → itraconazole ≥12m
Coccidioidomycosis (moderate-severe/disseminated)Fluconazole 400–800mg OD or Itraconazole 200mg BDL-AmB if severe/meningitisMeningitis: fluconazole 800–1200mg OD lifelong
Blastomycosis (moderate-severe/disseminated)L-AmB 3–5mg/kg ×1–2w → Itraconazole 200mg BD ×6–12mFluconazole (less effective)CNS/meningitis: L-AmB + fluconazole 800mg OD
[!important]
Voriconazole TDM: Essential — non-linear kinetics, drug interactions (CYP2C19, CYP3A4), hepatotoxicity, visual disturbances, phototoxicity, periostitis (long-term). Isavuconazole = no TDM needed, fewer interactions, QTc shortening (not prolongation), renal adjust not needed.

9. Prophylaxis

PopulationAgentDuration
AML induction / HSCT (neutropenic)Posaconazole DR 300mg PO OD (or IV)Until neutrophil recovery
Voriconazole (if posaconazole intolerant)
Micafungin 50mg IV OD (echinocandin)
Lung transplantVoriconazole / itraconazole / nebulised amphotericin3–12 months (centre-specific)
HIV CD4<200TMP-SMX 960mg PO OD (or 3x/week)Until CD4>200 ×3m
Dapsone; atovaquone; aerosolised pentamidineAlternatives
Prolonged steroids (≥20mg prednisolone >4w)TMP-SMX 960mg OD 3x/weekDuration of immunosuppression

10. Red Flags / Emergencies

  • Haemoptysis (massive) in IPA → bronchial artery embolisation, surgery
  • Airway obstruction from Aspergillus tracheobronchitis / mucormycosis → bronchoscopic debridement, stenting
  • Cavitary rupture → pneumothorax, pyopneumothorax
  • Cerebral mucormycosis (rhino-orbito-cerebral) → emergent surgical debridement + L-AmB
  • Raised ICP in cryptococcal meningitis → serial LP, ventriculostomy
  • Adrenal insufficiency in disseminated histoplasmosis/coccidioidomycosis → steroid replacement

11. Differential Diagnosis

MimicClues
TBCavitation, upper lobe, night sweats, AFB +ve, GeneXpert; endemic overlap
MalignancyMass, weight loss, smoking history; PET-CT; biopsy
Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA)Cavitary nodules, renal, sinuses, c-ANCA/PR3 +ve
Septic emboliMultiple cavitary nodules, IVDU, tricuspid endocarditis, blood cultures
Organising pneumoniaCOP pattern, responsive to steroids, no organism
NocardiaSubacute, cavitating, immunocompromised; modified AFB +ve; sulfa treatment

12. Special Situations

SituationAdjustment
Renal impairmentL-AmB preferred over conventional AmB; voriconazole IV (SBECD vehicle) accumulates — use PO or isavuconazole; flucytosine adjust by CrCl; echinocandins safe
Hepatic impairmentVoriconazole/itraconazole/posaconazole caution (hepatotoxicity); isavuconazole safer; L-AmB safe
Drug interactions (voriconazole/itraconazole/posaconazole)Strong CYP3A4 inhibitors/inducers — avoid with rifampicin, carbamazepine, phenytoin, St John's wort; check all meds (TDM essential)
PregnancyAvoid azoles (teratogenic); L-AmB safe; echinocandins 2nd/3rd trimester if needed; TMP-SMX avoid 1st trimester
Liver transplantVoriconazole/posaconazole interactions with tacrolimus/ciclosporin — dose reduce CNI 50–75%, TDM mandatory

13. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • IPA diagnosis: Host + halo sign/air-crescent + GM + culture/histology (EORTC/MSG criteria: proven/probable/possible)
  • Halo sign = early CT finding in neutropenic IPA (haemorrhagic infarct); air-crescent = recovery (cavitation)
  • Reverse halo sign = mucormycosis (central GGO + consolidated ring)
  • Galactomannan: serum ≥0.5 (screening), BAL ≥1.0 (diagnostic); false +ve with pip-tazo
  • β-D-glucan: pan-fungal; negative in Mucorales and Cryptococcus
  • PCP: bilateral perihilar GGO, CD4<200, high LDH, ↑BDG; induced sputum/BAL PCR/DFA
  • First-line IPA: voriconazole (TDM); alternative isavuconazole (no TDM)
  • First-line mucormycosis: L-AmB 5–10mg/kg + urgent surgical debridement
  • PCP treatment: TMP-SMX high-dose; adjunctive steroids if hypoxic (PaO₂<70)
  • Cryptococcal pneumonia: screen for meningitis (LP); L-AmB + flucytosine induction
  • Endemic mycoses: Histoplasma (urine Ag), Coccidioides (serology CF titre), Blastomyces (broad-based budding yeast, urine Ag)
  • Prophylaxis: posaconazole in AML/HSCT; TMP-SMX in HIV CD4<200 and steroid immunosuppression

14. Common Viva Questions

  1. What is the halo sign and in which condition is it seen? Early CT in neutropenic IPA — haemorrhagic infarction around nodule; indicates angioinvasion.
  2. How does β-D-glucan differ from galactomannan? BDG = pan-fungal (Aspergillus, Candida, Pneumocystis, Histoplasma); GM = Aspergillus-specific. BDG negative in Mucorales, Cryptococcus.
  3. First-line treatment for invasive aspergillosis? Voriconazole (with TDM); alternative isavuconazole.
  4. Mucormycosis management cornerstone? Liposomal amphotericin B + urgent surgical debridement.
  5. When do you give adjunctive steroids in PCP? HIV + PaO₂<70 mmHg or A-a gradient≥35; non-HIV: also if severe hypoxia.
  6. What is the reverse halo sign? Central ground-glass opacity surrounded by ring of consolidation — suggestive of mucormycosis (also organising pneumonia, TB).
  7. Which antifungal requires TDM? Voriconazole, itraconazole, posaconazole, flucytosine. Isavuconazole does not.
  8. Histoplasma vs Blastomyces urine antigen cross-reactivity? Histoplasma Ag cross-reacts with Blastomyces (~80%); Blastomyces Ag more specific.

15. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
Halo sign = air-crescent signHalo = early (hemorrhagic rim); air-crescent = late (cavitation with crescent of air)
BDG positive in all fungiNegative in Mucorales (no β-glucan in cell wall) and Cryptococcus (capsule masks)
Voriconazole = first-line for everythingMucormycosis = L-AmB; CPA = itraconazole; ABPA = steroids + itraconazole
GM positive = proven aspergillosisGM = probable (with host + radiology); proven = histology + culture from sterile site
PCP only in HIVAlso in transplant, steroids, biologics, rheumatology — any T-cell defect
Itraconazole capsules = solutionSolution has better bioavailability; capsules need food/acid; use solution for prophylaxis
Fluconazole for aspergillosisNo activity against moulds (Aspergillus, Mucorales) — only yeasts
Echinocandins for mucormycosisNo activity against Mucorales (lack target β-1,3-glucan synthase)

16. Mnemonics

  • HALO: Haemorrhagic Angioinvasion in Leukopenia On CT = IPA
  • AIR-CRESCENT: Air crescent = Recovery in Chest = Early Sign of Cavitation Emerging Now Treatment working
  • REVERSE HALO = MUCOR (central GGO + consolidation ring)
  • GM FALSE +VE: Pip-tazo, Food, Gut translocation, Candida, Antibiotics (β-lactams)
  • BDG NEGATIVE: Mucorales, Cryptococcus

17. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Fungal Pneumonias))
    By Host
      Neutropenic/HSCT

*...continued (truncated to 200 lines for renderer)*

topics.title: Hiv Infection And Aids
topics.html:
Related: [[Tuberculosis (Pulmonary and Extrapulmonary)]], [[Pneumocystis Pneumonia]], [[Cryptococcal Meningitis]], [[Toxoplasma Encephalitis]], [[CMV Disease]], [[Kaposi Sarcoma]], [[Antiretroviral Therapy]], [[Opportunistic Infections Prophylaxis]]

[!important]
HIV = human immunodeficiency virus (retrovirus, Lentivirus). Targets CD4+ T cells → progressive immune deficiency. Diagnosis: 4th generation Ag/Ab combo → confirmatory HIV-1/HIV-2 differentiation assay. Staging: CDC clinical categories (A/B/C) + CD4 count. ART = lifelong combination therapy (2 NRTI + INSTI preferred). Goal: viral load <50 copies/mL (undetectable = untransmittable, U=U). OI prophylaxis by CD4 thresholds. Cryptococcal antigen screen if CD4<100.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Understand HIV virology, transmission, natural history
  • Diagnose HIV using recommended testing algorithm
  • Stage HIV using CDC clinical categories and CD4 count
  • Initiate ART using preferred regimens (2 NRTI + INSTI)
  • Monitor treatment response (viral load, CD4)
  • Manage virological failure and resistance
  • Prescribe OI prophylaxis by CD4 thresholds
  • Manage HIV in special populations (pregnancy, TB coinfection, HBV/HCV coinfection)
  • Recognise and manage immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS)

2. Virology & Transmission

AspectDetail
VirusHIV-1 (global), HIV-2 (West Africa, slower progression)
TargetCD4+ T lymphocytes (also macrophages, dendritic cells)
CoreceptorsCCR5 (R5-tropic, early infection), CXCR4 (X4-tropic, late)
TransmissionSexual (vaginal, anal), Blood (needles, transfusion), Vertical (MTCT), NOT casual contact
Risk per actReceptive anal > Insertive anal > Receptive vaginal > Insertive vaginal > Oral (negligible)
Infectiousness↑ with high VL, acute infection, STI co-infection; ↓ with ART (U=U)

3. Natural History

StageFeatures
Acute (Primary) HIV2–4 weeks post-exposure: mononucleosis-like illness (fever, pharyngitis, lymphadenopathy, rash, arthralgia), high VL (>10⁶), p24 Ag+, Ab-
Clinical LatencyAsymptomatic, progressive CD4 decline (~50–100 cells/year), VL stabilises (set point)
Symptomatic HIVPersistent generalised lymphadenopathy, mild constitutional symptoms, OIs (e.g., herpes zoster, thrush)
AIDSCD4 <200 OR AIDS-defining illness (Category C)

4. Diagnosis — Testing Algorithm

Step 1: 4th Generation HIV Ag/Ab Combo (p24 Ag + HIV-1/2 Ab)
    ↓
Reactive → Step 2: HIV-1/HIV-2 Differentiation Assay (Geenius, Multispot)
    ↓
    HIV-1 + / HIV-2 −  → HIV-1 POSITIVE
    HIV-1 − / HIV-2 +  → HIV-2 POSITIVE
    HIV-1 + / HIV-2 +  → HIV DUAL POSITIVE
    HIV-1 − / HIV-2 −  → **Step 3: HIV-1 RNA (Viral Load) / NAT**
        ↓
        RNA +  → ACUTE HIV-1 INFECTION
        RNA −  → FALSE POSITIVE (repeat testing)
[!key]
Window period: 4th gen ~18–45 days; NAT ~10–15 days. Acute HIV: p24 Ag+, Ab-, RNA+. Test at baseline, 6 weeks, 3 months if high-risk exposure.

5. Staging — CDC Classification (1993/2014)

CD4 Categories

CategoryCD4 Count
1≥500 cells/μL
2200–499 cells/μL
3<200 cells/μL (AIDS threshold)

Clinical Categories

CategoryDescription
AAsymptomatic, acute HIV, PGL only
BSymptomatic, not Category C (e.g., herpes zoster, thrush, Listeria, bacillary angiomatosis, cervical dysplasia)
CAIDS-defining illnesses (see below)

AIDS-Defining Illnesses (Category C) — Key Examples

SystemConditions
FungalCryptococcal meningitis, Pneumocystis pneumonia (PCP), Oesophageal candidiasis, Histoplasmosis, Coccidioidomycosis
MycobacterialTB (pulmonary/extrapulmonary), MAC, M. kansasii
ViralCMV (retinitis, colitis, encephalitis), HSV (chronic ulcer >1m), VZV (disseminated), HIV encephalopathy, Progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML)
ParasiticToxoplasma encephalitis, Cryptosporidiosis (>1m), Isosporiasis, Microsporidiosis
BacterialRecurrent bacterial pneumonia (≥2/year), Salmonella sepsis (recurrent)
MalignancyKaposi sarcoma, NHL (primary CNS, Burkitt, immunoblastic), Invasive cervical cancer
OtherHIV wasting syndrome, HIV encephalopathy

6. Antiretroviral Therapy (ART)

Goals

  1. Viral suppression: VL <50 copies/mL (undetectable)
  2. Immune recovery: CD4 increase, reduce OIs
  3. Prevent transmission: U=U (Undetectable = Untransmittable)
  4. Improve quality of life and survival

Preferred First-Line Regimens (2 NRTI + INSTI)

RegimenNRTI BackboneINSTINotes
Bictegravir/Tenofovir alafenamide/Emtricitabine (BIC/TAF/FTC)TAF/FTC (single tablet)BICPreferred (once daily, minimal SE, no boosting, high barrier)
Dolutegravir (DTG) + TAF/FTCTAF/FTCDTG 50mg ODPreferred (DTG separate tablet)
DTG + TDF/FTCTDF/FTCDTG 50mg ODIf TAF unavailable; TDF = renal/bone toxicity
DTG + Abacavir/Lamivudine (ABC/3TC)ABC/3TCDTG 50mg ODOnly if HLA-B*57:01 NEGATIVE (hypersensitivity)

Alternative First-Line Regimens

RegimenUse Case
Raltegravir (RAL) + TAF/FTCINSTI option (twice daily)
Elvitegravir/cobicistat/TAF/FTC (EVG/c/TAF/FTC)Single tablet, but boosting (cobicistat) = interactions
Darunavir/ritonavir (DRV/r) + TAF/FTCPI-based (if INSTI contraindicated)
Doravirine (DOR) + TDF/3TCNNRTI-based (if INSTI/PI contraindicated)
[!critical]
INSTI-based = preferred (high barrier, fewer SE, rapid VL suppression). DTG: 50mg OD with rifampicin → 50mg BD. BIC: no dose adjustment with rifampicin (avoid if possible). ABC: MANDATORY HLA-B*57:01 testing before use.

NRTI Backbone Options

NRTI PairDoseKey Toxicity
TAF/FTC25/200mg ODPreferred (less renal/bone toxicity than TDF)
TDF/FTC300/200mg ODRenal (Fanconi), bone loss (↓ BMD)
ABC/3TC600/300mg ODHLA-B*57:01 hypersensitivity (fever, rash, hypoxia); CVD risk signal

7. ART Initiation — When to Start

ScenarioTiming
All HIV+ (regardless of CD4)ASAP (same day/rapid start if ready)
TB coinfectionCD4 <50: within 2 weeks; CD4 50–100: 2–8 weeks; CD4 >100: within 8 weeks
Cryptococcal meningitisDefer ART 4–6 weeks (after amphotericin induction, to reduce IRIS)
TB meningitisDefer ART 4–6 weeks
Acute OI (non-CM, non-TBM)Start ART as soon as OI treatment tolerated (usually within 2 weeks)

8. Monitoring on ART

ParameterTiming
Viral Load (VL)Baseline, 4–8 weeks, 3–4 months, then every 6 months (if suppressed)
CD4 CountBaseline, 3 months, then every 6–12 months (can stop if VL suppressed >1 year + CD4>350)
Renal function (eGFR)Baseline, 1 month, then 6-monthly (TAF/TDF)
Bone density (DEXA)If TDF long-term, risk factors
Lipids, GlucoseBaseline, 3–6 months (PIs, some INSTIs)
HLA-B*57:01Before ABC
Resistance testingBaseline (before ART), at virological failure

Virological Response Definitions

TermDefinition
Virological suppressionVL <50 copies/mL (or <200 copies/mL for some assays)
Virological failureVL >200 copies/mL on 2 consecutive tests (after 6 months on ART)
Low-level viraemiaVL 50–200 copies/mL (blips usually transient)
BlipSingle VL 50–1000 then re-suppressed (not failure)

9. Management of Virological Failure

StepAction
1. Confirm failureVL >200 on 2 tests, adherence assessment
2. Resistance testingGenotypic (protease, RT, integrase) on current failing regimen
3. New regimen≥2 (preferably 3) fully active agents based on resistance
4. Preferred salvageDTG/BIC + optimised NRTI backbone + DRV/r or new agent (lenacapavir, fostemsavir, ibalizumab)
[!tip]
DTG/BIC have high genetic barrier; resistance rare with INSTI-based first-line. PI (DRV/r) also high barrier. NNRTI (EFV, NVP, RPV, DOR) low barrier — single mutation = resistance.

10. Opportunistic Infection Prophylaxis — CD4 Thresholds

OIProphylaxis IndicationAgentDiscontinue When
PCPCD4 <200 OR oropharyngeal candidiasis OR prior PCPTMP-SMX 160/800mg PO daily (or 3×/week)CD4 >200 for ≥3 months on ART
ToxoplasmaCD4 <100 + IgG+TMP-SMX (same as PCP)CD4 >200 for ≥3 months on ART
MACCD4 <50Azithromycin 1200mg PO weekly (or Clarithromycin 500mg PO daily + Rifabutin)CD4 >100 for ≥3 months on ART
CryptococcalScreen: CrAg if CD4 <100If CrAg+: Fluconazole 800mg OD ×2w → 400mg OD ×8w → 200mg OD maintenanceCD4 >100 on ART + VL suppressed
HSV/VZVSeropositive (or all if unknown)Acyclovir 400mg PO 12h (or Valacyclovir)Usually lifelong if seropositive
CMVNot routinely (pre-emptive therapy)Valganciclovir if reactivationN/A
[!key]
PCP/Toxo prophylaxis: TMP-SMX covers both. CrAg screen at CD4<100: if positive, pre-emptive fluconazole prevents meningitis. MAC prophylaxis: Azithromycin weekly.

11. Immune Reconstitution Inflammatory Syndrome (IRIS)

TypeTimingFeatures
Paradoxical IRIS2–12 weeks post-ARTWorsening of known, treated OI (TB, Cryptococcus, MAC, CMV, herpes)
Unmasking IRIS2–12 weeks post-ARTNew OI diagnosis after ART start (subclinical → clinical)

Risk Factors

  • Low CD4 at ART initiation (<50, especially <25)
  • High baseline VL
  • Rapid VL decline
  • Recent OI treatment (especially TB, Cryptococcus)

Management

SeverityManagement
MildContinue ART + OI treatment; NSAIDs
ModerateContinue ART + OI treatment; Prednisolone 1–1.5mg/kg/day taper 2–4 weeks
SevereHold ART temporarily (rare); high-dose steroids; specialist input
[!warning]
TB-IRIS: do NOT stop TB treatment; add prednisolone. Cryptococcal IRIS: higher mortality; manage aggressively. Do NOT delay ART for fear of IRIS (except CM/TBM).

12. HIV/TB Coinfection

CD4ART Timing
<50Within 2 weeks of TB treatment
50–1002–8 weeks
>100Within 8 weeks
  • Rifampicin + DTG: DTG 50mg BD (double dose)
  • Rifampicin + BIC: Avoid (significant interaction)
  • Rifampicin + PI: Avoid (use DRV/r with dose adjustment or DTG)
  • Rifampicin + EFV: Standard dose OK

13. HIV/HBV Coinfection

  • TAF/FTC or TDF/FTC covers both HIV and HBV (preferred backbone)
  • If HBV+ and HIV+: MUST use HBV-active NRTIs (TDF or TAF + FTC/3TC)

...continued (truncated to 200 lines for renderer)

Topic Overview

1. Learning Objectives
2. Virology & Transmission
3. Natural History
4. Diagnosis — Testing Algorithm
5. Staging — CDC Classification (1993/2014)
CD4 Categories
Clinical Categories
AIDS-Defining Illnesses (Category C) — Key Examples
6. Antiretroviral Therapy (ART)
Goals
Preferred First-Line Regimens (2 NRTI + INSTI)
Alternative First-Line Regimens
NRTI Backbone Options
7. ART Initiation — When to Start
8. Monitoring on ART
Virological Response Definitions
9. Management of Virological Failure
10. Opportunistic Infection Prophylaxis — CD4 Thresholds
11. Immune Reconstitution Inflammatory Syndrome (IRIS)
Risk Factors
Management
12. HIV/TB Coinfection
13. HIV/HBV Coinfection

topics.title: Healthcare Associated Infections Clabsi Cauti Ssi Vap
topics.html:
Related: [[Antimicrobial Prescribing Principles]], [[Antimicrobial Stewardship]], [[Infection in Immunocompromised Host (Transplant, Biologics)]], [[Sepsis and Septic Shock]], [[Clostridioides difficile Infection (CDI)]], [[Infection in Pregnancy and Neonates]], [[Urinary Tract Infections (Complicated, CA-UTI, Pyelonephritis)]], [[Hospital-Acquired and Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia (HAP-VAP)]]

[!important]
HAI = infection acquired ≥48h after hospital admission (or ≤48h if healthcare-associated). CLABSI, CAUTI, VAP, SSI = ~80% of HAI. Prevention bundles = evidence-based, reduce HAI 30–70%. Surveillance = NHSN definitions; rate per 1000 device-days (CLABSI, CAUTI, VAP) or per 100 procedures (SSI). Antimicrobial stewardship + IPC = core strategies.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Define HAI and classify by type (CLABSI, CAUTI, VAP, SSI, CDI)
  • Apply diagnostic criteria (CDC/NHSN) for each HAI type
  • Implement evidence-based prevention bundles (CLABSI, CAUTI, VAP, SSI)
  • Select appropriate empirical therapy by HAI type and local epidemiology
  • Implement surveillance and outbreak investigation
  • Apply antimicrobial stewardship principles to HAI management

2. Definitions & Surveillance

HAI TypeDefinitionRate Denominator
CLABSICentral line-associated BSI — laboratory-confirmed BSI in patient with central line ≥48h, not secondary to another site1000 central line-days
CAUTICatheter-associated UTI — symptomatic UTI with indwelling catheter ≥48h1000 catheter-days
VAPVentilator-associated pneumonia — pneumonia in patient on invasive ventilation ≥48h1000 ventilator-days
SSISurgical site infection — infection at surgical site within 30d (90d if implant)100 procedures
CDIClostridioides difficile infection — see CDI note10,000 patient-days
[!key]
HAI = infection ≥48h after admission (not incubating at admission). CLABSI/CAUTI/VAP rates per 1000 device-days; SSI per 100 procedures.

3. Central Line-Associated BSI (CLABSI)

Diagnosis (CDC/NHSN)

CriterionDetails
Central lineIn place ≥48h before culture (or day of device removal)
Laboratory-confirmed BSI≥1 blood culture with recognised pathogen OR ≥2 blood cultures with common skin commensal (CoNS, Corynebacterium, Bacillus, Micrococcus) + clinical signs (fever, hypotension)
Not secondaryNo other site infection explaining BSI

Common Pathogens

Pathogen%Key Feature
Coagulase-negative Staphylococci (CoNS)30–40%Most common; often contamination but true CLABSI if ≥2 cultures
Staphylococcus aureus15–20%MSSA/MRSA; high mortality
Enterococcus spp.10–15%VRE increasing
Candida spp.10–15%C. albicans, C. glabrata, C. parapsilosis (biofilm)
Gram-negative (Klebsiella, E. coli, Pseudomonas, Enterobacter)15–20%ESBL/CRE concern
Other<5%Bacillus, Corynebacterium, Stenotrophomonas

CLABSI Prevention Bundle (IHI/CDC)

ComponentAction
Hand hygieneBefore/after line access; alcohol-based rub
Maximal barrier precautionsSterile gloves, gown, cap, mask, large drape for insertion
Chlorhexidine skin prep2% CHG in 70% alcohol (superior to povidone-iodine)
Optimal site selectionSubclavian > Jugular > Femoral (lowest infection risk)
Daily review of line necessityRemove as soon as no longer needed
Chlorhexidine dressingsCHG-impregnated sponge/dressing for lines >7d
Hub disinfectionScrub hub 15s with 2% CHG/70% alcohol before each access
Avoid routine replacementGuidewire exchange NOT recommended for CLABSI prevention
[!tip]
Subclavian site = lowest CLABSI risk. CHG skin prep + maximal barrier = strongest evidence. Daily line necessity review = most impactful. CHG dressings for lines >7d reduce CLABSI 50%.

CLABSI Empirical Therapy

ScenarioRegimen
No MDR risk, stableCeftriaxone 2g IV 24h + Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg 6h
MDR risk (prior abx, ICU, known colonisation)Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g 6h OR Meropenem 1g 8h (+ Vancomycin)
Suspected CandidaEchinocandin (caspofungin/micafungin/anidulafungin)
Line removalStrongly recommended for S. aureus, Candida, Pseudomonas, Bacillus, mycobacteria; consider salvage for CoNS

4. Catheter-Associated UTI (CAUTI)

Diagnosis (CDC/NHSN)

CriterionDetails
Indwelling catheter≥48h (or day of removal)
SymptomsFever (>38°C), suprapubic pain, CVA tenderness, altered mental status, rigors
Microbiology≥10³ CFU/mL of ≥1 organism from catheter specimen OR ≥10⁵ CFU/mL from voided specimen

Common Pathogens

Pathogen%
E. coli25–30%
Klebsiella15–20%
Pseudomonas10–15%
Enterococcus10–15%
Candida5–10%
Proteus, Morganella, Providencia10–15%

CAUTI Prevention Bundle

ComponentAction
Avoid unnecessary catheterisationUse alternatives (condom catheter, intermittent cath, nappies)
Aseptic insertionSterile technique, smallest appropriate catheter
Closed drainage systemMaintain unobstructed flow; no disconnections
Catheter securementPrevent traction/urethral trauma
Drainage bag below bladderPrevent reflux
Daily necessity reviewNurse-driven protocol: remove ASAP
Periurethral cleaningRoutine soap/water (no antiseptic benefit)
Antibiotic prophylaxis at insertionNOT routinely recommended
Catheter materialSilver-alloy or hydrogel-coated if prolonged (>7d) anticipated
[!key]
Daily review of catheter necessity with nurse-driven removal protocol = single most effective intervention. Avoid unnecessary catheterisation = primary prevention.

CAUTI Management

StepAction
1. Remove/replace catheterIf ≥2 days old; send tip for culture if removed
2. Empirical antibioticsAs per complicated UTI (see UTI note)
3. Duration7 days if catheter removed; 10–14 days if catheter must remain
4. Catheter changeReplace after 48–72h of antibiotics if catheter must stay

5. Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia (VAP)

Diagnosis (CDC/NHSN — Clinical + Microbiological)

CriterionDetails
VentilationInvasive ventilation ≥48h
Chest X-rayNew/progressive infiltrate
ClinicalFever/hypothermia, ↑ WBC, purulent secretions, ↑ O₂ requirement
MicrobiologyQualitative/semi-quantitative culture (endotracheal aspirate ≥10⁵ CFU/mL; BAL ≥10⁴ CFU/mL)
ExcludeNon-infectious causes (atelectasis, ARDS, pulmonary oedema)

Common Pathogens

TimeframePathogens
Early VAP (≤5 days)S. aureus (MSSA), S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, Enterobacteriaceae
Late VAP (>5 days)MRSA, Pseudomonas, Acinetobacter, Stenotrophomonas, Enterobacteriaceae (ESBL/CRE)

VAP Prevention Bundle (IHI/CDC)

ComponentAction
Head of bed elevation30–45° (unless contraindicated)
Daily sedation vacationDaily sedation interruption + spontaneous breathing trial
Peptic ulcer prophylaxisPPI/H2 blocker (balance with C. difficile risk)
DVT prophylaxisPharmacological (unless contraindicated)
Oral careChlorhexidine 0.12–2% oral rinse q6–12h
Subglottic secretion drainageEndotracheal tube with subglottic suction port
Early extubationDaily assessment for readiness to extubate
Hand hygiene + PPEStandard + contact for MDRO
[!tip]
Head of bed 30–45° + daily sedation vacation + SBT = strongest evidence. Subglottic secretion drainage reduces VAP 50%.

VAP Empirical Therapy

ScenarioRegimen
Early VAP, no MDR riskCeftriaxone 2g 24h +/− Vancomycin (if MRSA risk)
Late VAP / MDR riskPiperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g 6h OR Meropenem 1g 8h + Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg 6h
MRSA riskAdd Vancomycin (trough 15–20) OR Linezolid
Pseudomonas riskAnti-pseudomonal β-lactam (Pip-tazo, Meropenem, Cefepime, Ceftazidime) +/− Aminoglycoside
Duration7 days (short-course non-inferior to 14d); stop if culture negative

6. Surgical Site Infection (SSI)

Classification (CDC)

TypeDepthTimeframe
Superficial IncisionalSkin/subcutaneous≤30 days
Deep IncisionalFascia/muscle≤30 days (90 days if implant)
Organ/SpaceOrgan/space manipulated≤30 days (90 days if implant)

Risk Factors

PatientOperativePostoperative
Diabetes, obesity, smoking, immunosuppression, malnutrition, age >60, prolonged pre-op stayDuration >T, contaminated/dirty wound, emergency, implant, blood loss >1L, poor techniqueHypothermia, hyperglycaemia, hypoxaemia, haematoma, drain, transfusion

SSI Prevention Bundle (WHO/CDC)

PhaseIntervention
Pre-operativeShower with CHG night before + morning; No hair removal (or clippers if necessary); Antibiotic prophylaxis within 60 min before incision (cefazolin 2g IV; add metronidazole if colorectal); Glycaemic control (perioperative glucose <180); Normothermia (>36°C); Oxygenation (FiO₂ 80% intraop + 2h post)
Intra-operativeSkin prep: CHG 2% in alcohol; Sterile technique; Minimise OR traffic; Maintain normothermia; Glycaemic control
Post-operativeContinue antibiotic ≤24h (no benefit beyond); Glycaemic control (first 48h); Normothermia; Early mobilisation; Wound care (sterile dressing 48h)

SSI Empirical Therapy

ScenarioRegimen
Superficial, MSSA/StrepFlucloxacillin 2g IV 6h / Cephalexin 500mg 6h
Deep/Organ space, no MDROCeftriaxone 2g 24h + Metronidazole 500mg 8h
MRSA riskVancomycin 15–20mg/kg 6h +/− Ceftriaxone/Metronidazole
Gram-negative / MDROPiperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g 6h OR Meropenem 1g 8h
Duration5–7 days (superficial); 7–14 days (deep/organ space); source control critical (drainage, debridement)

7. Antimicrobial Stewardship in HAI

InterventionDescription
Formulary restrictionCarbapenems, newer agents (CAV, MV, IR, plazomicin) require ID approval
Prospective audit & feedbackID pharmacist/physician reviews at 48–72h; recommends de-escalation/stop
IV→oral switchProtocol-driven at 48–72h if clinical stability
Automatic stop ordersDefault durations (e.g., VAP 7d, CAUTI 7d, SSI 5–7d) with mandatory renewal
Biomarker guidancePCT-guided antibiotic discontinuation in sepsis/VAP
Penicillin allergy de-labellingAssessment + graded challenge → ↓ vancomycin/fluoroquinolone use
Diagnostic stewardshipDo NOT culture asymptomatic patients (catheter tips, urine, blood without clinical signs)
Device removalCLABSI: remove for S. aureus, Candida, Pseudomonas, Bacillus, mycobacteria; CAUTI: remove if possible; VAP: no routine line removal

...continued (truncated to 200 lines for renderer)

Topic Overview

2. Definitions & Surveillance
3. Central Line-Associated BSI (CLABSI)
Diagnosis (CDC/NHSN)
Common Pathogens
CLABSI Prevention Bundle (IHI/CDC)

Diagnosis (CDC/NHSN)
Common Pathogens
CAUTI Prevention Bundle
CAUTI Management
5. Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia (VAP)
Diagnosis (CDC/NHSN — Clinical + Microbiological)
Common Pathogens
VAP Prevention Bundle (IHI/CDC)

topics.title: Hospital Acquired And Ventilator Associated Pneumonia Hap Vap
topics.html:
Related: [[Community-Acquired Pneumonia (CAP)]], [[Aspiration Pneumonia]], [[Sepsis and Septic Shock]], [[Clostridioides difficile Infection]], [[Antimicrobial Stewardship]]

[!important]
HAP = pneumonia ≥48h after hospital admission, not incubating at admission. VAP = pneumonia ≥48h after endotracheal intubation. Non-ventilator HAP (NV-HAP) = HAP without intubation. Diagnosis: clinical + radiographic + microbiological. Empirical antibiotics by local epidemiology, risk factors for MDR. De-escalate at 48–72h. Duration 7 days (shorter if clinical criteria met). VAP prevention bundle.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Define HAP, VAP, NV-HAP and distinguish from CAP
  • Apply clinical, radiological, and microbiological diagnostic criteria
  • Identify risk factors for MDR pathogens
  • Select empirical antibiotics by local antibiogram and MDR risk
  • Interpret quantitative vs qualitative cultures (VAP)
  • De-escalate therapy at 48–72h based on cultures
  • Apply 7-day treatment duration (shorter if criteria met)
  • Implement VAP prevention bundle

2. Definitions & Epidemiology

TermDefinitionTimingTypical Pathogens
HAPPneumonia ≥48h after admission, not incubating at admission≥48h post-admissionS. aureus, Pseudomonas, Enterobacteriaceae, Acinetobacter
VAPPneumonia ≥48h after endotracheal intubation≥48h post-intubationPseudomonas, S. aureus, Enterobacteriaceae, Acinetobacter
NV-HAPHAP without mechanical ventilation≥48h post-admissionSimilar to HAP but more S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae
Early-onset HAP/VAP≤4 days hospitalisation≤4 daysSimilar to CAP (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, MSSA)
Late-onset HAP/VAP≥5 days hospitalisation≥5 daysMDR pathogens (Pseudomonas, MRSA, Acinetobacter, ESBL)
[!tip]
HAP/VAP = 2nd most common nosocomial infection (after UTI). Mortality 20–50% (attributable ~10–15%). VAP increases ICU stay by 4–6 days, cost $40K+.

3. Risk Factors for MDR Pathogens

Risk FactorPathogens of Concern
Prior IV antibiotics (≤90 days)ESBL Enterobacteriaceae, MDR Pseudomonas, MRSA, Acinetobacter
Current hospitalisation ≥5 daysLate-onset flora (MDR Gram-neg, MRSA)
ICU admissionPseudomonas, Acinetobacter, Stenotrophomonas
Mechanical ventilationPseudomonas, Acinetobacter
Structural lung disease (bronchiectasis, COPD)Pseudomonas, S. aureus
ImmunosuppressionFungi, Pneumocystis, CMV, Nocardia, Mycobacteria
Recent travel (high MDR regions)NDM/ESBL Enterobacteriaceae, Acinetobacter
Known colonisationSame organism likely
[!key]
MDR risk factors: prior antibiotics, ≥5 days hospitalisation, ICU, ventilation, known colonisation. Local antibiogram essential for empirical choice.

4. Diagnostic Criteria

Clinical + Radiological (All Required for Possible HAP/VAP)

  1. New/progressive infiltrate on CXR/CT (or ≥2 serial CXRs for VAP)
  2. Plus ≥2 of:
  • Fever >38.3°C or hypothermia <36°C
  • Leukocytosis (>10×10⁹/L) or leukopenia (<4×10⁹/L)
  • Purulent respiratory secretions
  • Worsening gas exchange (↑FiO₂, ↓PaO₂/FiO₂, new/increased ventilator support)

Microbiological (For Probable/Confirmed)

Sample TypeThresholdSignificance
Endotracheal aspirate (ETA)≥10⁵ CFU/mLGood sensitivity, low specificity (colonisation)
Bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL)≥10⁴ CFU/mLBetter specificity
Protected specimen brush (PSB)≥10³ CFU/mLHigher specificity, more invasive
Mini-BAL≥10⁴ CFU/mLCompromise
Blood culturePositiveDefinitive (haematogenous spread rare)
Pleural fluidPositiveDefinitive if empyema
[!tip]
Quantitative cultures (BAL ≥10⁴, PSB ≥10³) > qualitative for VAP — reduce overtreatment. Clinical Pulmonary Infection Score (CPIS) ≥6 supports VAP but not diagnostic alone.

5. Empirical Antibiotic Therapy (Start IMMEDIATELY after cultures)

Strategy: Cover likely pathogens based on LOCAL ANTIBIOGRAM + MDR risk factors

Low MDR Risk (Early-onset, no risk factors, low local MDR prevalence)

RegimenDose
Piperacillin-tazobactam4.5g IV 6h
OR Cefepime2g IV 8h
OR Ceftriaxone2g IV 12h
OR Levofloxacin750mg IV OD
OR Meropenem1g IV 8h

High MDR Risk (≥1 risk factor) — MUST Cover: Pseudomonas, MRSA, ESBL

Regimen (Dual Pseudomonas + MRSA + ESBL coverage)Doses
Anti-pseudomonal β-lactam + Anti-MRSA agent
Pip-tazo 4.5g 6h OR Cefepime 2g 8h OR Meropenem 1g 8h OR Ceftazidime 2g 8h+
Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg IV 6h (trough 15–20) OR Linezolid 600mg IV 12h
[!warning]
If local Pseudomonas resistance to Pip-tazo >10–15% → use Meropenem/Cefepime as primary β-lactam. If MRSA prevalence >10–20% → ADD anti-MRSA agent empirically. If ESBL prevalence high → Carbapenem preferred.

Additional Coverage if Indicated

ScenarioAdd
Anaerobes suspected (aspiration, necrotising)Metronidazole 500mg IV 8h (if β-lactam not anaerobic-active)
Legionella (immunocompromised, water system outbreak)Azithromycin 500mg IV/PO OD
Fungal (prolonged ICU, immunosuppressed)Echinocandin (Caspofungin/Micafungin)

6. Culture Interpretation & De-escalation (At 48–72 Hours)

Culture ResultAction

| Negative (no growth)

  • Qualitative: Consider stopping antibiotics if clinical improvement
  • Quantitative (BAL <10⁴, PSB <10³): Stop antibiotics (high NPV) |
Single pathogen susceptibleDe-escalate to narrowest effective agent
MDR pathogen confirmedContinue/Adjust targeted therapy
PolymicrobialCover all; consider anaerobes if aspiration

De-escalation Principles

  1. Stop antibiotics if cultures negative + clinical improvement (especially quantitative)
  2. Narrow spectrum to targeted pathogen(s)
  3. Switch IV → oral if clinical stability (same criteria as CAP)
  4. Remove redundant agents (e.g., drop vancomycin if MSSA, drop second Gram-neg agent)

7. Treatment Duration

ScenarioDuration
Uncomplicated HAP/VAP7 days (IDSA/ATS 2016)
Non-fermenting Gram-neg (Pseudomonas, Acinetobacter)7 days (extend if slow response)
MRSA7 days
Clinical stability criteria at day 7Stop if: afebrile 48h, WBC normalising, improving gas exchange, tolerating oral
Slow response / immunocompromised / empyema / abscessExtend to 10–14 days
[!key]
7 days = standard for uncomplicated HAP/VAP. Shorter (5–7d) non-inferior in RCTs. Do NOT prolong "just in case" — drives resistance.

8. VAP Prevention Bundle (Institute for Healthcare Improvement)

ComponentAction
Head of bed elevation30–45° (unless contraindicated)
Daily sedation interruption + SBTDaily (spontaneous breathing trial)
Peptic ulcer prophylaxisPPI/H2 blocker (balance with C. diff risk)
DVT prophylaxisLMWH/UFH + mechanical
Oral care with chlorhexidine0.12–2% CHX q6–12h (controversial for mortality, reduces VAP)
Subglottic secretion drainageEndotracheal tube with subglottic suction port (reduces VAP ~50%)
Cuff pressure20–30 cmH₂O (prevents microaspiration)
Avoid unnecessary intubationNIV when appropriate
Early extubationDaily readiness assessment

9. Pathogen-Specific Targeted Therapy

PathogenPreferred Agent(s)AlternativesDuration
MSSAFlucloxacillin/Cefazolin 2g 4–6hPip-tazo, Meropenem7 days
MRSAVancomycin (trough 15–20) OR Linezolid 600mg 12hDaptomycin, Ceftaroline7 days
P. aeruginosa (susceptible)Pip-tazo/Cefepime/Meropenem/CeftazidimeCiprofloxacin/Levofloxacin7 days
MDR Pseudomonas (XDR)Ceftolozane-tazobactam, Ceftazidime-avibactam, Imipenem-relebactam, Polymyxin + high-dose meropenemAminoglycoside + β-lactam7–14 days
ESBL EnterobacteriaceaeMeropenem 1g 8h (preferred)Ertapenem (if no Pseudomonas risk)7 days
Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacteriaceae (CRE)Ceftazidime-avibactam, Meropenem-vaborbactam, Imipenem-relebactam, Plazomicin, Polymyxin-basedTigecycline (not for VAP — poor lung penetration)7–14 days
Acinetobacter baumanniiMeropenem/Sulbactam (if susceptible)Polymyxin, Tigecycline, Minocycline, Ampicillin-sulbactam7–14 days
Stenotrophomonas maltophiliaTMP-SMX 5mg/kg (TMP) 6hLevofloxacin, Minocycline, Ceftazidime-avibactam7–14 days

10. Special Situations

SituationAdjustment
ImmunocompromisedBroader empirical (add antifungal, PJP cover, antiviral); BAL preferred
Post-lung transplantCover Pseudomonas, Aspergillus, CMV, Nocardia; BAL mandatory
Structural lung disease (bronchiectasis, CF)Higher Pseudomonas risk; antipseudomonal β-lactam + fluoroquinolone/aminoglycoside
Penicillin allergy (anaphylaxis)Aztreonam + Fluoroquinolone + Vancomycin/Linezolid
Renal impairmentDose adjust all; avoid aminoglycosides if possible

11. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • HAP = ≥48h post-admission; VAP = ≥48h post-intubation; NV-HAP = HAP without vent
  • Early-onset (≤4d) = CAP-like flora; Late-onset (≥5d) = MDR flora
  • MDR risk factors: prior antibiotics (90d), ≥5d hospitalisation, ICU, vent, known colonisation
  • Diagnosis: new infiltrate + clinical signs + microbiological confirmation (quantitative cultures preferred for VAP)
  • Empirical: local antibiogram + MDR risk → anti-pseudomonal β-lactam ± anti-MRSA agent
  • Quantitative cultures: BAL ≥10⁴, PSB ≥10³ = positive; negative = stop antibiotics if improving
  • De-escalate at 48–72h: stop if negative, narrow if positive, switch IV→oral if stable
  • Duration: 7 days standard (shorter if criteria met)
  • VAP bundle: HOB 30–45°, daily sedation hold + SBT, subglottic suction, chlorhexidine oral care, cuff pressure 20–30
  • CRE: ceftazidime-avibactam/meropenem-vaborbactam/imipenem-relebactam; avoid tigecycline for VAP
  • Stenotrophomonas: TMP-SMX first-line

12. Common Viva Questions

  1. Difference between HAP, VAP, NV-HAP? HAP ≥48h post-admission; VAP ≥48h post-intubation; NV-HAP = HAP without ventilation.
  2. Early vs late onset HAP/VAP? Early ≤4d = CAP-like flora; Late ≥5d = MDR flora (Pseudomonas, MRSA, Acinetobacter).
  3. MDR risk factors for HAP/VAP? Prior IV antibiotics ≤90d, hospitalisation ≥5d, ICU, mechanical ventilation, known colonisation.
  4. VAP diagnostic criteria? New/progressive infiltrate + ≥2 clinical signs + quantitative culture (BAL ≥10⁴, PSB ≥10³).
  5. Empirical antibiotics for high MDR risk VAP? Anti-pseudomonal β-lactam (Pip-tazo/Meropenem/Cefepime) + anti-MRSA (Vancomycin/Linezolid).
  6. When to de-escalate? 48–72h: stop if cultures negative + improving; narrow to targeted pathogen; switch IV→oral if stable.
  7. Standard treatment duration? 7 days for uncomplicated HAP/VAP.
  8. VAP prevention bundle components? HOB 30–45°, daily sedation hold + SBT, subglottic suction, chlorhexidine oral care, cuff pressure 20–30 cmH₂O, DVT/PUD prophylaxis.
  9. CRE treatment options? Ceftazidime-avibactam, Meropenem-vaborbactam, Imipenem-relebactam, Plazomicin, Polymyxin-based.
  10. Stenotrophomonas maltophilia treatment? TMP-SMX first-line (5mg/kg TMP 6h).

13. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
HCAP still existsHCAP retired — assess MDR risk factors within HAP/VAP
Qualitative cultures enough for VAPQuantitative (BAL/PSB) preferred — higher specificity, allows stopping if negative
Always need dual Gram-neg coverageDual only if high resistance prevalence or severe sepsis; monotherapy with appropriate β-lactam often sufficient
Vancomycin for all HAP/VAPOnly if MRSA risk factors (prevalence >10–20%, prior MRSA, necrotising)
14–21 days standard duration7 days standard; extend only if slow response / complications
Tigecycline for VAPPoor lung penetration — avoid for VAP
Colistin first-line for CRENewer β-lactam/β-lactamase inhibitors preferred (ceftazidime-avibactam etc.)
Chlorhexidine oral care reduces mortalityReduces VAP incidence but NOT mortality
Subglottic suction for all ventsOnly for expected >24–48h ventilation
Stop antibiotics if qualitative culture negativeQualitative negative ≠ no infection; quantitative negative (BAL<10⁴) has high NPV

14. Mnemonics

  • HAP/VAP DEFINITIONS: HAP = Hospital ≥48h; VAP = Vent ≥48h; NV-HAP = Non-Vent HAP
  • MDR RISK: Prior abx (90d), Hospital ≥5d, ICU, Vent, Known colonisation (PHI VK)
  • EARLY vs LATE: Early (≤4d) = Easy flora (CAP-like); Late (≥5d) = Lethal MDR flora
  • VAP CULTURE THRESHOLDS: BAL = 10⁴, PSB = 10³, ETA = 10⁵
  • VAP BUNDLE: Head Of Bed 30–45°, Sedation hold + SBT, Subglottic Suction, CHX oral care, Cuff Pressure

...continued (truncated to 200 lines for renderer)

Topic Overview

1. Learning Objectives
2. Definitions & Epidemiology
3. Risk Factors for MDR Pathogens
4. Diagnostic Criteria
Clinical + Radiological (All Required for Possible HAP/VAP)
Microbiological (For Probable/Confirmed)
5. Empirical Antibiotic Therapy (Start IMMEDIATELY after cultures)
Strategy: Cover likely pathogens based on LOCAL ANTIBIOGRAM + MDR risk factors
6. Culture Interpretation & De-escalation (At 48–72 Hours)
De-escalation Principles
7. Treatment Duration
8. VAP Prevention Bundle (Institute for Healthcare Improvement)
9. Pathogen-Specific Targeted Therapy
10. Special Situations
11. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points
12. Common Viva Questions
13. Common Confusions / Exam Traps
14. Mnemonics

topics.title: Infection In Immunocompromised Host Transplant Biologics
topics.html:
Related: [[Febrile Neutropenia]], [[Invasive Candidiasis]], [[Mucormycosis]], [[Pneumocystis Pneumonia]], [[CMV Disease]], [[TB in HIV]], [[Healthcare-Associated Infections (CLABSI, CAUTI, SSI, VAP)]], [[Antimicrobial Prescribing Principles]]

[!important]
Immunocompromised host = defective innate/adaptive immunity → ↑ risk, atypical presentation, opportunistic pathogens. Risk stratification by deficit: neutropenia, T-cell deficiency, B-cell/humoral, complement, phagocyte, barrier. Transplant timeline: 0–1m (surgical, hospital flora), 1–6m (opportunistic: CMV, PCP, fungi), >6m (community + chronic rejection). Biologics: anti-TNF → TB, intracellular; anti-CD20 → HBV reactivation, PML; IL-17/23 → Candida, TB.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Classify immunodeficiency by immune deficit and risk profile
  • Apply transplant timeline for infection risk (0–1m, 1–6m, >6m)
  • Recognise biologic-specific infection risks (anti-TNF, anti-CD20, integrin inhibitors, JAK inhibitors)
  • Select appropriate prophylaxis (PCP, fungal, viral, TB)
  • Diagnose and manage key opportunistic infections (CMV, PCP, EBV-PTLD, BK virus, HHV-6)
  • Apply vaccination principles in immunocompromised (timing, live vs inactivated)

2. Classification by Immune Deficit

DeficitCausesTypical Infections
NeutropeniaChemo, HSCT, drugs (ganciclovir, valproate), aplastic anaemiaBacteria (Gram-neg, Staph), Candida, Aspergillus, Gram-neg sepsis
T-cell DeficiencyHIV, transplant (CNI, mTOR, steroids), biologics (anti-TNF, alemtuzumab), chemoViruses (CMV, EBV, BK, HHV-6, VZV), TB, fungi (Pneumocystis, Cryptococcus), Listeria, Nocardia, Toxoplasma
B-cell / HumoralAnti-CD20 (rituximab), BTK inhibitors, CLL, myeloma, hypogammaglobulinaemiaEncapsulated bacteria (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae), Giardia, enteroviruses, chronic sinopulmonary
ComplementC1q–C9 deficiency, eculizumab, C1-INH deficiencyEncapsulated bacteria (Neisseria, S. pneumoniae), immune complex disease
PhagocyteCGD, diabetes, steroids, dialysis, BurnsCatalase-positive organisms (S. aureus, Burkholderia, Nocardia, Aspergillus)
Barrier DefectBurns, surgery, lines, drains, mucositisSkin flora (Staph, Strep), Candida, Gram-neg

3. Transplant Timeline — Infection Risk by Period

PeriodTimeframeKey RisksTypical Pathogens
Early0–1 monthSurgical, hospital-acquiredWound infection, bacteremia (CoNS, Gram-neg), UTI, C. difficile, donor-derived
Intermediate1–6 monthsOPPORTUNISTIC (peak)CMV (peak 1–4m), PCP, invasive fungi (Aspergillus, Candida), EBV-PTLD, BK virus, HHV-6, adenovirus, TB, Nocardia, Listeria
Late>6 monthsCommunity-acquired + chronic rejectionCommunity viruses, encapsulated bacteria, chronic viral (BK, CMV), chronic rejection
All periodsAny timeNet state of immunosuppressionNet state = baseline risk + current immunosuppression + exposures
[!key]
CMV = MOST IMPORTANT opportunistic infection in transplant. Peak 1–4 months. PCP prophylaxis universal 6–12 months. Antifungal prophylaxis in high-risk (lung, liver, heart, re-transplant).

4. Biologic-Specific Infection Risks

Biologic ClassTargetKey Infection RisksScreening/Monitoring
Anti-TNF (infliximab, adalimumab, etanercept, certolizumab, golimumab)TNF-αTB (reactivation), intracellular bacteria (Listeria, Salmonella, Nocardia), histoplasmosis, coccidioidomycosis, hepatitis B reactivationTB screen (IGRA/TST) BEFORE; HBV screen; monitor for infection
Anti-IL-12/23 (ustekinumab)IL-12/IL-23TB, fungal, viral (similar to anti-TNF but lower risk)TB screen before
Anti-IL-17 (secukinumab, ixekizumab)IL-17ACandida (mucocutaneous), TB (rare)Monitor for Candida
Anti-IL-23 (guselkumab, risankizumab)IL-23TB, fungal (lower risk than anti-TNF)TB screen before
Anti-IL-6 (tocilizumab, sarilumab)IL-6RBacterial, TB, diverticulitis, hepatotoxicityTB screen; monitor LFTs, neutrophils
Anti-CD20 (rituximab, ocrelizumab, ofatumumab)CD20 (B-cells)HBV reactivation (HBsAg+ or anti-HBc+), PML (JCV), hypogammaglobulinaemia, encapsulated bacteria, VZVHBV screen (HBsAg, anti-HBc) BEFORE; IgG levels; JCV index if natalizumab hx
Anti-CD52 (alemtuzumab)CD52 (T/B/NK)Severe T-cell depletion → CMV, VZV, Listeria, Nocardia, TB, fungal, PMLIntensive monitoring; prophylaxis (PCP, viral, fungal)
Anti-integrin (natalizumab, vedolizumab)α4β1/α4β7 integrinPML (JCV) — natalizumab; vedolizumab gut-selective = lower PML riskJCV antibody index (natalizumab); monitor
JAK Inhibitors (tofacitinib, baricitinib, upadacitinib)JAK1/2/3Herpes zoster (VZV) ↑↑, TB, bacterial, fungal, CMV, EBV, lymphopeniaVZV vaccination before; TB screen; monitor lymphocytes
BTK Inhibitors (ibrutinib, acalabrutinib)BTKBleeding, atrial fibrillation, infections (sinopulmonary, fungal), PML riskMonitor IgG, infections
BCL-2 Inhibitor (venetoclax)BCL-2TLS, neutropenia, infectionsTLS prophylaxis; monitor counts
[!tip]
TB screen (IGRA/TST) REQUIRED BEFORE starting anti-TNF, anti-IL-12/23, anti-IL-17, anti-IL-23, JAK inhibitors, alemtuzumab. HBV screen (HBsAg, anti-HBc) REQUIRED BEFORE anti-CD20, anti-TNF, JAK inhibitors.

5. Key Opportunistic Infections in Transplant

CMV Disease

AspectDetails
RiskD+/R− (highest), lung/heart/intestinal transplant, lymphocyte-depleting induction
SyndromesViraemia, syndrome (fever, leucopenia, hepatitis), tissue-invasive (pneumonitis, colitis, retinitis, encephalitis)
DiagnosisQuantitative PCR (blood) — viral load guides pre-emptive therapy
Prophylaxis vs Pre-emptiveHigh-risk: prophylaxis (valganciclovir 900mg OD ×3–6m); Standard-risk: pre-emptive (weekly PCR ×3–6m)
TreatmentValganciclovir 900mg BD (oral) OR Ganciclovir 5mg/kg IV 12h ×2–3w → maintenance; Foscarnet/Cidofovir if resistant (UL97/UL54 mut)
ResistanceUL97 mutation = ganciclovir/valganciclovir resistance; UL54 = cidofovir/foscarnet resistance

PCP (Pneumocystis jirovecii Pneumonia)

AspectDetails
RiskAll transplant (T-cell suppression); HSCT; biologics (anti-TNF, alemtuzumab); HIV CD4<200
ProphylaxisTMP-SMX 960mg PO daily (or 3×/wk); alternatives: dapsone, atovaquone, inhaled pentamidine
Duration≥6–12 months post-transplant; lifelong if ongoing immunosuppression
TreatmentHigh-dose TMP-SMX 15–20mg/kg TMP IV/PO q6h ×21d + steroids if hypoxic

EBV-PTLD (Post-Transplant Lymphoproliferative Disorder)

AspectDetails
RiskEBV D+/R−, intense immunosuppression (OKT3, ATG), paediatric, intestinal/lung transplant
SpectrumEarly lesions → polymorphic PTLD → monomorphic PTLD (lymphoma)
DiagnosisEBV PCR (blood) — rising viral load; biopsy for histology
TreatmentReduce immunosuppression (RI) = cornerstone; Rituximab (anti-CD20); EBV-CTL; chemo for monomorphic

BK Virus Nephropathy

AspectDetails
RiskKidney transplant; immunosuppression (Tacrolimus, MMF); ureteric stent
DiagnosisQuantitative PCR (blood, urine) — decoy cells in urine; biopsy (SV40 staining)
TreatmentReduce immunosuppression (reduce/stop Tac/MMF); Leflunomide, Cidofovir, IVIG (adjunctive)

6. Fungal Infections in Immunocompromised

FungusHigh-Risk HostProphylaxisTreatment
AspergillusLung transplant, HSCT, prolonged neutropenia, steroidsPosaconazole (HSCT, AML), voriconazole (lung)Voriconazole 1st-line; isavuconazole; L-AmB
CandidaICU, abdominal surgery, neutropenia, TPN, broad antibioticsFluconazole (selective), echinocandin (high-risk)Echinocandin 1st-line; step-down fluconazole
MucormycosisDKA, neutropenia, deferoxamine, steroids, transplantNone standardL-AmB + urgent surgery
PneumocystisAll transplant, HIV CD4<200, biologicsTMP-SMX universalHigh-dose TMP-SMX + steroids
CryptococcusHIV CD4<100, transplant, steroidsFluconazole (targeted)L-AmB + flucytosine → fluconazole

7. Prophylaxis Protocols — Transplant (Example: Kidney)

PathogenAgentDurationHigh-Risk Indications
PCPTMP-SMX 960mg 3×/wk6–12 monthsAll (lifelong if ongoing immunosuppression)
CMVValganciclovir 900mg OD3–6 months (prophylaxis) OR pre-emptiveD+/R−, lung/heart, lymphocyte depletion
FungalFluconazole 200mg OD / Posaconazole1–3 monthsLung, liver, heart, re-transplant, prolonged ICU
VZVAcyclovir 400mg BD3–6 monthsSeropositive recipients
HBVEntecavir/TDFIndefiniteHBsAg+ recipients
UTITMP-SMX / Nitrofurantoin3–6 monthsRecurrent UTI, structural abnormality

8. Vaccination in Immunocompromised

VaccineTypeTimingNotes
Inactivated (Flu, Pneumococcal, Hep A/B, HPV, Tdap, COVID, Rabies, IPV, Hib, MenACWY)InactivatedAnytime; ideally ≥2 weeks BEFORE immunosuppressionMay have reduced response; give extra doses per schedule
Live (MMR, Varicella, Zoster live, Yellow Fever, BCG, Oral Typhoid, Rotavirus, LAIV)Live≥4 weeks BEFORE immunosuppression; CONTRAINDICATED duringExcept: Zoster recombinant (Shingrix) = safe during; MMR/Varicella if CD4>200 in HIV
Zoster Recombinant (Shingrix)Recombinant (non-live)Safe during immunosuppressionPreferred for immunocompromised ≥18y
Post-TransplantInactivated: 3–6m post; Live: ≥2 years post (if off immunosuppression)Check local guidelines
[!warning]
Live vaccines CONTRAINDICATED during immunosuppression. Exception: Shingrix (recombinant) safe. HIV CD4>200: MMR/Varicella may be given.

9. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Transplant timeline: 0–1m (surgical/hospital), 1–6m (opportunistic: CMV, PCP, fungi, EBV-PTLD), >6m (community)
  • CMV = most important opportunistic infection; D+/R− highest risk; prophylaxis vs pre-emptive; valganciclovir/ganciclovir
  • PCP prophylaxis: TMP-SMX universal 6–12m post-transplant; lifelong if ongoing immunosuppression
  • Anti-TNF: TB reactivation (screen IGRA/TST BEFORE); histoplasma, coccidioides, HBV reactivation
  • Anti-CD20 (rituximab): HBV reactivation (screen HBsAg/anti-HBc BEFORE); PML risk; hypogammaglobulinaemia
  • JAK inhibitors: VZV (shingles) ↑↑; TB; vaccinate VZV before; screen TB
  • Alemtuzumab: severe T-cell depletion; intense prophylaxis (PCP, viral, fungal)
  • PCP prophylaxis: TMP-SMX 6–12m post-transplant; lifelong if ongoing immunosuppression
  • Fungal prophylaxis: posaconazole/voriconazole in high-risk (lung, liver, heart, HSCT)
  • Vaccination: live vaccines contraindicated during immunosuppression; Shingrix safe; give inactivated ≥2w before

10. Common Viva Questions

  1. Transplant infection timeline? 0–1m: surgical/hospital; 1–6m: opportunistic (CMV, PCP, fungi, EBV); >6m: community.
  2. CMV prophylaxis vs pre-emptive? High-risk (D+/R−, lung/heart, lymphocyte depletion) = prophylaxis 3–6m; standard-risk = pre-emptive PCR-guided.
  3. Anti-TNF screening? TB (IGRA/TST), HBV BEFORE starting.
  4. Anti-CD20 (rituximab) screening? HBV (HBsAg, anti-HBc) BEFORE; monitor IgG, infections.
  5. JAK inhibitor infection risk? VZV (shingles) ↑↑, TB, bacterial, fungal; vaccinate VZV before; monitor lymphocytes.
  6. PCP prophylaxis post-transplant? TMP-SMX 960mg 3×/wk ×6–12m; lifelong if ongoing immunosuppression.
  7. CMV treatment? Valganciclovir 900mg BD or ganciclovir 5mg/kg IV 12h; foscarnet/cidofovir if resistant.
  8. BK virus nephropathy management? Reduce immunosuppression (Tac/MMF); leflunomide/cidofovir/IVIG adjunct.
  9. Anti-CD20 HBV reactivation? Screen HBsAg/anti-HBc BEFORE; prophylactic antivirals if HBsAg+ or anti-HBc+.
  10. JAK inhibitor VZV risk? Shingles ↑↑; vaccinate with Shingrix BEFORE starting.
  11. Alemtuzumab prophylaxis? PCP (TMP-SMX), viral (acyclovir/valganciclovir), fungal (posaconazole) — intensive.

11. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
CMV prophylaxis for all transplantOnly high-risk (D+/R−, lung/heart, lymphocyte depletion); standard-risk = pre-emptive
PCP prophylaxis only 6 monthsContinue lifelong if ongoing immunosuppression
Anti-TNF safe if TB screen negativeScreen IGRA/TST BEFORE; but can still reactivate latent TB (false neg) — educate patient
Rituximab only affects B-cellsAlso causes hypogammaglobulinaemia → encapsulated bacteria; PML risk (JCV)
JAK inhibitors = just oral DMARDsSignificant VZV ↑↑, TB, infection risk; vaccinate Shingrix BEFORE
Live vaccines always contraindicatedShingrix (recombinant) = SAFE during immunosuppression
CMV treatment = valganciclovir onlyResistance common (UL97/UL54); foscarnet/cidofovir for resistance
PCP only in HIVTransplant, biologics, steroids, malignancy all at risk
Fungal prophylaxis for all transplantOnly high-risk (lung, liver, heart, re-tx, prolonged neutropenia, steroids)

12. Mnemonics

  • TRANSPLANT TIMELINE: 0-1m = Surgical/Hospital; 1-6m = Opportunistic (CMV, PCP, Fungi, EBV); >6m = Community
  • CMV RISK: Donor+/Recipient-Neg = Highest; Lung/Heart/Intestinal Tx; Lymphocyte depletion
  • ANTI-TNF SCREEN: TB (IGRA/TST) + HBV BEFORE
  • RITUXIMAB SCREEN: HBV (HBsAg, Anti-HBc) BEFORE
  • JAK INHIBITORS: VZV ↑↑, TB, Lymphopenia → Shingrix BEFORE
  • PCP PROPHYLAXIS: TMP-SMX 6-12m post-tx; Lifelong if Immunosuppressed
  • LIVE VACCINES: Contraindicated During Immunosuppression; Shingrix = Safe
  • ALEMTUZUMAB: Severe T-cell depletion → Intense Prophylaxis (PCP, Viral, Fungal)

13. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Immunocompromised Host))
    Transplant Timeline
      0-1m: Surgical, Hospital
      1-6m: Opportunistic (CMV, PCP, Fungi, EBV-PTLD, BK)
      >6m: Community + Chronic rejection
    Biologics
      Anti-TNF: TB, Histo, Cocci, HBV
      Anti-CD20: HBV reactivation, PML, Hypogammaglobulinaemia
      JAK Inhibitors: VZV↑↑, TB, Lymphopenia
      Alemtuzumab: Severe T-cell depletion
      Anti-integrin: PML (natalizumab)
    Key OIs
      CMV: D+/R- highest; Prophylaxis vs Pre-emptive; Valganciclovir
      PCP: TMP-SMX 6-12m universal
      EBV-PTLD: Reduce IS + Rituximab
      BK Virus: Reduce IS
      Fungal: Aspergillus (Vorico), Candida (Echinocandin), Mucor (L-AmB+Surg)

*...continued (truncated to 200 lines for renderer)*

topics.title: Infection In Pregnancy Neonates
topics.html:
[!info] Davidson Ch 11 Alignment: Infectious Disease → Infection in Special Hosts → Pregnancy & Neonates
FCPS/MRCP Focus: TORCH infections, syphilis in pregnancy, HIV/hepatitis vertical transmission, GBS prevention, neonatal sepsis, congenital infections, breastfeeding with infections

1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Apply TORCH Screen: Toxoplasmosis, Other (Syphilis, Varicella, Parvovirus B19, Zika), Rubella, CMV, Herpes Simplex
  • [ ] Manage Syphilis in Pregnancy: Penicillin, Partner Treatment, Neonatal Evaluation
  • [ ] Manage HIV in Pregnancy: ART, Viral Load, Delivery Mode, Infant Prophylaxis, Breastfeeding
  • [ ] Manage Hepatitis B/C in Pregnancy: Vertical Transmission Prevention, HBIG + Vaccine, Tenofovir, Breastfeeding
  • [ ] Prevent GBS Neonatal Sepsis: Screening 36-37w, IAP Penicillin, Penicillin Allergy
  • [ ] Manage Neonatal Sepsis: Early vs Late Onset, Empirical Antibiotics, Duration
  • [ ] Manage Congenital Infections: CMV, HSV, Rubella, Syphilis, Toxoplasmosis, Zika, Parvovirus B19
  • [ ] Advise Breastfeeding with Maternal Infections (HIV, HBV, HCV, CMV, HSV, TB)

2. 📖 TORCH Infections — Congenital Infections Overview

PathogenTransmissionClassic Triad / Key FeaturesDiagnosisTreatment
ToxoplasmaTransplacentalChorioretinitis, Hydrocephalus, Intracranial CalcificationsIgM/IgG + Avidity, PCR (Amniotic Fluid)Spiramycin (<18w) → Pyrimethamine/Sulfadiazine/Folinic Acid (>18w)
Syphilis (Treponema pallidum)Transplacental (Any Trimester)Hepatosplenomegaly, Rash, Snuffles, Bone Lesions, PseudoparalysisVDRL/RPR (Non-treponemal), TPPA/FTA-ABS (Treponemal)Penicillin G (Penicillin Allergy → Desensitisation)
RubellaTransplacentalCataracts, Deafness, PDA, IUGR, "Blueberry Muffin" RashIgM/IgG, PCRSupportive; Prevention = MMR Vaccine Pre-Pregnancy
CMVMost Common Congenital InfectionSensorineural Hearing Loss, Microcephaly, Chorioretinitis, IUGR, Petechiae, HepatosplenomegalyUrine/Saliva PCR (Gold Standard), IgM, CultureValganciclovir 6 Months (Symptomatic)
HSV (Neonatal)Perinatal (85%), Transplacental (5%), Postnatal (10%)SEM (Skin/Eye/Mouth), CNS, DisseminatedPCR (CSF/Blood/Surface), CultureAcyclovir 20mg/kg IV Q8H × 14-21d (CNS: 21d)
ZikaTransplacentalMicrocephaly, Brain Calcifications, Arthrogryposis, OcularRT-PCR (Urine/Serum/Amniotic Fluid), IgMSupportive; Prevention = Mosquito Avoidance
SyphilisTransplacentalClassic Triad: Hutchinson's Teeth, Interstitial Keratitis, 8th Nerve DeafnessVDRL/RPR + TPPA, CSF VDRLPenicillin G (Aqueous Crystalline IV)
Rubella1st Trimester Highest RiskCataracts, Deafness, PDA, "Blueberry Muffin" RashIgM, PCRSupportive
Parvovirus B19TransplacentalHydrops Fetalis (Severe Anaemia), MyocarditisIgM, PCR (Maternal/Fetal Blood)IVIG, Intrauterine Transfusion
VZVVaricella (Chickenpox)Congenital Varicella Syndrome (Limb Hypoplasia, Skin Scarring, Eye/CNS)IgM/IgG, PCRAcyclovir (Severe/Neonate)
ZikaMosquito (Aedes), Sexual, TransplacentalMicrocephaly, Brain Calcifications, ArthrogryposisRT-PCR (Urine/Serum/Amniotic Fluid)Supportive; Prevention
[!tip] TORCH = Toxoplasmosis, Other (Syphilis, Varicella, Parvovirus, Zika), Rubella, CMV, Herpes Simplex. CMV = Most Common Congenital Infection.

3. 📖 Maternal Infections in Pregnancy — Key Management

Syphilis in Pregnancy

StageTreatment
Primary, Secondary, Early Latent (<1 Year)Benzathine Penicillin G 2.4MU IM Single Dose
Late Latent (>1 Year) / TertiaryBenzathine Penicillin G 2.4MU IM Weekly × 3 Doses
Penicillin AllergyDesensitisation → Penicillin (Doxycycline/Azithromycin NOT Effective for Congenital Prevention)
Neonatal AssessmentPhysical Exam, VDRL/RPR, CSF (If Mother Untreated/Inadequate Rx/Late Rx/Clinical Signs), Long Bone X-Ray
[!warning] Penicillin = Only Reliable Prevention of Congenital Syphilis. Doxycycline/Azithromycin Do NOT Prevent Congenital Syphilis. Penicillin Allergy → Desensitisation.

HIV in Pregnancy

AspectManagement
ART GoalViral Suppression (VL <50 copies/mL) by Delivery
ART RegimenDolutegravir + TDF/FTC (Preferred); Efavirenz (Avoid 1st Trimester Historically, Now OK)
Viral Load MonitoringBooking, 36wks, Delivery
DeliveryViral Load <50 → Vaginal OK; Viral Load 50-399 → Elective C-Section 38wks; Viral Load >400 → Emergency C-Section
Neonatal ProphylaxisZidovudine 4mg/kg BD × 4 Weeks (If Maternal VL <50) / Zidovudine + Nevirapine + Lamivudine (High Risk)
BreastfeedingContraindicated in High-Income Settings (Formula Feed); Low-Income: Exclusive BF + Maternal ART + Infant Prophylaxis

Hepatitis B in Pregnancy

ScenarioManagement
HBsAg+ MotherHBIG 100IU IM + HBV Vaccine 10µg IM to Infant Within 12h of Birth
High Infectivity (HBeAg+, VL >200,000 IU/mL)Tenofovir 300mg OD From 28-32 Weeks → Stop at Delivery/Postpartum
BreastfeedingSafe (If Infant Received HBIG + Vaccine)
Infant ScheduleHBIG + Vaccine at Birth → Vaccine at 1, 6 Months

Hepatitis C in Pregnancy

AspectManagement
Vertical Transmission~5-6% (Higher if HIV Co-infection, High VL)
TreatmentDAA Deferred Until Postpartum (Safety Data Limited in Pregnancy)
BreastfeedingSafe (If Nipples Not Cracked/Bleeding)
Infant TestingHCV RNA at 2-6 Months or Anti-HCV at 18 Months

GBS (Group B Streptococcus) Prevention

StrategyDetails
ScreeningRectovaginal Swab at 36-37 Weeks (Culture or PCR)
IAP IndicationGBS Positive, Previous GBS Neonate, Bacteriuria in Pregnancy, Unknown Status + Preterm Labour/Rupture/≥18h Rupture/Fever
IAP RegimenPenicillin G 5MU IV Load → 2.5-3MU Q4H (Until Delivery)
Penicillin AllergyCefazolin 2g IV Load → 1g Q8H (If No Anaphylaxis); Clindamycin/Vancomycin (If High Risk Anaphylaxis)
DurationUntil Delivery (≥4h Pre-Delivery Optimal)

4. 👶 Neonatal Sepsis

Classification

TypeTimingCommon Pathogens
Early-Onset (EOS)<72h (≤3d) of LifeGBS, E. coli, Listeria, Klebsiella, Enterococcus
Late-Onset (LOS)>72h (>3d) of LifeCoNS, S. aureus, Enterobacterales, Candida, Enterococcus

Clinical Features

FeatureEOSLOS
Onset<72h>72h
Risk FactorsGBS+, PROM >18h, Maternal Fever, PretermPreterm, Invasive Devices, Prolonged Hospitalisation, Antibiotics
PresentationRespiratory Distress, Apnoea, Temperature Instability, Lethargy, Poor FeedingSimilar + Abdominal Distension, Feed Intolerance

Empirical Therapy (Neonatal Sepsis)

ScenarioEmpirical RegimenDuration
EOS (Term)Ampicillin 50mg/kg Q12H + Gentamicin 4-5mg/kg OD48-72h (If Cultures Negative, Stop) / 7-10 Days (If Positive)
EOS (Preterm <37w)Ampicillin 50mg/kg Q12H + Gentamicin 4-5mg/kg ODAs Above
LOSVancomycin + Meropenem/Ceftazidime (Local Resistance)7-14 Days (If Positive)
Suspected MeningitisAdd Cefotaxime (or Ceftriaxone if >3d)14-21 Days
[!warning] Ampicillin + Gentamicin = Standard EOS Regimen. Ceftriaxone in Neonates >3d (Risk Biliary Sludge). Ceftriaxone Contraindicated <28d Corrected Gestation (Kernicterus Risk).

5. 📖 Congenital Infections — Specific Management

Congenital CMV (cCMV)

AspectDetails
EpidemiologyMost Common Congenital Infection (~0.5-1% Live Births); Symptomatic ~10-15%
TransmissionPrimary Maternal Infection (30-40% Transmission); Reactivation/Reinfection (<2%)
ClinicalAsymptomatic (85-90%) vs Symptomatic (10-15%): Petechiae, Hepatosplenomegaly, Jaundice, Microcephaly, Sensorineural Hearing Loss (Most Common Sequela), Chorioretinitis, Neurodevelopmental Delay
DiagnosisUrine/Saliva PCR (Gold Standard, <21 Days), CMV IgM, Culture
TreatmentSymptomatic: Valganciclovir 16mg/kg BD PO × 6 Months (Improves Hearing/Neurodevelopment)
Follow-UpAudiology (Every 6mo × 3yr), Developmental Assessment, Ophthalmology

Neonatal HSV

TypeFeaturesTreatment
SEM (Skin/Eye/Mouth)Vesicles (45%), Conjunctivitis, Oral LesionsAcyclovir 20mg/kg IV Q8H × 14 Days
CNSSeizures, Lethargy, CSF PleocytosisAcyclovir 20mg/kg IV Q8H × 21 Days
DisseminatedMulti-Organ (Liver, Lung, CNS, DIC)Acyclovir 20mg/kg IV Q8H × 21 Days
[!warning] Neonatal HSV = Acyclovir 20mg/kg IV Q8H; CNS/Disseminated = 21 Days, SEM = 14 Days. CSF PCR = Gold Standard.

Congenital Syphilis

ScenarioManagement
Maternal Treatment AdequateBaby: Benzathine Penicillin G 50,000U/kg IM Single Dose (If Asymptomatic, Normal Exam, Normal CSF/Long Bones)
Maternal Treatment Inadequate/UnknownBaby: Aqueous Penicillin G 50,000U/kg IV Q12H × 10-14 Days
CSF Abnormal / Clinical SignsAqueous Penicillin G 50,000U/kg IV Q12H × 10-14 Days

6. 🤱 Breastfeeding with Maternal Infections

InfectionBreastfeeding Recommendation
HIVContraindicated (High-Income); Low-Income: Exclusive BF + Maternal ART + Infant Prophylaxis
HBVSafe (After HBIG + Vaccine to Infant)
HCVSafe (If Nipples Intact)
CMVSafe (If Term Infant); Preterm <32w/1500g: Pasteurised Donor Milk / Freeze-Thaw
HSVSafe (If No Breast Lesions); Avoid if Active Lesion on Breast
TBSafe After 2 Weeks Effective Therapy; Separate Mother/Baby Initially
MalariaSafe (If Mother Treated)
ZikaSafe (No Live Virus in Breast Milk)

7. 💡 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
TORCHToxoplasmosis, Other (Syphilis, Varicella, Parvovirus, Zika), Rubella, CMV, Herpes Simplex
Congenital CMVMost Common Congenital Infection; Urinary PCR Gold Standard; Valganciclovir 6mo if Symptomatic
Congenital SyphilisPenicillin G Only Effective Prevention; Benzathine Penicillin G 2.4MU IM (Maternal); Aqueous Penicillin G IV (Neonate)
HIV in PregnancyART Goal: VL<50 by Delivery; Zidovudine Neonatal Prophylaxis 4wks; C-Section if VL>400
Hepatitis BHBIG + Vaccine Within 12h Birth; Tenofovir 28-32wks if VL>200,000
Hepatitis C~5-6% Vertical Transmission; DAA Postpartum; Breastfeeding Safe
GBS PreventionScreen 36-37w; IAP: Penicillin G 5MU Load → 2.5-3MU Q4H
Neonatal SepsisEOS <72h: Amp-Gent; LOS: Vanco + Mero/Ceftazidime; Meningitis: Add Cefotaxime
Congenital CMVUrine PCR Gold Standard; Valganciclovir 6mo if Symptomatic
Neonatal HSVAcyclovir 20mg/kg IV Q8H; CNS/Disseminated 21d, SEM 14d
BreastfeedingHIV: Contraindicated (High-Income); HBV/HCV/CMV: Safe (With Conditions)

8. ❓ Viva Questions

  1. What is the most common congenital infection?
  • Cytomegalovirus (CMV).
  1. What is the treatment for congenital syphilis in the neonate?
  • Aqueous Penicillin G 50,000U/kg IV Q12H × 10-14 Days (If Maternal Treatment Inadequate/CSF Abnormal).
  1. How do you prevent mother-to-child transmission of HIV?
  • ART to Achieve VL<50 by Delivery, Elective C-Section if VL>400, Neonatal Zidovudine 4mg/kg BD × 4 Weeks, Avoid Breastfeeding (High-Income).
  1. How do you prevent GBS neonatal sepsis?
  • Screen at 36-37 Weeks (Rectovaginal Swab) → Penicillin G 5MU IV Load → 2.5-3MU Q4H Until Delivery if Positive.
  1. What is the management of a neonate born to HBsAg+ mother?
  • HBIG 100IU IM + HBV Vaccine 10µg IM Within 12 Hours of Birth; Complete Vaccine Series.
  1. What is the treatment for neonatal HSV infection?
  • Acyclovir 20mg/kg IV Q8H; 14 Days (SEM), 21 Days (CNS/Disseminated).
  1. What is the risk of vertical transmission of Hepatitis C?
  • ~5-6% (Higher if HIV Co-infection or High Viral Load).
  1. How do you diagnose congenital CMV?
  • Urine or Saliva PCR (Gold Standard) Within 21 Days of Life; Culture (Gold Standard); IgM Less Reliable.
  1. What is the management of congenital toxoplasmosis?
  • Pyrimethamine + Sulfadiazine + Folinic Acid × 12 Months.
  1. When do you give HBIG + Hepatitis B vaccine to a neonate?
  • Within 12 Hours of Birth (HBsAg+ Mother).
  1. What is the empirical antibiotic regimen for early-onset neonatal sepsis?
  • Ampicillin 50mg/kg Q12H + Gentamicin 4-5mg/kg OD (Term) — Adjust for Preterm.
  1. How do you prevent mother-to-child transmission of Hepatitis B?
  • HBIG + Vaccine at Birth → Vaccine at 1 & 6 Months; Tenofovir 300mg OD from 28-32 Weeks if Viral Load >200,000 IU/mL.
  1. What is the treatment for congenital toxoplasmosis?
  • Pyrimethamine + Sulfadiazine + Folinic Acid × 12 Months.

9. 🧠 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
TORCH ComponentsT=Toxo, O=Other (Syph, VZV, Parvo, Zika), R=Rubella, C=CMV, H=HSV
CMV vs RubellaCMV = Most Common, Hearing Loss; Rubella = Cataracts, Deafness, PDA
Congenital Syphilis RxPenicillin ONLY; Doxy/Azithro NOT Effective for Congenital Prevention
HIV BreastfeedingHigh-Income: Formula Only; Low-Income: Exclusive BF + ART + Infant Prophylaxis
CMV vs RubellaCMV: Hearing Loss, Microcephaly, Petechiae; Rubella: Cataracts, Deafness, PDA
CMV vs HSV NeonatalCMV: Hearing Loss, Microcephaly; HSV: Vesicles, CNS/Disseminated, Acyclovir
MnemonicMeaning
"TORCH = Toxo, Other, Rubella, CMV, Herpes"Congenital Infections
"CMV = Most Common Congenital Infection"CMV Prevalence
"Syphilis = Penicillin Only"Syphilis Treatment
"HIV = ART + Zidovudine Baby + C-Section if VL>400"HIV Pregnancy
"GBS = Screen 36-37w, Penicillin G IAP"GBS Prevention
"CMV = Urine PCR Gold Standard"CMV Diagnosis
"HSV Neonate = Acyclovir 20mg/kg IV Q8H"Neonatal HSV
"CMV = Valganciclovir 6mo if Symptomatic"cCMV Treatment

10. 🗺️ Mind Map

mindmap

      Zika: Microcephaly, Prevention

      VZV: Congenital Varicella Syndrome
    Maternal Infections
      Syphilis: Penicillin Only, Benzathine 2.4MU IM
      HIV: ART + VL<50, Zidovudine Baby 4wks, C-Section if VL>400
      HBV: HBIG + Vaccine 12h, Tenofovir 28-32w if VL>200k

      LOS >72h: Vanco + Mero/Ceftaz
      Meningitis: Add Cefotaxime
    Congenital Infections

      HSV: Acyclovir 20mg/kg Q8H (14d SEM, 21d CNS)
      Syphilis: Aqueous Penicillin G IV
      Zika: Microcephaly, Supportive
      Parvovirus: Hydrops, IVIG + IUT
    Breastfeeding
      HIV: No (High-Income), Yes+ART (Low-Income)
      HBV/HCV/CMV/HSV: Safe (Conditions)

11. 📋 One-Page Revision Card

INFECTION IN PREGNANCY & NEONATES – FCPS/MRCP REVISION CARD
TORCH: Toxo, Other (Syph/VZV/Parvo/Zika), Rubella, CMV, HSV
CMV: Most Common Congenital, Urine PCR Gold, Valganciclovir 6mo if Symptomatic
Syphilis: Penicillin ONLY, Benzathine 2.4MU IM Maternal, Neonatal Aqueous Penicillin G IV
HIV: ART→VL<50, Zidovudine Baby 4wk, C-Section if VL>400, No BF (High-Income)
HBV: HBIG+Vaccine 12h, Tenofovir 28-32w if VL>200k
HCV: 5-6% Transmission, DAA Postpartum, BF Safe
GBS: Screen 36-37w, Penicillin G 5MU Load→2.5-3MU Q4H
Neonatal Sepsis: EOS<72h: Amp+Gent; LOS>72h: Vanco+Mero/Ceftaz; Meningitis: Add Cefotaxime
Congenital CMV: Urine PCR<21d, Valganciclovir 6mo if Symptomatic
Neonatal HSV: Acyclovir 20mg/kg Q8H (14d SEM, 21d CNS/Dissem)
Congenital Syphilis: Penicillin G Aqueous IV 50,000U/kg Q12H×10-14d
Breastfeeding: HIV No (High-Income), Yes+ART (Low-Income); HBV/HCV/CMV/HSV Safe

13. 🎯 Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

LevelContent
Must KnowTORCH components, CMV most common congenital infection, Syphilis penicillin only, HIV vertical transmission prevention (ART, C-section, zidovudine), GBS screening & IAP, HBV vertical transmission prevention (HBIG+vaccine), Neonatal sepsis EOS vs LOS antibiotics, Congenital CMV diagnosis (urine PCR) & treatment (valganciclovir), Neonatal HSV acyclovir dosing, Breastfeeding guidelines by infection
Should KnowCongenital rubella syndrome (cataracts, deafness, PDA), Toxoplasmosis management in pregnancy (spiramycin vs pyrimethamine), Parvovirus B19 hydrops fetalis management, Zika congenital syndrome, Neonatal HSV types (SEM/CNS/disseminated) & treatment, Congenital syphilis staging & treatment, Congenital CMV long-term sequelae (hearing loss), Hepatitis B tenofovir in pregnancy, Hepatitis C postpartum DAA, GBS penicillin allergy management, Neonatal sepsis meningitis antibiotic choice (cefotaxime), Breastfeeding with maternal infections
Nice to KnowCongenital infection long-term neurodevelopmental outcomes, Antiviral resistance in neonates, Emerging congenital infections (Zika, novel coronaviruses), Intrauterine transfusion for parvovirus, Neonatal immunization with maternal antibiotics, Probiotic prevention of NEC/sepsis, Cost-effectiveness of screening programs, Global burden of congenital infections, Ethical issues in prenatal diagnosis
