topics.title: Infective Diarrhoea And Food Poisoning
topics.html:
Related: [[Fever and Septic Syndrome Approach]], [[Sepsis and Septic Shock]], [[Clostridioides difficile Infection]], [[Traveller's Diarrhoea]], [[Inflammatory Bowel Disease]]

[!important]
Diarrhoea = ≥3 loose stools/24h. Acute <14d, Persistent 14–30d, Chronic >30d. Infective = majority acute. Assess hydration, red flags, need for stool culture/PCR, empirical therapy.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Classify diarrhoea by duration and mechanism
  • Identify red flags for invasive/bloody diarrhoea and systemic illness
  • Select appropriate investigations (stool culture, PCR, toxins, endoscopy)
  • Apply empirical and pathogen-directed therapy
  • Manage dehydration, electrolytes, and complications

2. Classification by Duration

CategoryDurationCommon Causes
Acute<14 daysViral (norovirus, rotavirus), bacterial (Campylobacter, Salmonella, Shigella, ETEC), protozoal (Giardia, Cryptosporidium)
Persistent14–30 daysGiardia, Cryptosporidium, Cyclospora, C. difficile, post-infectious IBS
Chronic>30 daysIBD, IBS, malabsorption, chronic infections (HIV, TB), medications, neoplasia

3. Classification by Mechanism

TypeMechanismExamples
SecretoryActive ion secretion (CFTR) → water followsCholera (CTX), ETEC (LT/ST), VIPoma, laxatives
OsmoticNon-absorbable solutes retain waterLactose intolerance, magnesium, sorbitol, malabsorption
Inflammatory/InvasiveMucosal damage → blood, pus, mucusShigella, Salmonella, Campylobacter, EIEC, C. difficile, IBD
Fatty (Steatorrhoea)Fat malabsorptionPancreatic insufficiency, coeliac, bile acid diarrhoea
Motility-relatedAltered transitHyperthyroidism, diabetic autonomic neuropathy, carcinoid

4. Clinical Assessment

History

ElementKey Questions
DurationAcute (<14d) vs persistent vs chronic
Frequency/VolumeLarge volume (secretory/osmotic) vs small frequent (inflammatory)
Blood/MucusYes = inflammatory/invasive (dysentery); No = secretory/osmotic
Severity≥6/day, nocturnal, weight loss
AssociatedFever, abdominal pain, vomiting, tenesmus
EpidemiologyTravel, antibiotics, hospitalisation, food source, contacts, immunocompromise

Red Flags (Admit / Urgent Investigation)

Red FlagSignificance
Bloody diarrhoeaInvasive (Shigella, Campylobacter, EHEC, Salmonella, EIEC, C. difficile, IBD)
High fever (>38.5°C)Systemic invasion (bacteraemia, sepsis)
Signs of dehydrationHypovolaemia, shock risk
Severe abdominal painSurgical abdomen, toxic megacolon, ischaemia
ImmunocompromiseHIV, chemo, steroids — broader differential, severe disease
Elderly / ComorbiditiesHigher mortality, atypical presentation
Recent antibiotics / hospitalisationC. difficile risk
Recent travelETEC, Giardia, Cyclospora, Cholera, parasitic
Neurological symptomsBotulism, listeria, ciguatera, shellfish poisoning

5. Investigations

When to Investigate (NICE / IDSA)

IndicationTests
Bloody/mucoid diarrhoeaStool culture + PCR, C. difficile toxin
Fever ≥38.5°CBlood cultures, stool culture/PCR
Severe / dehydratedU&E, CRP, lactate, VBG/ABG, FBC
ImmunocompromisedExtended PCR panel, O&P, viral PCR, fungal
Persistent >7dStool culture/PCR, O&P, Giardia/Crypto antigen
Recent antibiotics / hospitalC. difficile toxin/PCR
Recent travelO&P, Cyclospora, microsporidia, viral PCR
Foodborne outbreakCulture, WGS, public health notification

Stool Culture vs PCR

TestAdvantagesLimitations
Culture + ASTEssential for resistance, susceptibility, outbreakSlow (2–5d); lower sensitivity
Multiplex PCR panelRapid (1–2h), high sensitivity, multiple targetsNo AST; detects DNA not viability; cost
Ova & Parasites (O&P)Required for Giardia, Crypto, Cyclospora, helminthsLow sensitivity (3 samples); operator-dependent
C. difficile toxin/PCRGold standard for CDIPCR more sensitive; toxin more specific

Pathogen-Specific Tests

PathogenBest Test
C. difficilePCR (toxin B gene) +/− toxin EIA
Giardia / CryptoStool antigen (EIA) or PCR
Cyclospora / MicrosporidiaModified acid-fast stain / PCR
Vibrio choleraeCulture (TCBS) + PCR
EHEC (O157:H7)Culture (sorbitol-MacConkey) + PCR (stx1/stx2)
VDRL / RPRNot for diarrhoea! (syphilis)

6. Empirical Management Principles

Rehydration (Cornerstone)

SeverityORS / IV Fluid
No dehydrationORS ad lib (50–100mL/kg/24h); continue feeding
Some dehydrationORS 75mL/kg over 4h; reassess
Severe dehydrationIV Ringer's Lactate / Normal Saline 100mL/kg over 3h (adults: 1L bolus, reassess)
ORS = WHO reduced osmolarity (245 mOsm/L); Zinc 20mg/day × 10–14d in children <5y

Antimotility Agents

AgentUseContraindications
LoperamideAcute watery (no blood/fever); adjunct to antibioticsBloody diarrhoea, fever, suspected C. difficile, children <12y
RacecadotrilSecretory diarrhoea (adjunct)Similar to loperamide

Antibiotic Therapy (Indicated for Specific Pathogens / Severity)

Pathogen / Scenario1st-lineDuration
ShigellaCiprofloxacin 500mg BD / Azithromycin 500mg OD3–5d
CampylobacterAzithromycin 500mg OD3d
Salmonella (non-typhoidal, invasive)Ciprofloxacin 500mg BD / Ceftriaxone 2g IV7–10d (IV→oral)
Salmonella Typhi/ParatyphiCeftriaxone 2g IV / Azithromycin 1g OD7–14d
ETEC (traveller's diarrhoea)Azithromycin 500mg OD × 1–3d / Rifaximin 200mg TDS × 3d1–3d
Vibrio choleraeDoxycycline 100mg BD × 3d / Azithromycin 1g single3d / 1d
C. difficile (mild-mod)Vancomycin 125mg QDS PO / Fidaxomicin 200mg BD10d
C. difficile (severe)Vancomycin 500mg QDS PO/IV + Metronidazole 500mg IV QDS10–14d
GiardiaMetronidazole 400mg TDS / Tinidazole 2g single5–7d / 1d
CryptosporidiumNitazoxanide 500mg BD (if immunocompetent)3d
EHEC O157:H7No antibiotics (↑ HUS risk)Supportive only
Food poisoning (S. aureus, B. cereus)Supportive (toxin-mediated)—
Avoid antibiotics in: EHEC (↑ HUS), mild viral, self-limiting

7. Food Poisoning (Toxin-Mediated)

SyndromeOrganism / ToxinIncubationKey Features
StaphylococcalS. aureus enterotoxin1–6hVomiting prominent, abdominal cramps, diarrhoea; self-limiting 24–48h
Bacillus cereus (emetic)Cereulide1–6hVomiting > diarrhoea; fried rice
Bacillus cereus (diarrhoeal)Enterotoxin8–16hDiarrhoea > vomiting; meat, vegetables
Clostridium perfringensEnterotoxin8–24hDiarrhoea, cramps; no fever/vomiting; meat/gravy
BotulismC. botulinum neurotoxin12–72hDescending paralysis, diplopia, dysphagia, respiratory failure; ANTITOXIN urgent
CiguateraCiguatoxin2–24hGI + neurological (paradoxical temp reversal, paraesthesia)
ScombroidHistamine10–60minFlushing, headache, urticaria, palpitations; antihistamines

8. Complications

ComplicationHigh-Risk Pathogens
HUS (Haemolytic Uraemic Syndrome)EHEC O157:H7 (Shiga toxin); Shigella dysenteriae
TTPSimilar to HUS; ADAMTS13 deficiency
Reactive ArthritisSalmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, Yersinia, Chlamydia (HLA-B27)
Guillain-Barré SyndromeCampylobacter jejuni (molecular mimicry)
SepticaemiaSalmonella, Campylobacter, invasive E. coli
Toxic MegacolonC. difficile, Shigella, IBD
Post-infectious IBSCampylobacter, Salmonella, Giardia

9. Traveller's Diarrhoea

AspectRecommendation
Definition≥3 loose stools/24h + cramps/nausea/fever during/after travel
AetiologyETEC (~50%), Campylobacter, Salmonella, Shigella, norovirus, Giardia
ProphylaxisNot routine; Rifaximin 200mg BD (high-risk short-term)
Self-treatmentAzithromycin 500mg OD × 1–3d + Loperamide + ORS
Standby antibioticsAzithromycin 500mg OD × 3d (SE Asia); Rifaximin 200mg TDS (low-risk)

10. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Bloody diarrhoea = invasive → culture/PCR + empirical antibiotics
  • EHEC O157:H7 = NO antibiotics (↑ HUS risk)
  • C. difficile = recent antibiotics/hospital → PCR + vancomycin/fidaxomicin
  • Traveller's diarrhoea = ETEC most common; azithromycin standby
  • Cholera = rice-water stool, massive secretory → doxycycline/azithro + massive ORS
  • Botulism = descending paralysis → antitoxin + ventilatory support
  • Reactive arthritis = post-Salmonella/Shigella/Campylobacter (HLA-B27)
  • HUS = EHEC O157:H7 (no antibiotics!) + Shigella
  • Food poisoning = short incubation (S. aureus 1–6h) = vomiting predominant

11. Common Viva Questions

  1. When do you give antibiotics for diarrhoea?
  2. Why no antibiotics in EHEC O157:H7?
  3. How do you manage C. difficile?
  4. What is the management of traveller's diarrhoea?
  5. When do you suspect HUS?

12. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

  • Antibiotics in EHEC = ↑ HUS (NEVER give)
  • Antibiotics in mild viral = unnecessary, ↑ resistance
  • Loperamide in bloody diarrhoea = contraindicated
  • Antibiotics for all traveller's diarrhoea = only moderate-severe
  • C. difficile = stop causative antibiotic first; metronidazole no longer 1st-line
  • Cholera = rice-water stool, massive fluid loss → doxycycline + massive ORS
  • Botulism = antitoxin + ventilatory support (not antibiotics)
  • HUS triad = microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia + thrombocytopenia + AKI

13. Mnemonics

  • Bloody diarrhoea pathogens: Shigella, Salmonella, Campylobacter, EHEC, C. difficile, EIEC = SSCEE
  • No antibiotics: EHEC, Viral, Mild self-limiting = EVM
  • HUS triad: Microangiopathic Haemolytic Anaemia + Thrombocytopenia + AKI = MHA-T
  • Traveller's diarrhoea: ETEC > Campylobacter > Salmonella > Shigella = ECSS
  • Food poisoning incubation: S. aureus/B. cereus (emetic) = 1–6h = SB-6

14. One-Page Revision Summary

Diarrhoea = Acute (<14d) / Persistent (14–30d) / Chronic (>30d)

  • Bloody = invasive → culture/PCR + antibiotics
  • Watery = secretory/osmotic → rehydration ± antiemetics
  • EHEC O157:H7 = NO antibiotics (HUS risk)
  • C. difficile = recent antibiotics → vancomycin/fidaxomicin
  • Traveller's = ETEC most common; azithromycin standby
  • Cholera = rice-water, massive loss → doxy/azithro + massive ORS
  • Food poisoning = short incubation = vomiting (S. aureus/B. cereus)
  • Botulism = descending paralysis → antitoxin + ventilator
  • HUS = EHEC (NO antibiotics!)

15. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • Bloody diarrhoea = invasive → culture + antibiotics
  • EHEC = NO antibiotics (HUS risk)
  • C. difficile = vancomycin/fidaxomicin (metronidazole 2nd-line)
  • Cholera = rice-water + doxycycline + massive ORS
  • Traveller's diarrhoea = azithromycin standby
  • Botulism = antitoxin + ventilator
  • HUS = EHEC (no antibiotics!)

Should Know

  • Food poisoning syndromes (S. aureus, B. cereus, C. perfringens)
  • Parasitic (Giardia, Crypto, Cyclospora)
  • Reactive arthritis (post-infectious)
  • Paediatric dehydration assessment
  • Probiotic evidence

Nice to Know

  • FMT for recurrent C. difficile
  • New PCR panels (syndromic testing)
  • Post-infectious IBS mechanisms
  • Vaccine development (ETEC, Shigella, cholera)
  • Microbiome therapeutics

16. MCQs (10)

  1. Invasive diarrhoea (bloody/mucoid) — most common bacterial causes:

A. ETEC, Vibrio

B. Shigella, Salmonella, Campylobacter, EHEC

C. Norovirus, Rotavirus

D. Giardia, Cryptosporidium

  1. EHEC O157:H7 — antibiotic treatment:

A. Ciprofloxacin 500mg BD

B. No antibiotics (↑ HUS risk)

C. Azithromycin 500mg OD

D. Ceftriaxone 2g IV

  1. C. difficile infection — 1st-line treatment (mild-moderate):

A. Metronidazole 500mg TDS

B. Vancomycin 125mg QDS PO (or Fidaxomicin 200mg BD)

C. Ciprofloxacin

D. Doxycycline

  1. Cholera — characteristic stool:

A. Bloody, mucoid

B. Rice-water, massive volume

C. Fatty, foul-smelling

D. Small volume, frequent

  1. Traveller's diarrhoea — most common cause:

A. Norovirus

B. ETEC (enterotoxigenic E. coli)

C. Salmonella

D. Giardia

  1. Food poisoning with vomiting predominant, incubation 2h — likely:

A. Salmonella

B. S. aureus / B. cereus (emetic)

C. C. perfringens

D. EHEC

  1. Botulism — definitive treatment:

A. Penicillin

B. Antitoxin + ventilatory support

C. Clindamycin

C. IVIG

  1. HUS triad:

A. Haemolytic anaemia + thrombocytosis + AKI

B. Microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia + thrombocytopenia + AKI

C. Haemolytic anaemia + leukocytosis + AKI

D. Thrombocytopenia + leukocytosis + AKI

  1. C. difficile — when to use fidaxomicin:

A. Mild only

B. Recurrent CDI / high recurrence risk / cost not barrier

C. Severe only

D. First episode only

  1. Reactive arthritis — classic triad post-diarrhoea:

A. Arthritis + conjunctivitis + urethritis

B. Arthritis + conjunctivitis + rash

C. Arthritis + conjunctivitis + urethritis (HLA-B27 associated)

D. Arthritis + rash + fever

17. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 25-year-old returns from India, 3 days watery diarrhoea, cramps, low-grade fever. No blood. Best empirical treatment:

A. Ciprofloxacin 500mg BD × 3d

B. Azithromycin 500mg OD × 1–3d (resistance to fluoroquinolones in SE Asia)

C. Loperamide only

D. Rifaximin 200mg TDS × 3d

  1. A 3-year-old with bloody diarrhoea, fever 39°C, abdominal pain. Stool culture: Shigella flexneri. Best treatment:

A. Supportive only

B. Ciprofloxacin 500mg BD × 3d (or Azithromycin 20mg/kg OD × 3d)

C. Loperamide + ORS

D. IV fluids only

  1. A 65-year-old, 5 days post-clindamycin for dental abscess, profuse watery diarrhoea ×8/day, fever 38.5°C, abdominal tenderness. PCR: C. difficile toxin positive. Best initial treatment:

A. Metronidazole 500mg TDS

B. Vancomycin 125mg QDS PO × 10d (mild-moderate)

C. Fidaxomicin 200mg BD

D. Oral metronidazole + vancomycin

  1. A 30-year-old returns from Mexico, severe watery diarrhoea "rice-water", vomiting, profound dehydration. Suspected cholera. Best antibiotic:

A. Ciprofloxacin

B. Doxycycline 100mg BD × 3d (or Azithromycin 1g single)

C. Metronidazole

C. Ceftriaxone

  1. A 6-year-old with bloody diarrhoea, HUS (Hb 70, platelets 50, creatinine 150). Stool: EHEC O157:H7. Management:

A. Ciprofloxacin 500mg BD

B. Supportive only (NO antibiotics — ↑ HUS); renal replacement if needed

C. Ceftriaxone 2g IV

D. Azithromycin

  1. A 40-year-old post-cholecystectomy, 10 days clindamycin, watery diarrhoea, mild abdominal pain. C. difficile PCR positive, mild. Treatment:

A. Metronidazole 500mg TDS

B. Vancomycin 125mg QDS PO × 10d

C. Stop clindamycin only

D. Fidaxomicin 200mg BD

  1. A traveller to Thailand, 2 days watery diarrhoea, no blood/fever. Prescribed standby azithromycin. When to take?

A. Immediately

B. If >6 stools/24h OR blood OR fever OR severe cramps (moderate-severe)

C. Only if persists >48h

D. Only if fever

  1. A patient with Campylobacter jejuni infection develops ascending paralysis 2 weeks later. Diagnosis:

A. Stroke

B. Guillain-Barré Syndrome (molecular mimicry)

C. Transverse myelitis

D. Botulism

  1. A 25-year-old returns from Bangladesh, 2 weeks watery diarrhoea, weight loss. Stool O&P: Giardia cysts. Treatment:

A. Metronidazole 400mg TDS × 5–7d

B. Tinidazole 2g single dose

C. Both A and B acceptable

D. Albendazole

  1. Scombroid poisoning — management:

A. Antibiotics

B. Antihistamines (H1/H2 blockers)

C. Adrenaline

D. Steroids

18. Flashcards

  • Q: Bloody diarrhoea pathogens

A: Shigella, Salmonella, Campylobacter, EHEC, C. difficile, EIEC

  • Q: EHEC antibiotics

A: NEVER (↑ HUS)

  • Q: C. difficile 1st-line

A: Vancomycin 125mg QDS PO (fidaxomicin alt)

  • Q: Cholera

A: Rice-water stool + doxycycline + massive ORS

  • Q: Traveller's diarrhoea

A: ETEC most common; azithromycin standby

  • Q: Food poisoning 1–6h

A: S. aureus / B. cereus (emetic) = vomiting

  • Q: Botulism

A: Descending paralysis → antitoxin + ventilator

  • Q: HUS

A: MAHA + thrombocytopenia + AKI (EHEC most common)

  • Q: Reactive arthritis

A: Post-Salmonella/Shigella/Campylobacter/Yersinia (HLA-B27)

  • Q: C. difficile 1st-line

A: Vancomycin 125mg QDS PO (not metronidazole)

19. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQ 1: B — Shigella, Salmonella, Campylobacter, EHEC = invasive.

MCQ 2: B — EHEC = NO antibiotics (↑ HUS risk with antibiotics).

MCQ 3: B — Vancomycin/fidaxomicin 1st-line; metronidazole 2nd-line.

MCQ 4: B — Rice-water stool = massive secretory diarrhoea.

MCQ 5: B — ETEC ~50% of traveller's diarrhoea.

MCQ 6: B — S. aureus/B. cereus emetic = 1–6h, vomiting predominant.

MCQ 7: B — Antitoxin + ventilatory support (paralysis).

MCQ 8: B — MAHA + thrombocytopenia + AKI.

MCQ 9: B — Fidaxomicin for recurrent/high recurrence risk.

MCQ 10: C — Arthritis + conjunctivitis + urethritis (Reiter's triad).

SBA 1: B — Azithromycin preferred in SE Asia (fluoroquinolone resistance).

SBA 2: B — Shigella = ciprofloxacin/azithromycin (invasive = antibiotics).

SBA 3: B — Mild-mod CDI = vancomycin 125mg QDS (metronidazole 2nd-line).

SBA 4: B — Cholera = doxycycline 100mg BD × 3d or azithromycin 1g.

SBA 5: B — EHEC + HUS = NO antibiotics, supportive ± RRT.

SBA 6: B — CDI mild = vancomycin 125mg QDS (metronidazole no longer 1st-line).

SBA 7: B — Standby antibiotics for moderate-severe (>6 stools/blood/fever/cramps).

SBA 8: C — Post-Campylobacter = Guillain-Barré.

SBA 9: C — Both metronidazole and tinidazole effective for Giardia.

SBA 10: B — Scombroid = antihistamines (histamine-mediated).

topics.title: Infective Endocarditis
topics.html:
Related: [[Sepsis and Septic Shock]], [[Fever and Septic Syndrome Approach]], [[Staphylococcus aureus Bacteremia]], [[Prosthetic Valve Endocarditis]], [[Culture-Negative Endocarditis]]

[!important]
Infective endocarditis (IE) = microbial infection of endothelial surface of heart (valves, mural endocardium, implanted devices). Duke Criteria (Modified) for diagnosis. Blood cultures ×3 sets BEFORE antibiotics. Empirical antibiotics by valve type (native vs prosthetic) and timing. Surgery indications: heart failure, uncontrolled infection, prevention of embolism. Mortality 15–30%.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Apply Modified Duke Criteria for definite/possible/rejected IE
  • Obtain optimal blood cultures (timing, volume, sets)
  • Select empirical antibiotics by valve type, timing, and risk factors
  • Recognise complications: heart failure, embolic events, abscess, conduction abnormalities
  • Apply surgical indications and timing (urgent vs elective)
  • Manage special situations: prosthetic valve, device-related, culture-negative, right-sided, HIV

2. Definitions

TermDefinition
Native valve endocarditis (NVE)Infection of native valve leaflets
Prosthetic valve endocarditis (PVE)Infection of prosthetic valve (mechanical/bioprosthetic) or reconstruction material
Early PVE<1 year post-implant (usually nosocomial, Staphylococcus, Gram-neg, fungi)
Late PVE>1 year post-implant (community-acquired flora similar to NVE)
Cardiac device-related IE (CDRIE)Infection of pacemaker/ICD leads, generator pocket
Right-sided IETricuspid/pulmonic valve (IVDU, central lines)
Culture-negative IEClinical IE with negative blood cultures (prior antibiotics, fastidious organisms)

3. Modified Duke Criteria (2000 / ESC 2015)

Major Criteria

  1. Positive blood cultures for typical IE organism:
  • Typical microorganism from 2 separate blood cultures: Viridans streptococci, Streptococcus gallolyticus (bovis), HACEK, Staphylococcus aureus, Enterococcus faecalis OR
  • Persistently positive blood cultures: ≥2 positive cultures >12h apart, or all of 3/4+ sets, or single positive culture for Coxiella burnetii / antiphase I IgG >1:800
  1. Evidence of endocardial involvement:
  • Echocardiogram (TTE/TOE): Oscillating intracardiac mass (vegetation) on valve/supporting structures, abscess, new partial dehiscence of prosthetic valve
  • New valvular regurgitation (worsening of pre-existing not sufficient)

Minor Criteria

  1. Predisposition: Predisposing heart condition or IVDU
  2. Fever: ≥38°C
  3. Vascular phenomena: Major arterial emboli, septic pulmonary infarcts, mycotic aneurysm, intracranial haemorrhage, conjunctival haemorrhages, Janeway lesions
  4. Immunological phenomena: Glomerulonephritis, Osler nodes, Roth spots, rheumatoid factor
  5. Microbiological: Positive blood culture not meeting major criterion (e.g., single culture, atypical organism), serological evidence of active infection

Diagnosis

ClassificationCriteria
Definite IE2 Major OR 1 Major + 3 Minor OR 5 Minor
Possible IE1 Major + 1 Minor OR 3 Minor
RejectedFirm alternative diagnosis, resolution with ≤4 days antibiotics, no pathological evidence at surgery/autopsy
[!tip]
TOE superior to TTE (sensitivity ~95% vs ~65% for vegetations). Repeat TOE in 7–10d if initial negative but high suspicion.

4. Microbiology — Aetiology by Clinical Scenario

ScenarioCommon Organisms%
Native valve (community-acquired)Streptococcus viridans (20–30%), S. gallolyticus (bovis) (5–10%), Staphylococcus aureus (20–30%), Enterococcus (5–10%), HACEK (1–3%)~80%
Native valve (healthcare-associated)S. aureus (MRSA/MSSA), Enterococcus, CoNS, Gram-negatives
Prosthetic valve (early <1y)CoNS (30–40%), S. aureus, Gram-negatives (Pseudomonas, Serratia), Candida, Cutibacterium
Prosthetic valve (late >1y)Similar to NVE: Viridans strep, S. gallolyticus, Enterococcus, S. aureus
IVDU (right-sided)S. aureus (60–80%), Enterococcus, Streptococci, Gram-negatives, Candida
Cardiac device-relatedCoNS (50–70%), S. aureus, Enterococcus, Gram-negatives, Cutibacterium
Culture-negativePrior antibiotics (40%), Bartonella, Coxiella (Q fever), Brucella, Tropheryma whipplei, Mycoplasma, Chlamydia, Legionella, fungi, Mycobacteria
[!important]
S. gallolyticus (bovis) bacteraemia/IE → MANDATORY COLONOSCOPY (association with colonic adenoma/carcinoma 25–50%). HACEK = Haemophilus, Aggregatibacter, Cardiobacterium, Eikenella, Kingella — subacute, often culture-negative if not held long enough.

5. Blood Culture Optimisation

ParameterStandard
Sets3 sets (each set = 1 aerobic + 1 anaerobic bottle) from different venipuncture sites
Volume20–30mL per set (10mL per bottle) — low volume ↓ sensitivity
TimingBefore antibiotics; if already on abx → culture at trough, hold abx if possible
Incubation14 days (standard 5d misses HACEK, Bartonella, Brucella, Coxiella, T. whipplei)
Special requestsProlonged incubation, lysis-centrifugation (fungi, mycobacteria), PCR/16S rRNA, serology (Coxiella phase I/II, Bartonella, Brucella)

6. Clinical Features

FeatureFrequency
Fever90–95%
New/Changed murmur85–90%
Fatigue, weight loss, anorexia70–80%
Embolic phenomena20–50% (stroke, splenic infarct, mesenteric ischaemia, mycotic aneurysm)
Immunological phenomena10–20% (Osler nodes, Roth spots, Janeway lesions, glomerulonephritis)
Splenomegaly30–50%
Heart failure30–40% (acute AR/MR, valve perforation)
Conduction abnormalities5–10% (PR prolongation, AV block = perivalvular abscess)

Peripheral Signs (Classic but Uncommon)

SignDescription
Osler nodesTender, erythematous nodules on pulp of fingers/toes (immune complex)
Janeway lesionsNon-tender, haemorrhagic macules on palms/soles (septic emboli)
Roth spotsRetinal haemorrhages with pale centres
Splinter haemorrhagesLinear subungual haemorrhages
Conjunctival haemorrhagesPale conjunctivae with petechiae

7. Echocardiography

ModalitySensitivityRole
TTE~65%First-line; assess valve function, haemodynamics, complications
TOE~95%Definitive; vegetations <5mm, abscess, fistula, prosthetic dehiscence, prosthetic valve detail
Repeat TOE—7–10d if initial negative + high suspicion; or new complication
[!tip]
Vegetation size >10mm = higher embolic risk. Mobile/pedunculated > sessile. Abscess = perivalvular extension → conduction abnormalities, fistula, dehiscence.

8. Empirical Antibiotic Therapy (Before Culture Results)

Clinical ScenarioRegimenNotes
Native valve (community-acquired)Ceftriaxone 2g IV 12h + Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg IV 6h (+/- Gentamicin 3mg/kg IV OD ×2w)Covers viridans strep, S. gallolyticus, S. aureus, Enterococcus; gentamicin for synergy (optional, nephrotoxicity)
Native valve (healthcare-associated / IVDU)Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg IV 6h + Ceftriaxone 2g IV 12h + Gentamicin 3mg/kg IV ODMRSA coverage; gentamicin for Enterococcus synergy if suspected
Prosthetic valve (early <1y)Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg 6h + Meropenem 2g 8h + Rifampicin 300mg PO/IV 12h + Gentamicin 3mg/kg OD ×2wCoNS, S. aureus, Gram-neg; rifampicin for biofilm
Prosthetic valve (late >1y)Ceftriaxone 2g 12h + Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg 6h + Rifampicin 300mg 12h + Gentamicin 3mg/kg OD ×2wSimilar to NVE + rifampicin
Right-sided IE (IVDU, tricuspid)Ceftriaxone 2g 12h + Flucloxacillin 2g 4h (or Cefazolin) + Gentamicin 3mg/kg OD ×2w (+/- Vancomycin if MRSA risk)S. aureus dominant; shorter course possible (2–4w)
Penicillin allergy (anaphylaxis)Vancomycin + Meropenem + Gentamicin + Rifampicin (avoid cephalosporins)
Culture-negative (subacute, community)Ceftriaxone 2g 12h + Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg 6h + Gentamicin 3mg/kg OD ×2wAdd doxycycline 100mg 12h if Bartonella/Coxiella suspected

9. Targeted Therapy — Duration & Regimens

Organism / ScenarioRegimenDuration
Viridans streptococci / S. gallolyticus (NVE, susceptible)Penicillin G 24MU/day IV ÷4–6h OR Ceftriaxone 2g IV 12h + Gentamicin 3mg/kg IV OD ×2w (synergy for shorter course)4 weeks (2w if uncomplicated + gentamicin)
Viridans streptococci (penicillin MIC 0.12–0.5)Ceftriaxone 2g 12h + Gentamicin 3mg/kg OD ×4–6w4–6 weeks
Enterococcus faecalis (NVE, susceptible)Ampicillin 12g/day IV ÷4–6h + Ceftriaxone 2g 12h (preferred, less nephrotoxicity) OR Ampicillin + Gentamicin 3mg/kg OD6 weeks (4w if ampicillin-ceftriaxone)
Enterococcus faecalis (high-level aminoglycoside resistance)Ampicillin 12g/day IV ÷4–6h + Ceftriaxone 2g 12h6 weeks
Enterococcus faecium (usually ampicillin-resistant)Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg 6h + Daptomycin 8–10mg/kg IV OD (or Linezolid)6 weeks
S. aureus MSSA (NVE)Flucloxacillin 2g IV 4h (or Cefazolin 2g 8h) + Gentamicin 3mg/kg OD ×2w6 weeks (NVE); 4w if uncomplicated right-sided
S. aureus MRSA (NVE)Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg 6h (trough 15–20) + Gentamicin 3mg/kg OD ×2w6 weeks
S. aureus (PVE)Vancomycin + Rifampicin 300mg 12h + Gentamicin 3mg/kg OD ×2w (MSSA: flucloxacillin/cefazolin instead of vancomycin)6 weeks
CoNS (PVE)Vancomycin + Rifampicin 300mg 12h + Gentamicin 3mg/kg OD ×2w6 weeks
HACEKCeftriaxone 2g 12h4 weeks
Culture-negative (suspected Bartonella)Ceftriaxone 2g 12h + Doxycycline 100mg 12h + Gentamicin 3mg/kg OD ×2w6 weeks
Culture-negative (suspected Coxiella / Q fever)Doxycycline 100mg 12h + Hydroxychloroquine 200mg 8h (raise phagosomal pH)18 months
Fungal (Candida)Liposomal Amphotericin B 3–5mg/kg + Flucytosine 25mg/kg 6h → step-down fluconazole/echinocandin6 weeks post-valve replacement (surgery mandatory)
[!key]
Key Principle: Rifampicin ESSENTIAL for PVE and device-related IE (biofilm penetration). Gentamicin synergy shortens course for strep/Enterococcus but nephrotoxicity risk. Ampicillin-ceftriaxone preferred over ampicillin-gentamicin for E. faecalis.

10. Surgical Indications (ESC 2017 / AHA 2015)

Class I (Strong Recommendation)

IndicationTiming
Heart failure due to valve dysfunction (acute severe AR/MR, dehiscence)URGENT / EMERGENCY
Uncontrolled infection: persistent fever/bacteraemia >5–7d appropriate antibiotics, abscess, fistula, enlarging vegetationURGENT (within days)
Recurrent emboli despite appropriate antibiotics + large (>10mm) mobile vegetationURGENT
Fungal / multidrug-resistant organism IEURGENT

Class IIa (Reasonable)

IndicationTiming
Large vegetation >10mm + other high-risk features (embolic, severe valve stenosis)ELECTIVE (after 1–2w antibiotics if stable)
Vegetation >15mm (high embolic risk)Consider early surgery
Perivalvular abscess / fistula without HFURGENT / ELECTIVE
Severe valve stenosisELECTIVE

Neurological Complications & Surgery Timing

Neurological StatusSurgery Timing
Ischaemic stroke WITHOUT haemorrhageDelay NOT required — surgery if indicated (Class IIa)
Ischaemic stroke WITH haemorrhagic transformationDelay ≥4 weeks (if possible)
Intracranial haemorrhageDelay ≥4 weeks
Brain abscess / mycotic aneurysmMultidisciplinary; often delay surgery

11. Complications

ComplicationFeaturesManagement
Heart failureAcute AR/MR, valve perforation, chordal ruptureUrgent surgery
Perivalvular abscessNew conduction defect (PR↑, AV block), fistula, dehiscenceSurgery (often urgent); TOE for diagnosis
Embolic eventsStroke (MCA), splenic infarct, mesenteric ischaemia, mycotic aneurysm, septic pulmonary emboli (right-sided)Anticoagulation generally AVOIDED; surgery if recurrent/large veg
Septic metastatic fociOsteomyelitis, discitis, septic arthritis, endophthalmitis, renal infarctsSource control + antibiotics
GlomerulonephritisImmune complex; haematuria, proteinuria, RBC castsResolves with antibiotic treatment
Renal embolism / infarctFlank pain, haematuriaSupportive

12. Special Situations

SituationKey Points
Prosthetic valve IERifampicin mandatory (biofilm); early (<1y) = nosocomial flora; late (>1y) = community flora; surgery often needed
Cardiac device-related IEDevice removal mandatory if pocket infection, S. aureus, Gram-neg, fungaemia, sepsis; lead extraction (laser/mechanical); antibiotics 2–6w post-removal
Right-sided IE (IVDU)S. aureus tricuspid; shorter course (2–4w) if uncomplicated; septic pulmonary emboli common; surgery rare
Culture-negative IEPrior antibiotics (stop 2w if possible); serology (Coxiella phase I IgG >1:800, Bartonella, Brucella); 16S rRNA PCR on valve tissue; PET-CT for metastatic foci
HIVSame organisms; S. aureus more common; drug interactions (rifampicin + ART); immune reconstitution
PregnancyAvoid aminoglycosides (otic toxicity), fluoroquinolones, doxycycline; ceftriaxone, vancomycin, ampicillin safe; TOE safe; surgery if indicated

13. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Modified Duke Criteria: 2 Major / 1M+3m / 5m = definite; 1M+1m / 3m = possible
  • Blood cultures: 3 sets, 20–30mL/set, 14-day incubation, BEFORE antibiotics
  • TOE > TTE for vegetations, abscess, prosthetic detail
  • NVE empirical: Ceftriaxone + Vancomycin (+/- Gentamicin)
  • PVE empirical: Vancomycin + Meropenem + Rifampicin + Gentamicin (early); Ceftriaxone + Vancomycin + Rifampicin + Gentamicin (late)
  • Rifampicin ESSENTIAL for PVE and device IE (biofilm)
  • Targeted therapy durations: Strep 4w (2w + gentamicin), Enterococcus 6w, S. aureus 6w, PVE 6w
  • Ampicillin-ceftriaxone for E. faecalis (less nephrotoxicity than gentamicin)
  • S. gallolyticus (bovis) → COLONOSCOPY mandatory
  • Surgery indications: HF (urgent), uncontrolled infection (urgent), recurrent emboli + large veg (urgent), fungal/MDR (urgent)
  • Neurological: ischaemic stroke no haemorrhage → no delay; haemorrhage → delay 4w
  • Device IE: REMOVE device if pocket infection, S. aureus, Gram-neg, fungaemia, sepsis

14. Common Viva Questions

  1. What are the Modified Duke Criteria for definite IE? 2 Major OR 1 Major + 3 Minor OR 5 Minor.
  2. How many blood culture sets for IE? 3 sets (aerobic + anaerobic each) from different sites, 20–30mL/set, 14-day incubation.
  3. What is the empirical regimen for community-acquired NVE? Ceftriaxone 2g 12h + Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg 6h (+/- Gentamicin).
  4. What is the empirical regimen for early PVE (<1y)? Vancomycin + Meropenem + Rifampicin + Gentamicin.
  5. Why is rifampicin essential in PVE? Biofilm penetration on prosthetic material.

...continued (truncated to 200 lines for renderer)

Topic Overview

1. Learning Objectives
2. Definitions
3. Modified Duke Criteria (2000 / ESC 2015)
Major Criteria
Minor Criteria
Diagnosis
4. Microbiology — Aetiology by Clinical Scenario
5. Blood Culture Optimisation
6. Clinical Features
Peripheral Signs (Classic but Uncommon)
7. Echocardiography
8. Empirical Antibiotic Therapy (Before Culture Results)
9. Targeted Therapy — Duration & Regimens
10. Surgical Indications (ESC 2017 / AHA 2015)
Class I (Strong Recommendation)
Class IIa (Reasonable)
Neurological Complications & Surgery Timing
11. Complications
12. Special Situations
13. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points
14. Common Viva Questions

topics.title: Influenza And Respiratory Viruses
topics.html:
Related: [[Community-Acquired Pneumonia (CAP)]], [[Hospital-Acquired and Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia (HAP-VAP)]], [[Fever and Septic Syndrome Approach]], [[Sepsis and Septic Shock]]

[!important]
Influenza causes seasonal epidemics and occasional pandemics. Rapid diagnosis, early antivirals (≤48h), and complication recognition are exam essentials. Other respiratory viruses (RSV, hMPV, parainfluenza, adenovirus, coronaviruses) cause overlapping syndromes — distinguish by epidemiology, age, and testing.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Classify influenza viruses and understand antigenic drift vs shift
  • Recognise clinical syndromes: uncomplicated flu, flu pneumonia, exacerbations, post-viral complications
  • Apply testing strategies (POC PCR, multiplex panels) and interpret results
  • Initiate early antiviral therapy (oseltamivir, baloxavir) in high-risk groups
  • Manage complications: secondary bacterial pneumonia, myocarditis, encephalitis, ARDS
  • Understand vaccination indications, timing, and effectiveness

2. Definition

  • Influenza A/B/C/D: Orthomyxoviridae; A and B cause human epidemics; C = mild; D = cattle
  • Influenza A subtypes: Defined by HA (H1–H18) and NA (N1–N11); current seasonal = H1N1, H3N2
  • Antigenic drift: Point mutations in HA/NA → seasonal epidemics
  • Antigenic shift: Reassortment of gene segments → novel subtype → pandemic potential

3. Core Microbiology

VirusFamilyKey Features
Influenza AOrthomyxoviridaePandemics, avian/swine reservoirs, H1N1/H3N2 seasonal
Influenza BOrthomyxoviridaeNo pandemics, two lineages (Victoria, Yamagata), seasonal
RSVPneumoviridaeBronchiolitis in infants, severe in elderly/immunocompromised
hMPVPneumoviridaeRSV-like, all ages, winter/spring
Parainfluenza 1–4ParamyxoviridaeCroup (PIV-1,2), bronchiolitis (PIV-3), year-round
AdenovirusAdenoviridaePharyngoconjunctival fever, pneumonia (types 3, 4, 7, 14), military recruits
Seasonal coronavirusesCoronaviridaeOC43, 229E, NL63, HKU1 — common cold, sometimes LRTI
SARS-CoV-2CoronaviridaeCOVID-19 — separate chapter

4. Normal Values / Important Cut-offs

ParameterThresholdSignificance
Rapid antigen test sensitivity50–70%High specificity; negative does not rule out
RT-PCR / multiplex PCRSensitivity >95%Gold standard; detects co-infections
Oseltamivir window≤48h from onsetMax benefit; still consider if severe/hospitalised >48h
Baloxavir window≤48hSingle dose; active against oseltamivir-resistant strains
Vaccine effectiveness40–60% (good match)Lower in elderly; reduces severity/hospitalisation

5. Clinical Syndromes

SyndromeFeaturesKey Viruses
Uncomplicated influenzaAbrupt fever, myalgia, headache, dry cough, sore throat, fatigueInfluenza A/B
Influenza pneumoniaPrimary viral pneumonia — dyspnoea, hypoxia, bilateral infiltratesInfluenza A (H1N1, H3N2, avian)
Exacerbation of chronic diseaseCOPD/asthma/heart failure decompensationInfluenza, RSV, hMPV
CroupBarking cough, stridor, hoarseness — childrenParainfluenza 1, 2, 3
BronchiolitisWheeze, crackles, respiratory distress <2yRSV, hMPV, PIV-3
Post-influenza bacterial pneumoniaBiphasic illness: flu → improvement → fever/cough/purulent sputumS. pneumoniae, S. aureus, H. influenzae

6. Approach / Algorithm

flowchart TD
  A[Acute respiratory illness +/- fever] --> B{Severe? Hypoxia, haemodynamic instability, altered mental status}
  B -->|Yes| C[ABCDE, O2, IV access, lactate, CXR, blood cultures, multiplex PCR, start oseltamivir + empirical CAP antibiotics]
  B -->|No| D{High-risk for complications?}
  D -->|Yes| E[Multiplex PCR nose/throat swab; start oseltamivir if ≤48h or hospitalised]
  D -->|No| F[Clinical diagnosis OK; supportive care; oseltamivir if ≤48h and wish to shorten duration]
  C --> G[Admit; monitor for bacterial superinfection]
  E --> H[Review PCR at 24-48h; de-escalate antibiotics if viral only]

7. Investigations

TestIndicationInterpretation
Multiplex respiratory PCR (nose/throat swab)Hospitalised, immunocompromised, high-risk, outbreak investigationDetects 15–25 pathogens; co-infection 5–10%
POC rapid antigen (flu/RSV/SARS-CoV-2)ED, primary care, triageFast (15 min); low sensitivity — negative ≠ rule out
Viral cultureReference lab, surveillanceSlow (days); not for clinical management
Serology (paired acute/convalescent)Retrospective diagnosis, surveillanceNot acute management
CXRSuspected pneumoniaInfluenza: bilateral interstitial/ground-glass; bacterial superinfection: lobar consolidation
ProcalcitoninDistinguish viral vs bacterial; guide antibioticsLow (<0.25) supports viral; high (>0.5) suggests bacterial co-infection
Full blood countAllLymphopenia common in influenza; neutrophilia suggests bacterial

8. Antiviral Therapy

DrugDoseWindowKey Points
Oseltamivir75mg BD PO ×5d (adult); renal adjust≤48h ideal; still give if hospitalised/severe >48hReduces duration 1–2d, complications, mortality in hospitalised; nausea common
Baloxavir marboxil40mg (<80kg) or 80mg (≥80kg) single dose PO≤48hCap-dependent endonuclease inhibitor; single dose; active vs oseltamivir-resistant; not in pregnancy/severe immunosuppression
Zanamivir10mg (2 inhalations) BD ×5d≤48hInhaled; avoid in asthma/COPD (bronchospasm risk)
Peramivir600mg IV single dose≤48hIV option; renal adjust
[!important]
Oseltamivir in severe/hospitalised influenza: give regardless of time since onset — observational data show mortality benefit even >48h. Dose: 75mg BD; consider 150mg BD in critically ill/ICU (off-label, some guidelines).

9. High-Risk Groups for Antivirals (Treat Empirically)

  • Age ≥65 or <2 years
  • Pregnancy / ≤2 weeks postpartum
  • Chronic pulmonary (COPD, asthma), cardiac, renal, hepatic, haematological, metabolic (diabetes), neurological/neuromuscular
  • Immunosuppression (HIV, transplant, chemo, steroids ≥20mg prednisolone/day)
  • BMI ≥40
  • Nursing home residents
  • All hospitalised patients with suspected/confirmed influenza

10. Complications

ComplicationFeaturesManagement
Primary viral pneumoniaProgressive dyspnoea, hypoxia, bilateral ground-glass — high mortalityICU, oseltamivir, supportive; corticosteroids controversial (avoid routine)
Secondary bacterial pneumoniaBiphasic: flu → improvement → recrudescence fever/purulent sputum; S. pneumoniae, S. aureus (MRSA), H. influenzaeEmpirical antibiotics covering above; CXR consolidation
Myositis/rhabdomyolysisSevere calfpain, elevated CK, myoglobinuriaHydration, monitor renal function
Myocarditis/pericarditisChest pain, elevated troponin, ECG changes, arrhythmiaSupportive, cardiology input
Encephalitis/encephalopathyAltered mental status, seizures — esp. childrenICU, oseltamivir IV (if available), supportive
ARDSRefractory hypoxia, bilateral infiltratesLung-protective ventilation, prone, ECMO if refractory
Reye syndromeAspirin in children + viral illness → hepatic encephalopathyAvoid aspirin <16y; stavudine?

11. Red Flags / Emergencies

  • Hypoxia (SpO₂ <92%) → admit, oxygen, consider HFNC/NIV/ICU
  • Haemodynamic instability → septic shock protocol
  • Altered mental status → consider encephalitis, treat empirically
  • Rapid progression → primary viral pneumonia or bacterial superinfection
  • Immunocompromised + respiratory symptoms → low threshold for admission, multiplex PCR, early antivirals

12. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
COVID-19Anosmia/ageusia, longer incubation, higher thrombosis risk, multiplex PCR distinguishes
CAP (bacterial)Lobar consolidation, high procalcitonin, neutrophilia, purulent sputum from onset
Atypical pneumoniaMycoplasma, Legionella, Chlamydia — subacute, extrapulmonary features
Pulmonary embolismSudden dyspnoea, pleuritic pain, risk factors, CTI D-dimer/CTPA
Heart failureOrthopnoea, PND, elevated BNP, fluid overload signs

13. Special Situations

SituationManagement
PregnancyOseltamivir 75mg BD ×5d (category C, but benefit > risk); baloxavir contraindicated; vaccinate any trimester
ImmunocompromisedPCR testing mandatory; oseltamivir 75mg BD ×10d (prolonged); consider IV peramivir; monitor for prolonged shedding/resistance
Renal impairmentOseltamivir: CrCl 10–30 → 30mg BD; CrCl <10 → 30mg OD; dialysis → 30mg post-HD
Oseltamivir resistance (H275Y)Rare in current strains; if suspected → baloxavir or IV zanamivir/peramivir
Outbreak in care homeTreat all symptomatic + post-exposure prophylaxis for contacts (oseltamivir 75mg OD ×10d)

14. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Influenza A vs B: A = pandemics, avian reservoirs, H1N1/H3N2; B = two lineages, no pandemics
  • Antigenic drift vs shift: drift = point mutations (seasonal); shift = reassortment (pandemic)
  • Oseltamivir window: ≤48h for outpatients; give to all hospitalised regardless of timing
  • High-risk groups for antivirals: age extremes, pregnancy, chronic disease, immunosuppression, obesity
  • Secondary bacterial pneumonia: biphasic illness — S. pneumoniae > S. aureus > H. influenzae
  • Procalcitonin: low = viral; high = bacterial co-infection — guides antibiotics
  • Vaccine: quadrivalent (2A + 2B); annual; effectiveness 40–60%; reduces severity even if mismatch
  • Baloxavir: single dose; not in pregnancy/severe immunosuppression; active vs oseltamivir-resistant
  • Reye syndrome: aspirin + viral illness in children — avoid aspirin <16y
  • Multiplex PCR: gold standard; detects co-infections; preferred over rapid antigen for hospitalised

15. Common Viva Questions

  1. When do you give oseltamivir >48h after symptom onset? Hospitalised patients, severe disease, immunocompromised — observational mortality benefit.
  2. What is the typical CXR finding in primary influenza pneumonia? Bilateral interstitial/ground-glass infiltrates (ARDS pattern).
  3. Name three bacterial pathogens causing post-influenzal pneumonia. S. pneumoniae, S. aureus (including MRSA), H. influenzae.
  4. How does baloxavir differ from oseltamivir? Single dose, cap-dependent endonuclease inhibitor, active vs oseltamivir-resistant strains, not in pregnancy.
  5. What is Reye syndrome and how is it prevented? Aspirin in children with viral illness → hepatic encephalopathy; avoid aspirin <16y.

16. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
Rapid antigen negative = no fluSensitivity 50–70% — never rule out based on negative antigen; PCR if high suspicion
Oseltamivir only if ≤48hHospitalised/severe: give regardless of timing
Influenza B milder than ACan be equally severe; both cause hospitalisation/death
Vaccine prevents all fluEffectiveness 40–60%; mismatch years lower; still reduces severity
Baloxavir for everyoneContraindicated in pregnancy, severe immunosuppression, <5y (some regions)
Secondary pneumonia = same as CAPBiphasic history key; different empiric cover (add MRSA if recent flu)

17. Mnemonics

  • FLU HIGH-RISK: Flu season, Lungs (COPD/asthma), U (immunocompromised), Heart disease, Important (pregnancy), Glucose (diabetes), Heavy (BMI≥40), Renal failure, Infants/elderly, Steroids, Kids in care homes
  • ANTIVIRALS: Abilify? no — Agent (oseltamivir/baloxavir), Nephro adjust, Time (≤48h ideal but hospitalised anyway), ICU (higher dose considered), Vaccine annually, Immunocompromised (longer course), Resistance rare (H275Y), Aspirin avoid <16y, Lactation OK, Secondary bacterial pneumonia

18. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Influenza & Respiratory Viruses))
    Influenza
      A (pandemics, H1N1/H3N2)
      B (Victoria/Yamagata)
      Drift vs Shift
    Other Viruses
      RSV
      hMPV
      Parainfluenza
      Adenovirus
      Seasonal CoV
    Clinical
      Uncomplicated
      Viral pneumonia
      Exacerbations
      Croup/Bronchiolitis
      Post-viral bacterial
    Diagnosis
      Multiplex PCR (gold)
      Rapid Ag (low sens)
      Procalcitonin
    Treatment
      Oseltamivir (BD x5d)
      Baloxavir (single dose)
      Zanamivir/Peramivir
      High-risk groups
    Complications
      Viral pneumonia
      Bacterial superinfection
      Myocarditis/Encephalitis
      Reye syndrome
    Prevention
      Quadrivalent vaccine
      Antiviral prophylaxis

...continued (truncated to 200 lines for renderer)

topics.title: Intestinal Protozoa Amoebiasis Giardiasis
topics.html:
Related: [[Fever in Returned Traveller and FUO]], [[Infective Diarrhoea and Food Poisoning]], [[HIV-Associated Opportunistic Infections]], [[Infection in Immunocompromised Host (Transplant, Biologics)]], [[Travel Medicine and Pre-Travel Advice]]

[!important]
Amoebiasis = Entamoeba histolytica; faecal-oral; dysentery + liver abscess. Giardiasis = Giardia lamblia; faecal-oral; malabsorption + steatorrhoea. Both: cysts in stool = diagnosis; metronidazole = treatment. Amoebic liver abscess = metronidazole + luminal agent.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Differentiate pathogenic E. histolytica from non-pathogenic E. dispar
  • Recognise clinical syndromes: amoebic dysentery, amoebic liver abscess, giardiasis
  • Select appropriate diagnostics (antigen, PCR, microscopy)
  • Choose appropriate therapy: tissue-active + luminal agent for amoebiasis; metronidazole for giardiasis
  • Apply prevention strategies for travellers and endemic areas

2. Entamoeba histolytica (Amoebiasis)

Epidemiology & Transmission

AspectDetails
AgentEntamoeba histolytica (pathogenic); E. dispar, E. moshkovskii (non-pathogenic)
TransmissionFaecal-oral (cysts in contaminated food/water, person-to-person); sexual (MSM)
GeographyTropical/subtropical; endemic in India, Africa, Latin America, SE Asia
RiskTravellers, immigrants, MSM, institutionalised, immunocompromised

Pathogenesis

StepMechanism
1. IngestionMature quadrinucleate cysts survive gastric acid
2. ExcystationTerminal ileum/caecum → 8 trophozoites per cyst
3. ColonisationTrophozoites adhere to colonic mucosa (lectin-galactose)
4. InvasionLytic enzymes → flask-shaped ulcers (caecum, ascending colon, rectosigmoid)
5. Haematogenous SpreadPortal venous → liver abscess (right lobe); rare: lung, brain, spleen

Clinical Syndromes

SyndromeFeatures
Asymptomatic Cyst PasserCysts in stool; no symptoms; carrier
Intestinal Amoebiasis (Dysentery)Bloody mucoid diarrhoea (10–20/day), tenesmus, lower abdominal pain, low-grade fever; weight loss
Fulminant ColitisSevere bloody diarrhoea, fever, peritoneal signs, toxic megacolon, perforation; high mortality
AmoebomaGranulomatous mass (caecum/ascending colon) mimicking carcinoma
Amoebic Liver Abscess (ALA)Most common extraintestinal; right lobe (80%); fever, RUQ pain, hepatomegaly, anchovy sauce pus; no concurrent dysentery in 50%
Rare ExtraintestinalPleuropulmonary (rupture), cerebral (haematogenous), cutaneous, genital

Diagnosis

TestSensitivity/SpecificityUtility
Stool Antigen (EIA)High (>95%); distinguishes E. histolytica from E. dispar1st-line
Stool PCRHighest (near 100%); species-specificGold standard if available
Stool MicroscopyLow (60%); trophozoites with ingested RBCs = pathogenicImmediate; look for cysts (quadrinucleate)
Serology (IHA/ELISA)High sensitivity; persists yearsALA (stool often negative); not for acute dysentery
Imaging (US/CT/MRI)Liver abscess: round, hypoechoic, peripheral enhancementALA diagnosis; rule out pyogenic
[!warning]
Stool microscopy cannot distinguish E. histolytica from E. dispar. Antigen/PCR required for species ID. ALA: stool often negative; serology + imaging diagnostic.

Treatment

SyndromeRegimenDuration
Intestinal Amoebiasis / ALAMetronidazole 800mg PO 8h (or Tinidazole 2g OD)7–10 days
Severe/FulminantMetronidazole 500mg IV 8h7–10 days
Luminal Agent (ESSENTIAL after tissue-active)Paromomycin 500mg PO 8h OR Diloxanide furoate 500mg PO 8h OR Iodoquinol 650mg PO 8h10 days (start after metronidazole)
[!critical]
Metronidazole alone = high relapse (luminal cysts persist). ALWAYS follow with luminal agent (paromomycin/diloxanide/iodoquinol). Tinidazole single-dose alternative for intestinal amoebiasis.

Amoebic Liver Abscess — Specific Management

AspectManagement
MedicalMetronidazole 800mg 8h ×7–10d → Paromomycin 500mg 8h ×10d
AspirationIndicated: imminent rupture, >5cm, left lobe, no response 5–7d, diagnostic uncertainty
AntibioticsAdd ceftriaxone if secondary bacterial infection suspected
Follow-upUS at 2–4w; serology may persist years

3. Giardia lamblia (Giardiasis)

Epidemiology & Transmission

AspectDetails
AgentGiardia lamblia (G. intestinalis, G. duodenalis)
TransmissionFaecal-oral (cysts in water/food, person-to-person); waterborne outbreaks; day-care centres, hikers (stream water), MSM
GeographyWorldwide; endemic in areas with poor sanitation; travellers' diarrhoea
RiskChildren, hikers, swimmers, immunocompromised, hypogammaglobulinaemia

Clinical Features

PresentationFeatures
AsymptomaticCyst shedding; common in endemic areas
Acute DiarrhoeaWatery, foul-smelling, no blood/mucus; cramps, bloating, flatulence, nausea, steatorrhoea (floating, foul stools)
Chronic GiardiasisRecurrent diarrhoea, weight loss, malabsorption (vitamins A, D, E, K, B12), lactose intolerance, failure to thrive (children)
ExtraintestinalRare; urticaria, reactive arthritis, retinal arteritis

Diagnosis

TestUtility
Stool Antigen (EIA)High sensitivity/specificity; 1st-line
Stool PCRHigh sensitivity; multiplex panels
Stool MicroscopyCysts (oval, 4 nuclei) / trophozoites (pear-shaped, "falling leaf" motility); 3 samples = 90% sensitivity
String Test (Enterotest)Duodenal sampling; historical
SerologyNot useful for acute diagnosis

Treatment

RegimenDoseDuration
Metronidazole400mg PO 8h (2g single dose alternative)5–7 days
Tinidazole2g PO single doseSingle dose (preferred)
Nitazoxanide500mg PO 12h3 days (alternative, paediatric)
Paromomycin500mg 8h5–10 days (pregnancy: 1st trimester)
Albendazole400mg 12h5–10 days (alternative)
[!tip]
Tinidazole 2g single dose = preferred (high cure, compliance). Metronidazole 5–7d = standard. Pregnancy: paromomycin 1st trimester; metronidazole 2nd/3rd trimester.

Chronic/Refractory Giardiasis

CauseManagement
ReinfectionRepeat treatment; hygiene; screen contacts
HypogammaglobulinaemiaIVIG replacement; prolonged metronidazole/tinidazole
Drug ResistanceSwitch class (nitazoxanide, albendazole, paromomycin)
Post-Giardia SyndromeIBS-like; low FODMAP; exclude SIBO

4. Differential Diagnosis

FeatureAmoebiasisGiardiasisBacterial Dysentery
StoolBloody, mucoidWatery, fatty, no bloodBloody, mucoid
TenesmusProminentMild/absentProminent
SteatorrhoeaNoYesNo
Liver AbscessCommon (ALA)NoNo
MicroscopyTrophozoites + ingested RBCsCysts/trophozoites (no RBCs)WBCs, bacteria
Antigen/PCRE. histolytica specificGiardia specificCulture

5. Prevention

MeasureAmoebiasisGiardiasis
WaterBoil/filter/iodine/chlorine (cysts resistant to chlorine)Same; cysts resistant to chlorine
FoodCook/peel; avoid raw salads in endemic areasSame
HygieneHandwashing; safe faecal disposalHandwashing; day-care hygiene
SexualBarrier protection (MSM)Barrier protection (MSM)
TravellersBoil/peel/cook; bottled waterSame; avoid stream water

6. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Amoebiasis: E. histolytica; dysentery (bloody mucoid stool) + liver abscess (ALA); metronidazole + luminal agent (paromomycin/diloxanide); ALA = metronidazole + aspiration if >5cm/imminent rupture
  • Giardiasis: G. lamblia; watery diarrhoea, steatorrhoea, bloating, no blood; tinidazole 2g single dose (preferred) or metronidazole 5–7d
  • Diagnosis: Antigen/PCR for both (microscopy insufficient for E. histolytica vs E. dispar); stool antigen 1st-line
  • Amoebic liver abscess: RUQ pain, fever, hepatomegaly, "anchovy sauce" pus; serology + imaging; metronidazole + paromomycin (luminal agent essential)
  • Tinidazole: 2g single dose = preferred for giardiasis; also for amoebiasis
  • Luminal agent mandatory after metronidazole (paromomycin, diloxanide, iodoquinol) to eradicate cysts
  • Amoebic liver abscess aspiration: >5cm, left lobe, imminent rupture, no response 5–7d

7. Common Viva Questions

  1. Difference between E. histolytica and E. dispar? E. histolytica = pathogenic (ingested RBCs in trophozoites, causes dysentery/ALA); E. dispar = non-pathogenic commensal.
  2. Why luminal agent after metronidazole for amoebiasis? Metronidazole kills trophozoites (tissue) but not cysts (lumen); luminal agent eradicates cysts → prevents relapse.
  3. Diagnosis of amoebic liver abscess? RUQ pain, fever, hepatomegaly; US/CT: hypoechoic lesion right lobe; serology positive; "anchovy sauce" pus on aspiration.
  4. Giardiasis treatment of choice? Tinidazole 2g single dose (preferred) or metronidazole 400mg 8h ×5–7d.
  5. Difference amoebic vs bacillary dysentery? Amoebic: bloody mucoid stool, tenesmus, no high fever, trophozoites with RBCs; Bacillary: high fever, profuse bloody stool, neutrophils, culture positive.
  6. Trophozoite with ingested RBCs = ? Entamoeba histolytica (pathogenic).
  7. Amoebic liver abscess aspiration indications? >5cm, left lobe, imminent rupture, no clinical response 5–7 days, diagnostic uncertainty.
  8. Giardia cyst vs trophozoite? Cyst: oval, 4 nuclei, resistant; Trophozoite: pear-shaped, 2 nuclei, 4 flagella, "falling leaf" motility.

8. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
Metronidazole alone cures amoebiasisLuminal agent (paromomycin/diloxanide) MANDATORY after metronidazole to eradicate cysts
Microscopy distinguishes E. histolytica from E. disparCannot distinguish microscopically; need antigen/PCR
Amoebic liver abscess always with dysentery50% have NO concurrent dysentery
Giardia always causes diarrhoeaAsymptomatic carriage common (especially endemic)
Metronidazole 1st trimester safeAvoid 1st trimester; paromomycin preferred; tinidazole avoid
Tinidazole single dose ineffective2g single dose = preferred for giardiasis (high cure, compliance)
Amoebic liver abscess = pyogenicALA: "anchovy sauce" pus, serology +ve, single right lobe; Pyogenic: multiple, polymicrobial, septic

9. Mnemonics

  • AMOEBIASIS: Amoebic Liver Abscess = Metronidazole + Paromomycin; Dysentery = Metronidazole + Luminal
  • E. HISTOLYTICA: Entamoeba Histolytica = Erythrophagia (ingests RBCs); Endemic tropics; Flask ulcers
  • GIARDIASIS: Giardia = Steatorrhoea + Bloating; Tinidazole 2g single; Metronidazole 5-7d
  • ALA: Anchovy Sauce pus; Right lobe; Metronidazole + Paromomycin; Aspiration if >5cm
  • LUMINAL AGENT: Paromomycin / Diloxanide / Iodoquinol = Post-Metronidazole Essential

10. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Intestinal Protozoa))
    Amoebiasis (E. histolytica)
      Transmission: Faecal-oral, cysts
      Dysentery: Bloody mucoid, tenesmus
      ALA: RUQ pain, fever, anchovy sauce pus
      Diagnosis: Antigen/PCR (E. histolytica vs dispar); Serology for ALA
      Treatment: Metronidazole 7-10d + Luminal agent (Paromomycin 10d)
      ALA: Metronidazole + Paromomycin; Aspiration if >5cm/rupture risk
    Giardiasis (G. lamblia)
      Transmission: Faecal-oral, waterborne
      Symptoms: Watery diarrhoea, steatorrhoea, bloating, NO blood
      Diagnosis: Antigen/PCR (1st line); Microscopy (cysts/trophozoites)
      Treatment: Tinidazole 2g single (preferred) OR Metronidazole 5-7d
      Chronic: Hypogammaglobulinaemia, post-infectious IBS

*...continued (truncated to 200 lines for renderer)*

topics.title: Intra Abdominal Infections Secondary Peritonitis Abscess
topics.html:
Related: [[Sepsis and Septic Shock]], [[Clostridioides difficile Infection]], [[Acute Pancreatitis]], [[Liver Abscess]], [[Antimicrobial Stewardship]]

[!important]
Intra-abdominal infection (IAI) = peritoneal inflammation ± abscess from GI tract microbes. Primary = spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP); Secondary = GI perforation/visceral rupture; Tertiary = recurrent/persistent after adequate source control. Source control = DEFINITIVE treatment (surgery/drainage); antibiotics = ADJUNCT. Empirical: cover Gram-neg + anaerobes (Enterococcus situationally). Duration: 4 days post-source-control if adequate (STOP-IT trial). MDR risk: healthcare-associated, prior abx, immunosuppression.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Classify IAI: primary (SBP), secondary (perforation), tertiary (recurrent)
  • Diagnose using clinical, laboratory, imaging criteria
  • Apply source control principles (surgery, percutaneous drainage)
  • Select empirical antibiotics by acquisition (community vs healthcare) and MDR risk
  • Determine treatment duration (4 days post-source-control)
  • Manage special scenarios: SBP, tertiary peritonitis, pancreatic necrosis infection, liver abscess
  • Recognise when antifungal coverage needed

2. Classification

TypeDefinitionTypical Pathogens
Primary (SBP)Ascites fluid infection without surgically treatable source (cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome)E. coli, Klebsiella, Streptococcus spp., Enterococcus (usually monomicrobial)
SecondaryPerforation/rupture of GI tract (appendicitis, diverticulitis, peptic ulcer, ischaemic bowel, trauma, anastomotic leak)Polymicrobial: Gram-neg (E. coli, Klebsiella) + Anaerobes (Bacteroides fragilis) ± Enterococcus
TertiaryRecurrent/persistent infection ≥48h after adequate source control + antibioticsMDR: Pseudomonas, Acinetobacter, Enterococcus (VRE), Candida, Enterobacteriaceae (ESBL/CRE)
[!key]
Source control (surgery/drainage) = PRIMARY treatment. Antibiotics alone FAIL without source control. Tertiary peritonitis = failure of source control or host factors.

3. Diagnosis

Clinical

FeatureSecondary PeritonitisSBPTertiary Peritonitis
PainSevere, localised → generalisedMild, diffusePersistent/recurrent
FeverHigh, rigorsLow-grade or absentPersistent/recurrent
SignsRigid abdomen, guarding, absent bowel soundsShifting dullness, mild tendernessVariable, may be masked
SepsisCommonVariableCommon

Laboratory

TestFindings
WCCElevated (or low in severe sepsis)
CRP / PCTMarkedly elevated
Lactate↑ if hypoperfusion/sepsis
Ascitic fluid (SBP)PMN ≥250 cells/mm³ (diagnostic); culture (blood culture bottles at bedside)
Blood culturesPositive 30–50% (secondary); 20–30% (SBP)

Imaging

ModalityRole
CT abdomen/pelvis with contrastGold standard — free air, fluid collections, abscess, thickened bowel, source identification
UltrasoundBedside, ascites, gallbladder, pelvic collections
X-ray (erect chest/abdomen)Free air (perforation) — quick but less sensitive

4. Source Control — The DEFINITIVE Treatment

SourceIntervention
Perforated peptic ulcerLaparoscopic/primary repair + omental patch
AppendicitisAppendicectomy (+/- drainage if abscess)
Diverticulitis (perforated)Resection (Hartmann's) or laparoscopic lavage (selected)
Anastomotic leakRe-laparotomy, diversion, drainage
Ischaemic bowelResection of necrotic segment
Intra-abdominal abscessPercutaneous drainage (CT/US-guided) ± surgery
Infected pancreatic necrosisStep-up approach: percutaneous drainage → endoscopic necrosectomy → minimally invasive surgery
[!critical]
Source control within 6–12h of diagnosis. Antibiotics WITHOUT source control = treatment failure. Damage control surgery for unstable: quick source control, temporary abdomen closure, ICU resuscitation, planned re-laparotomy.

5. Empirical Antibiotic Therapy

Community-Acquired Secondary Peritonitis (Low MDR Risk)

RegimenDosesSpectrum
Piperacillin-tazobactam4.5g IV 6hGram-neg + Anaerobes + Enterococcus (some) — 1st line
Ceftriaxone 2g IV 12h + Metronidazole 500mg IV 8hAlternativeGram-neg + Anaerobes (NO Enterococcus)
Meropenem 1g IV 8hAlternative (if β-lactam allergy not anaphylaxis)Broad Gram-neg + Anaerobes
Cefepime 2g IV 8h + Metronidazole 500mg IV 8hAlternativeGram-neg (Pseudomonas) + Anaerobes
Ertapenem 1g IV 24hAlternative (if NO Pseudomonas risk)Gram-neg + Anaerobes

Healthcare-Associated / High MDR Risk (Prior abx, hospitalisation, immunosuppression, tertiary peritonitis)

RegimenDosesSpectrum
Meropenem 1g IV 8hPreferredGram-neg (Pseudomonas, ESBL) + Anaerobes
Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV 6hAlternative (if local Pseudomonas susceptibility)Gram-neg + Anaerobes + Enterococcus
Cefepime 2g IV 8h + Metronidazole 500mg IV 8hAlternativePseudomonas + Anaerobes
+ Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg IV 6hIf Enterococcus risk (prosthetic material, prior Enterococcus, severe sepsis)VRE: Linezolid/Daptomycin
+ Fluconazole 400mg IV/PO ODIf Candida risk (recent abx, TPN, immunosuppression, tertiary peritonitis, gastric leak)Candida: Echinocandin if fluconazole-R or unstable
[!tip]
Enterococcus coverage: NOT routine for community secondary peritonitis. Add if: prosthetic material, immunosuppression, severe sepsis, valvular disease, prior Enterococcus, tertiary peritonitis. Candida coverage: add if risk factors (recent broad abx, TPN, immunosuppression, tertiary peritonitis, gastric/oesophageal leak, prolonged ICU).

SBP (Primary Peritonitis)

RegimenDoseDuration
Cefotaxime 2g IV 8h (or Ceftriaxone 2g IV 24h)1st line5 days (extend if slow response)
AlternativePiperacillin-tazobactam, Meropenem
Albumin infusion1.5g/kg day 1 + 1g/kg day 3Prevents renal dysfunction (↓ mortality)

Intra-abdominal Abscess (Drained)

ScenarioDuration
Adequate source control (drainage + antibiotics)4 days post-source-control (STOP-IT trial)
Inadequate source control / ongoing sepsisExtend until resolution

6. Treatment Duration — STOP-IT Trial Evidence

ScenarioDuration
Adequate source control achieved4 days FIXED after source control (non-inferior to 8–10 days)
Source control NOT achievable / inadequateContinue until clinical resolution + source control achieved
SBP5 days (extend if slow response)
Infected pancreatic necrosisIndividualised (usually 2–4 weeks after drainage)
[!key]
STOP-IT trial (2015): 4 days post-source-control = non-inferior to longer courses. Adequate source control = all purulent material removed, no ongoing leak, drains output <200mL/day enteric content.

7. Special Scenarios

Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis (SBP)

  • Diagnosis: Ascitic fluid PMN ≥250/mm³ (send in blood culture bottles at bedside)
  • Empirical: Cefotaxime 2g IV 8h ×5d (or Ceftriaxone 2g IV 24h)
  • Albumin: 1.5g/kg day 1 + 1g/kg day 3 (prevents HRS, ↓ mortality)
  • Prophylaxis: Secondary prophylaxis after 1st episode (Norfloxacin 400mg PO daily or TMP-SMX) — until transplant or resolution of ascites
  • Primary prophylaxis: In cirrhosis with ascitic protein <1.5g/dL + Child-Pugh ≥9 or variceal bleed (Norfloxacin 400mg daily)

Tertiary Peritonitis

  • Definition: Recurrent/persistent peritonitis ≥48h after adequate source control + antibiotics
  • Pathogens: MDR Gram-neg (Pseudomonas, Acinetobacter, ESBL/CRE), Enterococcus (VRE), Candida
  • Management: Source control re-assessment (CT, re-laparotomy), broad antibiotics (Meropenem ± Vancomycin ± Fluconazole/Echinocandin), immunomodulation, nutrition

Infected Pancreatic Necrosis

  • Step-up approach (PANTER trial):
  1. Percutaneous drainage (first)
  2. Endoscopic transluminal necrosectomy (if drainage fails)
  3. Minimally invasive surgery (VARD) → Open necrosectomy (last)
  • Antibiotics: Meropenem (penetrates necrosis) ± anti-MRSA ± antifungal
  • Timing: Delay intervention ≥4 weeks if possible (walled-off necrosis)

Pyogenic Liver Abscess

AetiologyPathogensTreatment
Biliary (most common)E. coli, Klebsiella, Enterococcus, BacteroidesPercutaneous drainage + antibiotics
Portal venous (diverticulitis, appendicitis)Polymicrobial, anaerobes prominentDrainage + antibiotics
HaematogenousS. aureus, Streptococcus, CandidaAntibiotics ± drainage
CryptogenicKlebsiella (K1/K2 hypervirulent), E. coliDrainage + antibiotics

Empirical: Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g 6h OR Ceftriaxone 2g 24h + Metronidazole 500mg 8h

Duration: 2–4 weeks (IV→oral after drainage + clinical improvement)

Klebsiella liver abscess: Metastatic infection risk (endophthalmitis, meningitis, brain abscess) — ophthalmology review

8. Antifungal Coverage Indications

IndicationPreferred Agent
Candida isolated from intra-abdominal cultureEchinocandin (Caspofungin/Micafungin/Anidulafungin) if unstable/fluconazole-R; Fluconazole if stable/susceptible
High-risk clinical scenario (no culture yet): recent broad abx, TPN, immunosuppression, tertiary peritonitis, gastric/oesophageal leak, prolonged ICUEmpirical echinocandin (preferred over fluconazole for critically ill)
Candida in pancreatic necrosisEchinocandin
[!tip]
Echinocandin preferred for empirical/definitive therapy in critically ill (fungicidal, no drug interactions, broad Candida coverage). Fluconazole if stable, susceptible, no recent azole exposure.

9. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • IAI classification: primary (SBP), secondary (perforation), tertiary (recurrent post-source-control)
  • Source control = DEFINITIVE treatment (surgery/drainage); antibiotics = adjunct
  • Community secondary peritonitis: Pip-tazo 4.5g 6h (1st line) covers Gram-neg + anaerobes + some Enterococcus
  • Healthcare-associated/high MDR risk: Meropenem 1g 8h (preferred) ± Vancomycin ± Fluconazole/Echinocandin
  • Enterococcus coverage: NOT routine; add if prosthetic material, severe sepsis, immunosuppression, prior Enterococcus
  • Candida coverage: add if recent broad abx, TPN, immunosuppression, tertiary peritonitis, gastric leak
  • Duration: 4 days FIXED post-adequate source control (STOP-IT trial)
  • SBP: Ascitic PMN ≥250 → Cefotaxime 5d + Albumin 1.5g/kg D1 + 1g/kg D3
  • SBP prophylaxis: secondary (after 1st episode) norfloxacin/TMP-SMX; primary if ascitic protein <1.5 + Child-Pugh ≥9
  • Infected pancreatic necrosis: step-up (drainage → endoscopic → surgery); Meropenem (penetrates necrosis)
  • Liver abscess: drainage + antibiotics 2–4w; Klebsiella → metastatic screening (eye, brain)

10. Common Viva Questions

  1. What is the difference between primary, secondary, and tertiary peritonitis? Primary = SBP (no surgical source); Secondary = GI perforation; Tertiary = recurrent after adequate source control.
  2. What is the most important treatment for secondary peritonitis? Source control (surgery/drainage) — antibiotics alone fail.
  3. Empirical antibiotics for community-acquired secondary peritonitis? Pip-tazo 4.5g 6h (covers Gram-neg, anaerobes, some Enterococcus).
  4. When do you add Enterococcus coverage? Prosthetic material, severe sepsis, immunosuppression, valvular disease, prior Enterococcus, tertiary peritonitis.
  5. When do you add antifungal coverage? Recent broad abx, TPN, immunosuppression, tertiary peritonitis, gastric/oesophageal leak, prolonged ICU.
  6. What is the recommended duration after adequate source control? 4 days fixed (STOP-IT trial).
  7. SBP diagnosis? Ascitic fluid PMN ≥250/mm³.
  8. SBP treatment? Cefotaxime 2g IV 8h ×5d + Albumin 1.5g/kg D1 + 1g/kg D3.
  9. Infected pancreatic necrosis management? Step-up: percutaneous drainage → endoscopic necrosectomy → minimally invasive surgery.
  10. Klebsiella liver abscess complication? Metastatic infection (endophthalmitis, meningitis, brain abscess) — ophthalmology review.

11. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
Antibiotics alone for secondary peritonitisSource control MANDATORY; antibiotics adjunct only
Enterococcus coverage for all secondary peritonitisNOT routine; only if risk factors present
10–14 days antibiotics for all IAI4 days post-source-control if adequate (STOP-IT)
SBP = secondary peritonitisSBP = primary peritonitis (no surgical source); ascites PMN ≥250
Albumin not needed in SBPAlbumin 1.5g/kg D1 + 1g/kg D3 prevents HRS, ↓ mortality
Fluconazole for all CandidaEchinocandin preferred for critically ill/empirical; fluconazole if stable/susceptible
Meropenem doesn't cover anaerobesMeropenem covers anaerobes EXCELLENTLY (including B. fragilis)
Tertiary peritonitis = just longer antibioticsTertiary = reassess source control + MDR pathogens + host factors
Pancreatic necrosis = immediate surgeryStep-up approach: delay ≥4w, drainage first, surgery last
Liver abscess = antibiotics aloneDrainage + antibiotics (except very small <3cm)

12. Mnemonics

  • IAI CLASSIFICATION: Primary = SBP (no source); Secondary = Source (perforation); Tertiary = Third time (recurrent post-source-control)
  • SOURCE CONTROL: Surgery/Drainage = PRIMARY; Abx = ADJUNCT
  • COMMUNITY SECONDARY: Pip-tazo (Gram-neg + Anaerobes + some Entero)
  • HEALTHCARE/TERIARY: Mero (Pseudo/ESBL) ± Vanco (Entero) ± Echinocandin (Candida)
  • ENTEROCoccus ADD: Prosthetic, Severe sepsis, Immunosuppressed, Valvular, Prior Entero, Tertiary
  • CANDIDA ADD: Recent broad abx, TPN, Immunosuppressed, Tertiary, Gastric leak, ICU prolonged
  • DURATION: 4 DAYS post-source-control (STOP-IT)
  • SBP: PMN ≥250 → Cefotaxime 5d + Albumin 1.5/1g/kg

...continued (truncated to 200 lines for renderer)

Topic Overview

3. Diagnosis

5. Empirical Antibiotic Therapy
Community-Acquired Secondary Peritonitis (Low MDR Risk)
Healthcare-Associated / High MDR Risk (Prior abx, hospitalisation, immunosupp...

Intra-abdominal Abscess (Drained)
