topics.title: Invasive Candidiasis Candidaemia
topics.html:
[!info] Davidson Ch 11 Alignment: Infectious Disease → Specific Organism Groups → Fungi → Candida
FCPS/MRCP Focus: Candidaemia, invasive candidiasis, echinocandins (1st line), azoles, source control (CVC removal), TEE, ophthalmology, prophylaxis in ICU

1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Define Candidaemia: Candida in Blood Culture; Invasive Candidiasis: Deep-seated Infection (Candidemia, Deep-Seated Candidiasis, Chronic Disseminated)
  • [ ] Identify Risk Factors: ICU, CVC, Broad-Spectrum ABX, TPN, Surgery, Neutropenia, Diabetes, Corticosteroids
  • [ ] Recognise Species Distribution: C. albicans (50%), C. glabrata (20-30%), C. parapsilosis (10-20%), C. tropicalis, C. krusei
  • [ ] Diagnose: Blood Culture (Gold Standard), T2Candida (Rapid), β-D-Glucan (Screening), Fundoscopy (Ocular), TEE (Endocarditis)
  • [ ] Manage: Echinocandins (1st Line) — Caspofungin/Micafungin/Anidulafungin; Azoles (Step-Down, Step-Up), Source Control (CVC Removal), TEE, Fundoscopy
  • [ ] Apply Prophylaxis: Fluconazole in High-Risk ICU (Expected Stay >3d, High Candida Colonisation), Neutropenia

2. 📖 Definition & Epidemiology

TermDefinition
CandidaemiaCandida spp. Isolated from Blood Culture
Invasive CandidiasisDeep-Seated Infection: Candidaemia, Deep-Seated Candidiasis (Hepatosplenic, Osteomyelitis, Endocarditis, Meningitis), Chronic Disseminated
Incidence~10-15/100,000 (Increasing); ICU Mortality 30-40%
Species DistributionC. albicans (~50%), C. glabrata (20-30%), C. parapsilosis (10-20%), C. tropicalis, C. krusei
Mortality30-40% (Attributable 20-30%)
[!tip] Candidaemia = Blood Culture Positive. Invasive Candidiasis = Candidaemia + Deep-Seated Infection. C. albicans ~50%, C. glabrata 20-30% (Azole Resistance), C. krusei (Intrinsic Fluconazole Resistance).

3. 📖 Risk Factors

Major Risk FactorsPopulation
Central Venous Catheter (CVC)Most Common Source (Biofilm Formation)
Broad-Spectrum AntibioticsProlonged, Multiple Classes
Total Parenteral Nutrition (TPN)Hyperglycaemia, Catheter
Abdominal SurgeryGastrectomy, Whipple, Transplant, Perforation
NeutropeniaANC <500, Prolonged (>7-10 Days)
Diabetes MellitusPoor Glycaemic Control
Corticosteroids / ImmunosuppressionHigh-Dose, Prolonged
ICU StayProlonged >7 Days, Mechanical Ventilation
Dialysis / Renal FailureCatheters, Immune Dysfunction
Age ExtremesNeonates (VLBW), Elderly
Malignancy / ChemotherapyMucositis, Neutropenia, CVC

4. 📖 Candida Species — Resistance Profiles

SpeciesFrequencyFluconazoleVoriconazoleEchinocandinsKey Features
C. albicans~50%SusceptibleSusceptibleSusceptibleMost Common, Hyphae/Formation, Biofilm
C. glabrata20-30%Resistant (Dose-Dependent / Resistant)Variable (Often Resistant)SusceptibleIncreasing, Azole Resistance Common, Biofilm
C. parapsilosis10-20%SusceptibleSusceptibleHigher MICs (Reduced Susceptibility)Catheter-Related, Biofilm Former
C. tropicalis5-10%SusceptibleSusceptibleSusceptibleNeutropenic Hosts
C. krusei1-5%Intrinsically ResistantSusceptibleSusceptibleIntrinsic Fluconazole Resistance
C. aurisEmergingMultidrug ResistantOften ResistantVariableOutbreaks, Multidrug Resistant, Environmental Persistence
[!warning] C. glabrata: Fluconazole Resistance Common → Echinocandin 1st Line. C. krusei: Intrinsic Fluconazole Resistance → Avoid Fluconazole. C. auris: XDR, Infection Control Critical.

5. 🔬 Diagnostic Workup

flowchart TD
    A[Suspected Invasive Candidiasis] --> B[**Blood Culture** (Gold Standard)]
    B --> C{**Positive?**}
    C -->|Yes| D[**Species ID + Susceptibility** (MALDI-TOF / PCR / Sequencing)]
    C -->|No (High Suspicion)| E[**Non-Culture Diagnostics**]
    E --> E1[**β-D-Glucan (BDG)** — Serum, High NPV, Serial]
    E --> E2[**T2Candida Panel (T2MR)** — Direct Blood, 3-5h, Species ID]
    E --> E3[**Mannan/Anti-Mannan Ab** — Serology]
    D & E --> F[**Source Control Assessment**]
    F --> F1[**CVC Removal** (Strongest Predictor of Survival)]
    F --> F2[**TEE (Endocarditis)** / **Fundoscopy (Ocular)** / **Imaging (CT/MRI)**]

Key Diagnostic Tests

TestSensitivity/SpecificityRoleTurnaround
Blood CultureGold Standard (Sens 50-70% per Set)Definitive (Species + Susceptibility)24-48h
T2Candida Panel (T2MR)Sens 85-95%, Spec >95%Rapid Species ID (Direct Blood)3-5 Hours
β-D-Glucan (BDG)Sens 70-85%, Spec 75-85%Screening / Serial Monitoring (Trend)Hours
Mannan/Anti-Mannan AgSens 50-70%, Spec 80-90%AdjunctHours
Candida ScoreClinical Score (Colonisation, Surgery, TPN, Sepsis, ICU)Risk Stratification (Score ≥3 = High Risk)Bedside
[!tip] T2Candida = Rapid Species ID Direct from Blood (3-5h). BDG = Screening/Monitoring (Serial Trends). Blood Culture = Gold Standard but Slow.

6. 💊 Management

1. Initial Antifungal Therapy (Empirical / Directed)

Species / Clinical Scenario1st LineAlternativeDuration
Candidaemia / Invasive Candidiasis (Most)Echinocandin (Caspofungin 70mg LD → 50mg OD; Micafungin 100mg OD; Anidulafungin 200mg LD → 100mg OD)Liposomal Amphotericin B 3-5mg/kg (If Echinocandin Contraindicated)14 Days from Last Positive Blood Culture + Clinical Resolution
C. krusei / C. glabrata (Fluconazole Resistant)Echinocandin (1st Line)Liposomal Amphotericin BAs Above
Step-Down Therapy (Stable, Negative Blood Cultures, Susceptible Isolate)Fluconazole 400-800mg OD (C. albicans, C. tropicalis, C. parapsilosis)Voriconazole (If Fluconazole Resistance)Complete 14 Days Total
NeonatesLiposomal Amphotericin B 3-5mg/kg OR Micafungin (Dose by Weight)Fluconazole (If Susceptible)14 Days
[!tip] Echinocandins = 1st Line for Candidaemia/Invasive Candidiasis (Caspofungin/Micafungin/Anidulafungin). Step-Down to Fluconazole ONLY if: Stable, Negative Blood Cultures, Susceptible Isolate (C. albicans, tropicalis, parapsilosis).

Source Control (Critical)

InterventionImpact
CVC RemovalStrongest Predictor of Survival; Remove ASAP (Within 24h of Positive Culture)
Infected Device Removal (Pacemaker, Prosthetic Joint, Drain)Essential for Cure
Drainage (Abscess, Pleural Empyema, Peritonitis)Source Control Essential
Ophthalmology ReviewAll Candidaemia → Fundoscopy (Endophthalmitis 5-15%)
Echocardiography (TEE Preferred)All Candidaemia → Rule Out Endocarditis (5-10%)
[!warning] CVC Removal = Single Strongest Predictor of Survival in Candidaemia. Do Not Delay.

7. 🩺 Complications & Organ-Specific Management

ComplicationManagement
EndocarditisLiposomal Ampho B 5mg/kg + Flucytosine 25mg/kg Q6H → Step-Down Fluconazole; Valve Surgery Usually Required
EndophthalmitisVitrectomy + Intravitreal Amphotericin/Voriconazole + Systemic Echinocandin/Fluconazole
CNS (Meningitis/Abscess)Liposomal Ampho B 5mg/kg + Flucytosine 25mg/kg Q6H → Step-Down Fluconazole 800mg OD
Hepatosplenic Candidiasis (Chronic Disseminated)Echinocandin / Fluconazole (Months); Immunosuppression Reduction
Osteomyelitis / Septic ArthritisEchinocandin / Fluconazole + Surgical Debridement

8. 🛡️ Prophylaxis

PopulationAgentDurationEvidence
ICU (High Risk)Fluconazole 400mg ODUntil ICU Discharge / Day 14Cochrane: ↓ Invasive Candidiasis, No Mortality Benefit
Selective (High Candida Colonisation + Risk Factors)Fluconazole 400mg OD / Micafungin 50mg ODICU StayTargeted Better Than Universal
Neutropenia (Prolonged >7d, ANC<100)Posaconazole / Fluconazole / MicafunginUntil Neutrophil Recovery↓ Invasive Fungal Infections
Stem Cell TransplantPosaconazole / Fluconazole / MicafunginEngraftment + 30-100dGVHD Prophylaxis Protocol
Solid Organ Transplant (Liver/Intestinal)Fluconazole / Micafungin4-12 WeeksHigh-Risk Period
[!warning] Universal Prophylaxis NOT Recommended — Targeted/Selective to High-Risk Only. Stewardship: Avoid Unnecessary Azole Exposure (Resistance Selection).

9. 🔄 Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDifferentiating Features
Candidaemia vs ContaminationMultiple Positive Sets, Clinical Signs, Risk Factors, Persistent Positivity
Candidaemia vs Other FungaemiaSpecies ID (Cryptococcus, Aspergillus, Mucormycosis, Histoplasma)
Candidaemia vs BacteriemiaGram Stain (Yeast vs Bacteria), Culture, Clinical Context
C. glabrata vs C. albicansFluconazole Resistance, Higher Mortality, Elderly, ICU
C. auris vs Other CandidaMDR, Outbreak Setting, Environmental Persistence, Infection Control

10. 💡 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionCandidaemia = Blood Culture +; Invasive Candidiasis = Candidaemia + Deep-Seated
SpeciesC. albicans (50%), C. glabrata (20-30%, Azole Resistant), C. parapsilosis (10-20%), C. krusei (Intrinsic Fluconazole Resistance)
1st Line RxEchinocandin (Caspofungin/Micafungin/Anidulafungin) — All Species, Including Resistant
Step-DownFluconazole 400-800mg OD — Only if Stable, Negative Cultures, Susceptible (C. albicans, tropicalis, parapsilosis)
Source ControlCVC Removal = Survival Predictor; TEE, Fundoscopy Mandatory
ProphylaxisFluconazole/ICU High-Risk; Targeted > Universal; Posaconazole/Micafungin in Neutropenia
C. krusei / C. glabrataIntrinsic / Acquired Fluconazole Resistance → Echinocandin 1st Line
C. aurisMDR, Outbreak Risk, IPC Critical
Duration14 Days from Last Positive Culture + Clinical Resolution

11. ❓ Viva Questions

  1. What is the first-line treatment for candidaemia?
  • Echinocandin (Caspofungin 70mg LD → 50mg OD / Micafungin 100mg OD / Anidulafungin 200mg LD → 100mg OD).
  1. Why are echinocandins preferred over fluconazole for initial therapy?
  • Broad Spectrum (Incl. C. glabrata, C. krusei), Fungicidal, Less Drug Interactions, Less Hepatotoxicity, Superior Outcomes in RCTs.
  1. When can you step down from echinocandin to fluconazole?
  • Clinically Stable, Blood Cultures Negative ×2, Isolate Susceptible to Fluconazole (C. albicans, C. tropicalis, C. parapsilosis).
  1. What is the single most important intervention for survival in candidaemia?
  • Central Venous Catheter Removal (Within 24h of Positive Culture).
  1. Which Candida species are intrinsically resistant to fluconazole?
  • C. krusei (Intrinsic Resistance); C. glabrata (Dose-Dependent/High-Level Resistance Common).
  1. What is the recommended duration of therapy for candidaemia?
  • 14 Days from Last Positive Blood Culture + Clinical Resolution.
  1. What is the role of β-D-Glucan in candidaemia?
  • Screening / Serial Monitoring (High NPV); Not Diagnostic Alone.
  1. How do you manage C. auris candidaemia?
  • Infection Control Paramount (Isolation, Contact Precautions, Environmental Cleaning); Echinocandin 1st Line; Susceptibility-Guided; Infection Control Team Involvement.
  1. What is the role of Fluconazole step-down therapy?
  • After Clinical Stability + Negative Cultures + Susceptible Isolate; Cost-Effective, Oral Bioavailability.
  1. What are the key complications of candidaemia requiring specific management?
  • Endocarditis (TEE + Surgery), Endophthalmitis (Fundoscopy + Vitrectomy), CNS (LAmB + Flucytosine), Hepatosplenic (Prolonged Therapy).
  1. How do you screen for invasive candidiasis in ICU?
  • Candida Score (≥3 = High Risk) → Empirical Echinocandin; Serial β-D-Glucan / T2Candida (If Available).
  1. What is the management of C. auris candidaemia?
  • Strict Isolation, Contact Precautions, Environmental Disinfection; Echinocandin 1st Line; Susceptibility Testing Critical; Infection Control Team Involvement.

12. 🧠 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Fluconazole vs Echinocandin 1st LineEchinocandin 1st Line (Broad, Cidal, Resistant Species); Fluconazole = Step-Down Only
C. glabrata vs C. albicansC. glabrata: Azole Resistance, Higher Mortality, Elderly/ICU; C. albicans: Most Common, Fluconazole Susceptible
C. krusei vs C. glabrataC. krusei = Intrinsic Fluconazole Resistance; C. glabrata = Dose-Dependent/Acquired Resistance
C. auris vs C. haemuloniiC. auris = MDR, Outbreaks, Identification by MALDI-TOF/PCR
Fluconazole Step-Down CriteriaStable + Negative Blood Cultures + Fluconazole-Susceptible Isolate
MnemonicMeaning
"Echinocandin = 1st Line for Candidaemia"1st Line Therapy
"CVC Out = Survival Up"Source Control
"Krusei = Fluconazole Resistant (Intrinsic)"Intrinsic Resistance
"Glabrata = Azole Resistant (Often)"Acquired Resistance
"Fundoscopy + TEE = Mandatory"Complications Screening
"Duration = 14d from Last Positive Culture"Duration Rule

13. 🗺️ Mind Map

14. 📋 One-Page Revision Card

INVASIVE CANDIDIASIS – FCPS/MRCP REVISION CARD
Definition: Candidaemia = Blood Culture +; Invasive = Candidaemia + Deep-Seated

topics.title: Leishmaniasis Trypanosomiasis
topics.html:
[!info] Davidson Ch 11 Alignment: Infectious Disease → Specific Organism Groups → Parasites (Protozoa) → Leishmania & Trypanosoma
FCPS/MRCP Focus: Visceral leishmaniasis (Kala-azar), cutaneous/mucocutaneous leishmaniasis, African trypanosomiasis (sleeping sickness), Chagas disease, diagnosis, treatment

1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Classify Leishmania Species: L. donovani/infantum (Visceral), L. tropica/major/ethiopica (Cutaneous), L. braziliensis (Mucocutaneous)
  • [ ] Recognise Visceral Leishmaniasis (VL/Kala-azar): Fever, Splenomegaly, Pancytopenia, Weight Loss, Hypergammaglobulinaemia
  • [ ] Recognise Cutaneous (CL) & Mucocutaneous (ML): Ulcers, Mucosal Destruction (Nose/Pharynx)
  • [ ] Diagnose Leishmaniasis: rK39 Dipstick (VL), Smear/PCR/Culture (CL/ML), MAT/ELISA (Serology)
  • [ ] Manage Leishmaniasis: Liposomal Amphotericin B (VL), Miltefosine (VL/CL), Amphotericin B (ML), Antimonials (Historical)
  • [ ] Classify Trypanosomes: T. brucei gambiense/rhodesiense (African Sleeping Sickness), T. cruzi (Chagas Disease)
  • [ ] Recognise African Trypanosomiasis: Stage 1 (Haemo-lymphatic) vs Stage 2 (Meningo-encephalitic)
  • [ ] Recognise Chagas Disease: Acute (Romaña Sign), Chronic (Cardiac, GI Megasyndromes)
  • [ ] Manage Trypanosomiasis: Suramin/Pentamidine (Stage 1), Eflornithine/Nifurtimox (Stage 2), Benznidazole/Nifurtimox (Chagas)

2. 📖 Leishmaniasis

Classification & Geography

Clinical FormSpeciesGeographyVector
Visceral (VL/Kala-azar)L. donovani (Indian Subcontinent, East Africa), L. infantum (Mediterranean, Middle East, China, Latin America)India (Bihar), East Africa (Sudan, Ethiopia, Kenya), BrazilPhlebotomus Sandfly
Cutaneous (CL)L. tropica (Urban, Middle East), L. major (Rural, Middle East, Africa), L. mexicana (Americas)Middle East, Central Asia, AmericasPhlebotomus / Lutzomyia
Mucocutaneous (ML)L. braziliensis (Brazil, Bolivia, Peru), L. panamensis (Central America)Latin AmericaLutzomyia Sandfly
Post-Kala-Azar Dermal Leishmaniasis (PKDL)L. donovaniIndia, SudanPhlebotomus
[!tip] VL = Kala-azar = Fever + Splenomegaly + Pancytopenia + Hypergammaglobulinaemia. CL = Orient Sore (Self-Healing Ulcer). ML = Mucosal Destruction (Espundia). PKDL = Post-Kala-Azar Dermal Leishmaniasis.

3. 📖 Visceral Leishmaniasis (VL / Kala-azar)

Clinical Features

FeatureDetails
Classic TriadFever (Intermittent, Double Quotidian), Massive Splenomegaly, Pancytopenia
OtherWeight Loss, Wasting, Hypergammaglobulinaemia (↑ IgG/IgM), Liver Enlargement, Lymphadenopathy (African VL), Skin Changes (Hyperpigmentation/Kala-azar = "Black Fever")
ComplicationsSecondary Infections (TB, Pneumonia), Bleeding (Thrombocytopenia), Renal Failure, PKDL (Post-Treatment)

Diagnosis

TestSensitivity/SpecificityRole
rK39 Dipstick (Rapid)Sens 95-98%, Spec 90-95%Field-Friendly, Rapid Screening (VL)
Serology (DAT, ELISA, IFAT)High Sens/SpecConfirmatory, Antibody Persists
Parasite DemonstrationSplenic Aspiration > BM > Lymph NodeGold Standard (Microscopy: LD Bodies in Macrophages)
PCRHigh Sens/SpecSpecies ID, Monitoring
Aldehyde Test (Formal Gel)Low SensitivityHistorical

Treatment (VL)

Region / Scenario1st LineAlternativeDuration
Indian Subcontinent (L. donovani)LAmB 10mg/kg Single Dose (or 5mg/kg Day 1-5)Miltefosine 50mg BD (Weight-Based) × 28dSingle Dose LAmB
East Africa (L. donovani)LAmB 3-5mg/kg/day × 5-10dSSG+PM (Sodium Stibogluconate + Paromomycin) × 17d5-10 Days LAmB
Mediterranean/Latin America (L. infantum)LAmB 3-5mg/kg/day × 5-10dMiltefosine5-10 Days
HIV Co-infectionLAmB 4mg/kg/day Days 1-5, 10, 14, 21, 28, 35, 42Secondary Prophylaxis: LAmB MonthlyInduction + Maintenance
[!warning] Miltefosine = Teratogenic (Contraindicated in Pregnancy). SSG = Cardiotoxicity/Pancreatitis (Monitor ECG/Amylase). LAmB = Preferred (Safety, Efficacy).

4. 📖 Cutaneous Leishmaniasis (CL) & Mucocutaneous (ML)

Cutaneous Leishmaniasis (CL)

FeatureOld World (L. tropica, L. major)New World (L. mexicana, L. braziliensis)
LesionSingle/Multiple Ulcers (Volcano-Shaped, Raised Edge, Indurated Base)Larger, More Inflammatory, Multiple
HealingSelf-Limiting (3-6 Months) → Hypopigmented ScarSlower, Higher Risk ML (L. braziliensis)
TypesDry (Urban/L. tropica) vs Wet (Rural/L. major)

Mucocutaneous Leishmaniasis (ML / Espundia)

FeatureDetails
AgentL. braziliensis (Predominant), L. panamensis, L. guyanensis
PathogenesisMetastatic Spread from Primary Cutaneous Lesion → Mucosa (Nose, Pharynx, Larynx)
PresentationNose (Epistaxis, Septal Perforation, Saddle Nose), Pharynx/Larynx (Dysphagia, Hoarseness, Airway Obstruction)
TimingMonths-Years After Primary Cutaneous Lesion Heals

Treatment (CL/ML)

Form1st LineAlternative
CL (Old World)Intral Lesional Sodium Stibogluconate / Cryotherapy / Topical Paromomycin / ThermotherapySystemic: Miltefosine / LAmB / Pentavalent Antimonials
CL (New World) / MLLAmB 3mg/kg/day × 5-10d / MiltefosinePentavalent Antimonials (SSG/MA)
MLLAmB 3-5mg/kg/day × 20-30dPentavalent Antimonials × 30d
[!warning] ML = Medical Emergency (Airway Risk). Early Systemic Treatment Essential.

5. 📖 Trypanosomiasis

African Trypanosomiasis (Sleeping Sickness)

FeatureT. b. gambiense (West/Central Africa)T. b. rhodesiense (East/Southern Africa)
EpidemiologyChronic (98% Cases), AnthroponoticAcute (Weeks), Zoonotic (Cattle/Game)
VectorGlossina palpalis (Riverine)Glossina morsitans (Savannah)
Stage 1 (Haemo-lymphatic)Fever, Lymphadenopathy (Winterbottom's Sign: Posterior Cervical), Splenomegaly, Pruritus, RashSimilar but Rapid Progression (Weeks)
Stage 2 (Meningo-encephalitic)Months-YearsWeeks-Months
Sleep Disturbance (Reversed Circadian), Neuropsychiatric, Tremor, Ataxia, Seizures, ComaSame but Faster
[!tip] Winterbottom's Sign = Posterior Cervical Lymphadenopathy = Pathognomonic for T.b. gambiense.

Stage 1 vs Stage 2 Diagnostic Criteria

CriterionStage 1 (Haemo-lymphatic)Stage 2 (Meningo-encephalitic)
CSF WBC<5/µL>5/µL (Usually >20)
CSF ProteinNormalElevated (>50mg/dL)
Trypanosomes in CSFAbsentPresent
ClinicalNo Neurological SignsNeurological Signs Present

Treatment (African Trypanosomiasis)

StageT. b. gambienseT. b. rhodesiense
Stage 1Fexinidazole 1800mg OD × 10 Days (Oral, All Stages) OR Pentamidine 4mg/kg IM × 7-10dSuramin 20mg/kg IV Days 1,3,7,14,21 (Test Dose First)
Stage 2Fexinidazole 1800mg OD × 10d (If No Severe Neuro) OR Nifurtimox-Eflornithine Combination (NECT)Melarsoprol 2.2mg/kg IV Days 1-3, 10, 17-19, 25-27 (Arsenical, Toxic)
[!warning] Fexinidazole = Oral, Effective for Both Stages (gambiense). NECT = Eflornithine 400mg/kg Q6H IV ×14d + Nifurtimox 15mg/kg BD PO ×14d (Safer than Melarsoprol). Melarsoprol = Arsenical, Encephalopathy Risk (5-10%).

6. 📖 Chagas Disease (American Trypanosomiasis)

Clinical Phases

PhaseFeatures
Acute (Weeks 1-8)Romaña's Sign (Unilateral Periorbital Oedema + Conjunctivitis), Chagoma (Inoculation Site), Fever, Lymphadenopathy, Hepatosplenomegaly, Myocarditis (Arrhythmia, Heart Failure), Meningoencephalitis (Rare)
Indeterminate (Latent)Asymptomatic, Positive Serology, Can Last Decades
Chronic (10-30 Years Later)Cardiac (Cardiomyopathy, Arrhythmias, Heart Failure, Thromboembolism, Sudden Death), GI (Megaesophagus, Megacolon), Neurological

Diagnosis

PhaseTest
AcuteMicroscopy (Blood - Motile Trypomastigotes), PCR (High Sens), Antigen Detection
ChronicSerology (IgG ELISA/IHA/IIF — Two Tests Required), PCR (Low Sensitivity in Chronic)

Treatment

PhaseRegimen
Acute / Congenital / ReactivationBenznidazole 5-7mg/kg/day BD × 60 Days (1st Line) OR Nifurtimox 8-10mg/kg/day BD × 60-90 Days
Chronic (Indeterminate/Early Cardiac)Benznidazole 5-7mg/kg/day BD × 60 Days (Benefit Controversial)
Advanced Cardiac/GISupportive (Heart Failure Rx, Pacemaker, Surgery for Megaesophagus/Colon)
[!warning] Benznidazole = Teratogenic (Contraindicated in Pregnancy). Nifurtimox = Neurotoxicity/Psychosis. Both Require 60 Days.

7. 💡 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
LeishmaniasisVL = Fever + Splenomegaly + Pancytopenia + Hypergammaglobulinaemia; CL = Self-Healing Ulcer; ML = Mucosal Destruction (Espundia)
VL DiagnosisrK39 Dipstick (Rapid), Splenic Aspirate (Gold Standard), DAT/ELISA
VL TreatmentLAmB Single Dose (India), LAmB 5d (Africa/L. infantum), Miltefosine (Contraindicated Pregnancy)
CL/MLCL = Self-Healing Ulcer (Old World) / Larger Ulcers (New World); ML = Mucosal Destruction (Espundia) = Emergency
African Trypanosomiasisgambiense = Chronic (Winterbottom's Sign); rhodesiense = Acute (Zoonotic)
Stage 1 vs 2CSF WBC ≤5 vs >5, Trypanosomes in CSF
Treatment (gambiense)Stage 1: Fexinidazole 10d / Pentamidine; Stage 2: Fexinidazole / NECT
Treatment (rhodesiense)Stage 1: Suramin; Stage 2: Melarsoprol (Toxic)
Chagas DiseaseAcute: Romaña's Sign + Myocarditis; Chronic: Cardiac (Cardiomyopathy) + GI (Megaesophagus/Colon)
Chagas TreatmentBenznidazole 60 Days (Acute/Congenital/Early Chronic); Supportive Chronic

8. ❓ Viva Questions

  1. What is the classic presentation of Visceral Leishmaniasis (Kala-azar)?
  • Fever + Massive Splenomegaly + Pancytopenia + Hypergammaglobulinaemia.
  1. What is the rapid diagnostic test for Visceral Leishmaniasis?
  • rK39 Dipstick (Sensitivity 95-98%, Specificity 90-95%).
  1. What is the first-line treatment for Visceral Leishmaniasis in the Indian Subcontinent?
  • Single Dose Liposomal Amphotericin B 10mg/kg.
  1. What is the difference between Old World and New World Cutaneous Leishmaniasis?
  • Old World (L. tropica/major): Self-Limiting, Single Ulcer; New World (L. braziliensis/mexicana): Larger, Multiple, Higher ML Risk.
  1. What is Mucocutaneous Leishmaniasis and which species causes it?
  • Mucosal Destruction (Nose/Pharynx/Larynx) = Espundia, caused by L. braziliensis (New World).
  1. What is Winterbottom's Sign and which disease is it associated with?
  • Posterior Cervical Lymphadenopathy = Pathognomonic for T. b. gambiense (African Trypanosomiasis).
  1. How do you distinguish Stage 1 from Stage 2 African Trypanosomiasis?
  • Stage 1: CSF WBC ≤5, No Trypanosomes in CSF, No Neuro Signs; Stage 2: CSF WBC >5, Trypanosomes in CSF, Neuro Signs Present.
  1. What is the treatment for Stage 2 T. b. gambiense?
  • Fexinidazole 1800mg OD × 10 Days OR NECT (Nifurtimox + Eflornithine).
  1. What is Romaña's Sign in Chagas Disease?
  • Unilateral Periorbital Oedema + Conjunctivitis at Inoculation Site (Acute Phase).
  1. What is the treatment for Acute Chagas Disease?
  • Benznidazole 5-7mg/kg/day BD × 60 Days (1st Line) or Nifurtimox 8-10mg/kg/day BD × 60-90d.
  1. What are the chronic manifestations of Chagas Disease?
  • Cardiac (Cardiomyopathy, Arrhythmias, Heart Failure, Sudden Death) + GI (Megaesophagus, Megacolon).
  1. How do you diagnose Chronic Chagas Disease?
  • Two Serological Tests Positive (ELISA + IHA/IIF) AND/OR PCR (Low Sensitivity in Chronic).

9. 🧠 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
VL vs CL vs MLVL = Systemic (Fever, Spleen, Pancytopenia); CL = Skin Ulcer; ML = Mucosal Destruction
Old World vs New World CLOld World (L. tropica/major): Self-Healing; New World (L. braziliensis): Larger, ML Risk
gambiense vs rhodesiensegambiense = Chronic, Anthroponotic, Gambia/West Africa; rhodesiense = Acute, Zoonotic, East Africa
Stage 1 vs Stage 2 HATCSF WBC ≤5 vs >5, Trypanosomes in CSF, Neuro Signs
Chagas Acute vs ChronicAcute: Romaña's Sign, Myocarditis; Chronic: Cardiac (Cardiomyopathy) + GI (Megaesophagus/Colon)
rK39 vs DATrK39 = Rapid Dipstick (Field); DAT = Quantitative Serology (Lab)
MnemonicMeaning
"Kala-Azar = Fever + Spleen + Pancytopenia + Hypergammaglobulinaemia"VL Triad
"rK39 = Rapid Test for Kala-Azar"Rapid Test
"LAmB = 1st Line VL Everywhere"Treatment
"Winterbottom = Gambiense"T.b. gambiense Sign
"Stage 1 = Blood/Lymph, Stage 2 = CNS"HAT Stages
"Fexinidazole = Oral, Both Stages (Gambiense)"Fexinidazole
"Romaña's Sign = Periorbital Oedema"Chagas Acute
"Chagas = Heart + Gut"Chronic Chagas
"Benznidazole = 60 Days"Chagas Treatment

10. 🗺️ Mind Map

mindmap

11. 📋 One-Page Revision Card

LEISHMANIASIS & TRYPANOSOMIASIS – FCPS/MRCP REVISION CARD
VL (Kala-azar): Fever + Massive Splenomegaly + Pancytopenia + Hypergammaglobulinaemia
rK39 Dipstick: Rapid, Sens 95-98%
VL Rx: LAmB 10mg/kg Single Dose (India), LAmB 5mg/kg ×5d (Africa), Miltefosine (No Preg)
CL: Old World = Self-Healing; New World = Larger, ML Risk
ML (Espundia): L. braziliensis, Mucosal Destruction → LAmB + Surgery
African Trypanosomiasis: gambiense (Chronic, Winterbottom's Sign), rhodesiense (Acute)
Stage 1: Haemo-lymphatic (CSF WBC ≤5); Stage 2: CNS (CSF WBC >5, Tryps+)
Rx: gambiense Stage 1 = Fexinidazole/Pentamidine; Stage 2 = Fexinidazole/NECT; rhodesiense Stage 1 = Suramin; Stage 2 = Melarsoprol
Chagas (T. cruzi): Acute = Romaña's Sign + Myocarditis; Chronic = Cardiac + GI (Megaesophagus/Colon)
Chagas Rx: Benznidazole 5-7mg/kg BD × 60d (Acute/Indeterminate)
Diagnosis: VL = rK39/Splenic Aspirate; HAT = CSF; Chagas = Serology (2 Tests) / PCR (Acute)

13. 🎯 Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

LevelContent
Must KnowVL Clinical Triad + rK39 + LAmB Rx, CL/ML Difference, HAT Stages + Winterbottom's Sign + Fexinidazole/NECT/Melarsoprol, Chagas Acute/Chronic + Romaña's Sign + Benznidazole, Diagnosis Methods (rK39, CSF, Serology)
Should KnowPKDL, ML Emergency Management, HIV-VL Co-infection Rx, HAT Surveillance/Control, Chagas Congenital Transmission, Benznidazole vs Nifurtimox, Vector Control Strategies, Drug Resistance, Post-Treatment Follow-up
Nice to KnowLeishmania Genomics/Species Complex, Trypanosome Antigenic Variation (VSG), Vaccine Development (Leish-F1, Leish-F3, Chagas), Drug Resistance Mechanisms, One Health Approach, Climate Change Impact on Vector Distribution, Elimination Programs (Kala-azar in India, HAT Elimination), Drug Development Pipeline (Oxaboroles, Oxadiazoles)

topics.title: Leptospirosis
topics.html:
[!info] Davidson Ch 11 Alignment: Infectious Disease → Specific Organism Groups → Spirochaetes → Leptospira
FCPS/MRCP Focus: Weil's disease triad, pulmonary haemorrhage, MAT/PCR, doxycycline/penicillin, Jarisch-Herxheimer

1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Identify Transmission: Rat urine → Contaminated water/soil → Skin/mucosal entry
  • [ ] Recognise Clinical Spectrum: Mild (Anicteric) → Severe (Weil's Disease: Jaundice, Renal Failure, Haemorrhage)
  • [ ] Diagnose: MAT (Gold Standard), PCR (Early), IgM ELISA (Early), Dark Field Microscopy (Urine)
  • [ ] Manage: Doxycycline (Mild), IV Penicillin G / Ceftriaxone (Severe), RRT for Renal Failure
  • [ ] Recognise Complications: Pulmonary Haemorrhage (Anaemic, High Mortality), AKI, Myocarditis, Uveitis
  • [ ] Apply Prophylaxis: Doxycycline 200mg Weekly for High-Risk Exposure

2. 📖 Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetails
Causative AgentLeptospira interrogans (Spirochaete); >250 Serovars (Icterohaemorrhagiae, Canicola, Grippotyphosa, Pomona)
ReservoirRats (Primary), Cattle, Pigs, Dogs, Wildlife; Asymptomatic Renal Carriage → Urine Shedding
TransmissionDirect/Indirect Contact with Infected Urine → Contaminated Water/Soil → Broken Skin/Mucosa/Conjunctiva
Risk FactorsOccupational (Farmers, Sewer Workers, Vets, Abattoir), Recreational (Freshwater Swimming, Rafting, Caving), Flooding (Outbreaks)
GeographyTropical/Subtropical (SE Asia, Caribbean, Oceania, Latin America); Temperate (Seasonal, Occupational)
Incubation5-14 Days (Range 2-30 Days)
[!tip] Leptospirosis = Rat Urine + Water Exposure + Fever + Jaundice/Renal Failure. Weil's Disease = Severe Icteric Form (5-10% of cases).

3. 📖 Clinical Spectrum

FormFeaturesProportion
Subclinical / Mild (Anicteric)Fever, Myalgia (Calf Classic), Headache, Conjunctival Suffusion, Rash~90%
Severe (Weil's Disease)Jaundice, Renal Failure, Haemorrhage (Pulmonary, GI, Adrenal), Pulmonary Haemorrhage (ARDS), Myocarditis, Aseptic Meningitis5-10%

Weil's Disease Triad (Classic)

FeaturePathophysiology
JaundiceHepatocellular Dysfunction + Haemolysis; Conjugated + Unconjugated Hyperbilirubinaemia
Renal FailureTubulointerstitial Nephritis → ATN; Non-oliguric Common; Hypokalaemia
HaemorrhageThrombocytopenia, Capillary Damage, Coagulopathy (DIC-like); Pulmonary Haemorrhage → Major Cause of Death

Pulmonary Haemorrhage (Severe Complication)

FeatureDetails
Incidence10-50% of Severe Cases; Mortality 50-70%
PresentationHaemoptysis, Dyspnoea, Hypoxaemia, Bilateral Consolidation on CXR
PathologyAlveolar Capillary Damage → Alveolar Haemorrhage; Immune-Mediated
ManagementHigh-Dose Methylprednisolone (1g IV Daily ×3), Ventilatory Support, RRT, Plasma Exchange (Refractory)

4. 🔬 Diagnostic Workup

flowchart TD
    A[Fever + Exposure History + Jaundice/Renal Failure] --> B[**Initial: CBC, LFT, Renal, Coag, CXR**]
    B --> C{**Key Findings**}
    C --> C1[**Leukocytosis + Neutrophilia**]
    C --> C2[**Thrombocytopenia** (Common)]
    C --> C3[**↑ Bilirubin (Conj+Unconj), ↑ ALT/AST (Mild)**]
    C --> C4[**↑ Creatinine, ↑ K+, Metabolic Acidosis**]
    C --> C4[**CXR: Bilateral Alveolar Infiltrates** (Pulm Haemorrhage)]
    C1 & C2 & C3 & C4 --> D[**Confirmatory Tests**]
    D --> D1[**MAT (Gold Standard)** - *Paired Sera 2-4w Apart, 4-fold Rise*]
    D --> D2[**IgM ELISA** - *Early (Day 5-7), Sensitivity 90%*]
    D3[**PCR (Blood/Urine)** - *Early (Day 1-5), High Sensitivity/Specificity*]
    D --> D4[**Dark Field Microscopy (Urine)** - *Motile Spirochaetes; Low Sensitivity*]

Key Diagnostic Tests

TestTimingSensitivity/SpecificityRole
MAT (Microscopic Agglutination Test)Convalescent (Day 10-14); Paired SeraGold Standard; Serovar IdentificationConfirmatory
IgM ELISADay 5-7 Onwards~90% Sens, 95% SpecEarly Diagnosis
PCR (Blood/Urine)Day 1-10 (Before Antibodies)High Sens/SpecEarly / Confirmatory
Dark Field MicroscopyFresh UrineLow Sensitivity (40-50%)Bedside / Rapid
Culture (Ellinghausen-McCullough-Johnson-Harris Medium)WeeksLow SensitivityRetrospective / Reference
[!tip] MAT = Gold Standard but Requires Paired Sera (Acute + Convalescent 2-4 Weeks Apart). IgM ELISA / PCR = Early Diagnosis.

5. 💊 Management

Antibiotics

SeverityRegimenDuration
Mild (Anicteric)Doxycycline 100mg PO BD7 Days
Severe (Weil's / ICU)IV Penicillin G 1.5g IV Q6H (or Ceftriaxone 2g OD IV / Cefotaxime 2g Q6H)7 Days
Penicillin AllergyDoxycycline 100mg IV/PO BD / Ceftriaxone 2g OD IV7 Days
[!tip] Doxycycline = First-Line for Mild/Outpatient; IV Penicillin/Ceftriaxone = Severe/ICU. Jarisch-Herxheimer Reaction Common (Fever, Rigors, Hypotension 2-4h Post-First Dose) — Supportive, Continue Antibiotics.

Supportive Care (Severe/Weil's)

ComplicationManagement
Renal FailureRRT (Early Initiation); K+ Management, Fluid Balance
Pulmonary HaemorrhageHigh-Dose Methylprednisolone 1g IV Daily ×3-5d; Ventilatory Support (Lung-Protective); rFVIIa / Tranexamic Acid (Refractory)
Thrombocytopenia/CoagulopathyPlatelet Transfusion (<20 or Bleeding); FFP/Cryo if DIC
MyocarditisSupportive, Inotropes if Needed
ElectrolytesHypokalaemia (Renal Tubular Dysfunction) → Aggressive K+ Replacement
[!warning] Jarisch-Herxheimer Reaction: Fever, Rigors, Hypotension, Worsening Rash 2-4h Post-First Dose — Supportive (Fluids, Vasopressors), Continue Antibiotics, Antipyretics.

6. 🔬 Laboratory Findings

ParameterTypical Findings
CBCLeukocytosis + Neutrophilia, Thrombocytopenia (Common), Anaemia (Haemolysis/Blood Loss)
LFT↑ Bilirubin (Conj+Unconj), Mild ↑ AST/ALT (Hepatocellular), ↑ ALP (Cholestatic)
Renal↑ Creatinine, ↑ Urea, Hyperkalaemia (Tubular Dysfunction), Metabolic Acidosis
CoagulationProlonged PT/APTT, ↓ Fibrinogen, ↑ D-dimer (DIC-like)
CPKMarkedly Elevated (Myositis)
CSFLymphocytic Pleocytosis, Normal/Raised Protein, Normal Glucose (Aseptic Meningitis)

7. 🔄 Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDifferentiating Features
Viral Hepatitis (A/E)No Renal Failure/Pulmonary Haemorrhage, Marked Transaminitis (>1000), Anti-HAV IgM / Anti-HEV IgM
MalariaParasitaemia on Film, No Renal Failure (Unless Severe), No Pulm Haemorrhage
Septicemia (Gram -ve)Blood Culture Positive, No Conjunctival Suffusion, No Myalgia (Calf)
Hantavirus (HFRS)Similar Syndrome (Renal Failure, Haemorrhage), Hantaan/Seoul Virus, Exposure to Rodents
Sepsis (Other)Blood Culture Positive, No Conjunctival Suffusion, No Calf Tenderness
Acute Viral Hepatitis (A/B/E)ALT/AST >> Bilirubin, No Renal Failure, Serology Positive
DengueNS1 Ag+, Thrombocytopenia, Plasma Leakage, No Renal Failure/Pulm Haemorrhage (Usually)
[!tip] Conjunctival Suffusion (Non-Purulent Conjunctival Injection) = Highly Suggestive of Leptospirosis (50-90%).

8. 🛡️ Prevention & Prophylaxis

StrategyDetails
Pre-Exposure ProphylaxisDoxycycline 200mg PO Weekly (Start 1-2 Days Before, Continue During Exposure) — High-Risk Occupations/Activities
Post-ExposureDoxycycline 100mg BD × 7 Days if High-Risk Exposure Recognised
EnvironmentalRodent Control, Drainage, Protective Clothing/Footwear, Avoid Contaminated Water
VaccinationAnimal Vaccines Available (Cattle, Dogs); Human Vaccine (Limited Serovar Coverage, Used in High-Risk Occupations in Some Countries)

9. 💡 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
TransmissionRat Urine → Contaminated Water/Soil → Skin/Mucosa
Clinical SpectrumAnicteric (90%) → Fever, Calf Myalgia, Conjunctival Suffusion → Weil's (10%) = Jaundice + Renal Failure + Haemorrhage
Weil's TriadJaundice + Renal Failure + Haemorrhage (Pulmonary Haemorrhage = Major Killer)
DiagnosisMAT (Gold Standard, Paired Sera); IgM ELISA (Early); PCR (Early, Blood/Urine)
TreatmentMild: Doxycycline 100mg BD PO × 7d; Severe: IV Penicillin G 1.5MU Q6H / Ceftriaxone 2g OD
ComplicationsPulmonary Haemorrhage (50-70% Mortality), AKI (RRT), Myocarditis, Uveitis
Jarisch-HerxheimerFever, Rigors, Hypotension 2-4h Post-First Dose — Supportive, Continue Antibiotics
ProphylaxisDoxycycline 200mg Weekly (High-Risk Exposure)

10. ❓ Viva Questions

  1. What is the classic triad of Weil's disease?
  • Jaundice + Renal Failure + Haemorrhage (Pulmonary Haemorrhage is Major Cause of Death).
  1. What is the most characteristic physical finding in Leptospirosis?
  • Conjunctival Suffusion (Non-Purulent Conjunctival Injection) — Present in 50-90%.
  1. What is the drug of choice for mild Leptospirosis?
  • Doxycycline 100mg PO BD × 7 Days.
  1. What is the drug of choice for severe Leptospirosis (Weil's Disease)?
  • IV Penicillin G 1.5MU IV Q6H (or Ceftriaxone 2g OD IV / Cefotaxime 2g Q6H IV).
  1. What is the Jarisch-Herxheimer Reaction and how is it managed?
  • Fever, Rigors, Hypotension 2-4h Post-First Dose → Supportive (Fluids, Vasopressors), Continue Antibiotics.
  1. What is the gold standard diagnostic test for Leptospirosis?
  • Microscopic Agglutination Test (MAT) on Paired Sera (Acute + Convalescent 2-4 Weeks Apart) showing 4-Fold Rise.
  1. What is the earliest diagnostic test for Leptospirosis?
  • PCR (Blood/Urine) — Detects DNA Day 1-10, Before Antibodies Appear.
  1. How is Pulmonary Haemorrhage in Leptospirosis managed?
  • High-Dose Methylprednisolone 1g IV Daily ×3-5 Days, Ventilatory Support, RRT, rFVIIa/Tranexamic Acid if Refractory.
  1. What is the Prophylaxis for High-Risk Exposure?
  • Doxycycline 200mg PO Weekly (Start 1-2 Days Before Exposure, Continue During Exposure).
  1. What is the typical CSF finding in Leptospiral Meningitis?
  • Lymphocytic Pleocytosis, Normal Glucose, Mildly Elevated Protein.
  1. Differentiate Leptospirosis from Viral Hepatitis, Malaria, and Hantavirus.
  • Lepto: Conjunctival Suffusion + Calf Myalgia + Renal Failure + Pulm Haemorrhage; Hepatitis: ALT/AST >> Bilirubin, No Renal Failure; Malaria: Parasitaemia, Cyclical Fever; Hantavirus: Similar but No Conjunctival Suffusion, Rodent Exposure, Hantaan/Seoul Serology.

11. 🧠 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Leptospirosis vs Viral HepatitisLepto: Renal Failure + Pulm Haemorrhage + Conjunctival Suffusion; Hepatitis: ALT/AST >> Bilirubin, No Renal Failure
Leptospirosis vs MalariaMalaria: Parasitaemia on Film, Cyclical Fever, No Conjunctival Suffusion, No Pulm Haemorrhage
Leptospirosis vs Hantavirus (HFRS)Both: Renal Failure + Haemorrhage; Hanta: No Conjunctival Suffusion, Hantaan/Seoul Serology, Rodent Exposure
Jarisch-Herxheimer vs Allergic ReactionJH: 2-4h Post-Abx, Fever/Rigors/Hypotension, Self-Limiting; Allergy: Immediate, Urticaria, Angioedema, Bronchospasm
Doxycycline vs PenicillinDoxy: Mild/Outpatient, PO; Penicillin/Ceftriaxone: Severe/ICU, IV
MnemonicMeaning
"Lepto = Rat Urine + Water + Weil's Triad"Transmission + Severe Form
"Weil's = Jaundice + Kidney + Blood"Triad
"Conjunctival Suffusion = Lepto Hallmark"Physical Sign
"Doxy for Mild, Pen for Severe"Treatment Algorithm
"JH Reaction = Fluids + Continue Abx"Jarisch-Herxheimer

12. 🗺️ Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Leptospirosis))
    Transmission
      Rat Urine → Water/Soil
      Skin/Mucosa Entry
      Floods/Recreation/Occupation
    Clinical Spectrum
      Anicteric (90%): Fever, Myalgia (Calf), Conjunctival Suffusion
      Weil's (10%): Jaundice + Renal Failure + Haemorrhage
    Weil's Triad

      Renal Failure
      Haemorrhage (Pulmonary = Killer)
    Diagnosis

      Uveitis (Late)

13. 📋 One-Page Revision Card

LEPTOSPIROSIS – FCPS/MRCP REVISION CARD
Transmission: Rat Urine → Water/Soil → Skin/Mucosa
Spectrum: Anicteric 90% (Fever, Calf Myalgia, Conjunctival Suffusion) → Weil's 10% (Jaundice + Renal Failure + Haemorrhage)
Weil's Triad: Jaundice + Renal Failure + Haemorrhage (Pulmonary Haemorrhage = Killer)
Diagnosis: MAT (Gold, Paired Sera), IgM ELISA (Early), PCR (Early, Blood/Urine)
Conjunctival Suffusion = Hallmark (50-90%)
Treatment: Mild = Doxy 100mg BD PO ×7d; Severe = Penicillin G IV / Ceftriaxone 2g OD IV
Jarisch-Herxheimer: 2-4h Post-Dose, Fever/Rigors/Hypotension → Supportive, Continue Abx

Prophylaxis: Doxycycline 200mg Weekly (High-Risk Exposure)

15. 🎯 Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

topics.title: Lyme Disease
topics.html:
[!info] Davidson Ch 11 Alignment: Infectious Disease → Specific Organism Groups → Spirochaetes → Borrelia burgdorferi sensu lato
FCPS/MRCP Focus: Erythema migrans, stages, two-tier serology, CSF analysis, doxycycline/ceftriaxone, post-treatment Lyme syndrome, Lyme carditis/neuroborreliosis

1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Identify Transmission: Ixodes Tick Bite (Nymph → Larva → Adult), B. burgdorferi sensu lato (B. burgdorferi s.s., B. afzelii, B. garinii, B. bavariensis)
  • [ ] Recognise Stage 1 (Early Localised): Erythema Migrans (EM) — Expanding Annular Rash, "Bull's Eye"
  • [ ] Recognise Stage 2 (Early Disseminated): Multiple EM, Neuroborreliosis (Radiculoneuritis, Facial Palsy, Meningitis), Lyme Carditis (AV Block)
  • [ ] Recognise Stage 3 (Late Disseminated): Lyme Arthritis (Large Joint, Knee), Acrodermatitis Chronica Atrophicans (ACA), Encephalomyelitis
  • [ ] Diagnose: Clinical (EM = Clinical Diagnosis), Two-Tier Serology (ELISA → Western Blot), CSF Analysis (Neuroborreliosis), PCR (Joint Fluid/CSF)
  • [ ] Manage: Doxycycline (Early/Non-CNS), Ceftriaxone IV (CNS/Carditis/Arthritis), Post-Treatment Lyme Syndrome (Symptomatic Management)

2. 📖 Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetails
Causative AgentBorrelia burgdorferi sensu lato Complex: B. burgdorferi s.s. (USA), B. afzelii (Europe/Asia), B. garinii (Europe/Asia), B. bavariensis
VectorIxodes Ticks: I. scapularis (NE USA), I. pacificus (West USA), I. ricinus (Europe), I. persulcatus (Asia)
ReservoirSmall Mammals (White-Footed Mouse, Rodents), Birds, Deer (Amplification Host)
TransmissionIxodes Tick Bite (Nymph = Primary Vector, Spring/Summer); Attachment ≥36-48h for Transmission
GeographyEndemic: NE/Mid-Atlantic/Upper Midwest USA, Europe (Scandinavia, Germany, Austria, Eastern Europe), Asia
Incubation3-30 Days (Median 7-10 Days) for EM; Weeks-Months for Disseminated
SeasonalitySpring/Summer (Nymphal Activity Peak May-July)
[!tip] Lyme = Tick Bite + Erythema Migrans + Endemic Area. Transmission Risk >36-48h Attachment. Not Transmitted Person-to-Person.

3. 📖 Clinical Stages

Stage 1: Early Localised (Days-Weeks)

FeatureDetails
Erythema Migrans (EM)Pathognomonic — Expanding Annular Erythematous Rash (≥5cm), Central Clearing ("Bull's Eye") in 20-30%, Expands ~1-2cm/Day, Non-Pruritic, Non-Painful
LocationBite Site (Often Groin, Axilla, Popliteal Fossa, Trunk, Behind Ear)
Systemic SymptomsLow-Grade Fever, Fatigue, Myalgia, Arthralgia, Headache, Lymphadenopathy (Regional)
DiagnosisClinical (EM in Endemic Area + Exposure) — No Serology Needed
[!tip] EM = Clinical Diagnosis. Do Not Order Serology for Solitary EM (False Negative Early). Treat Empirically.

Stage 2: Early Disseminated (Weeks-Months)

ManifestationFrequencyKey Features
Multiple EM Lesions20-50%Disseminated Haematogenous Spread
Neuroborreliosis10-15%Radiculoneuritis (Radicular Pain, Paresis), Facial Nerve Palsy (Bilateral ~25%), Lymphocytic Meningitis (Headache, Neck Stiffness, Photophobia)
Lyme Carditis1-5%AV Block (1st→2nd→3rd Degree), Myopericarditis, Palpitations, Syncope, ECG: PR Prolongation, AV Block
MusculoskeletalCommonMigratory Arthralgia/Myalgia, Transient Joint Swelling
OcularRareConjunctivitis, Uveitis, Keratitis, Optic Neuritis
Borrelial LymphocytomaRare (Europe)Bluish-Red Nodule (Earlobe, Nipple, Scrotum) — B. afzelii

Stage 3: Late Disseminated (Months-Years)

ManifestationFeatures
Lyme ArthritisLarge Joint (Knee 90%), Recurrent/Oligoarticular, Effusion, Minimal Constitutional Symptoms, PCR+ in Synovial Fluid
Acrodermatitis Chronica Atrophicans (ACA)Late B. afzelii Infection, Progressive Atrophic Skin Changes (Distal Extremities), Peripheral Neuropathy, Fibrosis
EncephalomyelitisRare, Cognitive Impairment, Spastic Paresis, Ataxia, CSF: Lymphocytic Pleocytosis, Intrathecal Antibody

4. 🔬 Diagnostic Workup

flowchart TD
    A[Suspected Lyme Disease] --> B{**Clinical Presentation**}
    B -->|**Solitary EM**| C[**Clinical Diagnosis** → **Treat Empirically** (No Serology)]
    B -->|**Disseminated / Late**| D[**Serology: Two-Tier Testing**]
    D --> E1[**Step 1: ELISA / EIA** (Screening — High Sensitivity)]
    E1 --> F{**Positive/Equivocal?**}
    F -->|Yes| G[**Step 2: Western Blot** (Confirmatory — Specificity)]
    F -->|No| H[**Negative** — Consider Other Dx / Early Infection]
    G --> I[**Interpretation**: IgM ≥2/3 Bands (Early), IgG ≥5/10 Bands (Late)]
    I --> J[**Positive Two-Tier = Confirmed**]
    B -->|**Neuroborreliosis Suspected**| K[**LP: CSF Analysis (Cell Count, Protein, Glucose, Intrathecal Ab Index)**]
    B -->|**Lyme Arthritis**| L[**Joint Aspiration: PCR (High Sens), Culture (Low)**]
    B -->|**Lyme Carditis**| M[**ECG (AV Block), Echo, Troponin**]

Serology — Two-Tier Testing (CDC/IDSA)

StepTestInterpretation
Step 1ELISA / EIA / CIA (Whole-Cell Sonicate or Peptide)Negative = No Further Testing (Unless Early Infection — Repeat 2-4 Weeks)

| Step 2 | Western Blot (IgM + IgG) | IgM Positive: ≥2 of 3 Specific Bands (23, 39, 41 kDa) — Early (<4 Weeks)

IgG Positive: ≥5 of 10 Specific Bands (18, 21, 28, 30, 39, 41, 45, 58, 66, 93 kDa) — Late (>4 Weeks) |

[!warning] IgM Western Blot Only Valid <4 Weeks. Do Not Use IgM Alone for Late Disease (False Positive). IgG Western Blot Required for Late Disease.

CSF Analysis (Neuroborreliosis)

ParameterNeuroborreliosis
Opening PressureNormal / Mildly Elevated
WBCLymphocytic Pleocytosis (50-500/µL)
ProteinElevated (50-200 mg/dL)
GlucoseNormal / Mildly Low
Intrathecal Antibody IndexAI = (CSF Ab/Serum Ab) / (CSF Albumin/Serum Albumin) >1.5 (Positive)
CSF PCRLow Sensitivity (~10-30%)
Oligoclonal BandsOften Present (Not Specific)

5. 💊 Management

Antibiotic Regimens (Adults)

Clinical Syndrome1st LineAlternative (Penicillin Allergy)Duration
Erythema Migrans (Early Localised)Doxycycline 100mg PO BDAmoxicillin 500mg TDS / Cefuroxime Axetil 500mg BD10-14 Days
Early Disseminated (No CNS/Carditis)Doxycycline 100mg BD POAmoxicillin 500mg TDS PO / Cefuroxime 500mg BD PO14-21 Days
Neuroborreliosis (Meningitis/Radiculitis/Facial Palsy)Ceftriaxone 2g IV OD (or Cefotaxime 2g Q6H)Penicillin G 4MU IV Q4H14-21 Days
Lyme Carditis (AV Block)Ceftriaxone 2g IV OD (Monitored Setting)Penicillin G 4MU IV Q4H14-21 Days (IV → PO Switch When Stable)
Lyme ArthritisDoxycycline 100mg BD PO (28 Days)Amoxicillin 500mg TDS PO / Ceftriaxone 2g IV OD (If Refractory)28 Days
Acrodermatitis Chronica AtrophicansDoxycycline 100mg BD PO / Amoxicillin 500mg TDSCeftriaxone 2g IV OD21-28 Days
[!warning] Doxycycline Contraindicated: Pregnancy, Children <8 Years — Use Amoxicillin / Cefuroxime / Ceftriaxone.
[!tip] No Proven Benefit of Extended Therapy (>28 Days for Arthritis). Post-Treatment Lyme Syndrome (PTLS) = Persistent Symptoms >6 Months Post-Treatment — Symptomatic Management, No Further Antibiotics.

6. 🔬 Diagnostic Algorithms

Erythema Migrans → Clinical Diagnosis → Treat Empirically (No Serology)

Disseminated Disease → Two-Tier Serology (ELISA → Western Blot)

Neuroborreliosis → LP + CSF Analysis + Intrathecal Antibody Index

Lyme Arthritis → Joint Aspiration (PCR High Sens, Culture Low Sens) + Serology

Lyme Carditis → ECG (AV Block), Troponin, Echo → Admit, Monitor, Ceftriaxone IV

7. 🔬 Laboratory Findings

TestFindings
CBCNormal / Mild Leukocytosis / Eosinophilia (Rare)
ESR/CRPElevated (Acute Phase)
LFT/RenalNormal
ECGPR Prolongation / AV Block (Carditis)
Synovial Fluid (Arthritis)Inflammatory (WBC 25,000-100,000, Neutrophilic), PCR Positive, Culture Rarely Positive
CSFLymphocytic Pleocytosis, Elevated Protein, Normal Glucose, Intrathecal Ab Index >1.5
SerologyTwo-Tier: ELISA → Western Blot (IgM <4wks, IgG >4wks)

8. 🔄 Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDifferentiating Features
Southern Tick-Associated Rash Illness (STARI)EM-like Rash, Lone Star Tick (Amblyomma americanum), No B. burgdorferi, Milder, Self-Limited
CellulitisRapidly Spreading, Hot/Tender, No Central Clearing, No Tick Bite History
Erythema MultiformeTarget Lesions, Mucosal Involvement, Drug/Infection Trigger
Fibromyalgia / CFSChronic Widespread Pain, No Objective Inflammatory Signs, Negative Serology
Rheumatoid ArthritisSymmetrical Polyarthritis, RF/CCP+, Morning Stiffness >1h
Viral MeningitisAcute Onset, CSF Lymphocytic, Negative Lyme Serology/PCR
Bell's Palsy (Idiopathic)Unilateral, No Other Neurological Signs, Negative Lyme Serology

9. 💡 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
TransmissionIxodes Tick Bite, ≥36-48h Attachment for Transmission; Nymph = Primary Vector
Stage 1 (EM)Clinical Diagnosis, No Serology, Doxycycline 100mg BD ×10-14d
Stage 2 (Disseminated)Multiple EM, Neuroborreliosis (Radiculitis, Facial Palsy, Meningitis), Carditis (AV Block)
Stage 3 (Late)Arthritis (Knee), ACA (B. afzelii), Encephalomyelitis
SerologyTwo-Tier: ELISA → Western Blot; IgM <4wks, IgG >4wks; IgM Alone Not Diagnostic for Late Disease
NeuroborreliosisCSF: Lymphocytic Pleocytosis, ↑Protein, Normal Glucose, Intrathecal Ab Index >1.5
CarditisAV Block (1st→3rd Degree), Ceftriaxone IV, Monitor in ICU
ArthritisKnee (90%), PCR+ Synovial Fluid, Doxy 28d / Ceftriaxone IV if Refractory
TreatmentDoxycycline 100mg BD PO (Early/Disseminated, Non-CNS); Ceftriaxone 2g IV OD (CNS/Carditis/Refractory Arthritis)
Post-Treatment Lyme SyndromeSymptoms >6mo Post-Tx, No Active Infection, No Benefit from Further Abx

10. ❓ Viva Questions

  1. What is the typical appearance of Erythema Migrans?
  • Expanding Annular Erythematous Rash (≥5cm), Central Clearing ("Bull's Eye") in 20-30%, Expands 1-2cm/Day, Non-Pruritic/Non-Painful.
  1. When is serology indicated in Lyme disease?
  • Not for Solitary EM (Clinical Diagnosis). Indicated for Disseminated/Late Disease (Multiple EM, Neuro/Cardiac/Arthritis).
  1. Describe the Two-Tier Serology for Lyme disease.
  • Step 1: ELISA/EIA (Sensitive Screening) → If Positive/Equivocal → Step 2: Western Blot; IgM ≥2/3 Bands (Early <4wks); IgG ≥5/10 Bands (Late >4wks).
  1. Why is IgM Western Blot not used for late Lyme disease?
  • IgM Persists/False Positives; False Positive Rate High in Late Disease; IgG Western Blot Standard for Late Disease.
  1. What is the treatment for Erythema Migrans?
  • Doxycycline 100mg PO BD × 10-14 Days; Alternative: Amoxicillin 500mg TDS / Cefuroxime 500mg BD.
  1. How do you treat Neuroborreliosis?
  • Ceftriaxone 2g IV OD × 14-21 Days (or Penicillin G 4MU IV Q4H).
  1. What are the ECG findings in Lyme Carditis?
  • PR Prolongation → 1st Degree AV Block → 2nd Degree (Mobitz I/II) → 3rd Degree (Complete AV Block); Monitor in ICU.
  1. What is the treatment for Lyme Arthritis?
  • Doxycycline 100mg BD PO × 28 Days (1st Line); Refractory: Ceftriaxone 2g IV OD × 2-4 Weeks.
  1. What is Post-Treatment Lyme Syndrome (PTLS)?
  • Persistent Symptoms (Fatigue, Pain, Cognitive) >6 Months Post-Treatment — No Active Infection, No Benefit from Further Antibiotics, Symptomatic Management.
  1. How do you diagnose Lyme Carditis?
  • Clinical (AV Block in Endemic Area + Exposure) + ECG (AV Block) + Positive Serology → Admit, Continuous ECG Monitoring, Ceftriaxone IV.

11. 🧠 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Lyme vs STARISTARI = Lone Star Tick (Amblyomma), Southern US, Milder, No B. burgdorferi, Self-Limited
EM vs CellulitisEM = Expanding, Central Clearing, Non-Tender, No Heat/Purulence; Cellulitis = Spreading, Hot, Tender, Purulent
IgM vs IgG Western BlotIgM: Early (<4wks), ≥2 Bands; IgG: Late (>4wks), ≥5 Bands; IgM Alone Not Diagnostic Late
Lyme vs Viral MeningitisLymphocytic CSF Both; Lyme = Intrathecal Ab+, Endemic Exposure, Radiculitis/Facial Palsy
Lyme Carditis vs Heart BlockLyme = Young, Endemic Exposure, Lyme Serology+; Other = Ischaemic, Drug, Idiopathic
MnemonicMeaning
"Lyme = Tick (Ixodes) + Bull's Eye Rash"Transmission + EM
"Stages: 1=EM, 2=Disseminated (Neuro/Cardiac), 3=Late (Arthritis/ACA)"Stages
"Doxy for Early, Ceftriaxone for CNS/Heart/Arthritis"Treatment
"Western Blot: IgM 2/3 Bands (<4wks), IgG 5/10 Bands (>4wks)"Serology Interpretation
"Carditis = AV Block → Ceftriaxone IV + ICU Monitoring"Lyme Carditis
"PTLS = >6mo Post-Tx Symptoms, No Active Infection, No More Abx"PTLS

12. 🗺️ Mind Map

mindmap

13. 📋 One-Page Revision Card

topics.title: Malaria
topics.html:
Related: [[Fever and Septic Syndrome Approach]], [[Sepsis and Septic Shock]], [[Fever in the Returned Traveller]], [[Antimicrobial Prescribing Principles]], [[Tuberculosis (Pulmonary and Extrapulmonary)]]

[!important]
Malaria = protozoal infection by Plasmodium spp. Medical emergency — P. falciparum can kill within 24–48h. Diagnose with thick/thin blood films + RDT; treat urgently with ACT (artemisinin-based combination therapy). Severe malaria = parenteral artesunate.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Identify clinical features of uncomplicated and severe malaria
  • Differentiate Plasmodium species by blood film and clinical features
  • Apply diagnostic algorithms (microscopy + RDT)
  • Treat uncomplicated and severe malaria per WHO/UK guidelines
  • Manage chemoprophylaxis and special populations (pregnancy, children)

2. Plasmodium Species

SpeciesGeographySeverityKey Features
P. falciparumAfrica, SE Asia, S America, HaitiMost severe; cerebral, severe anaemia, acidosis, renalRing forms only in peripheral blood; sequestration in deep vessels
P. vivaxAsia, Latin America, Horn of AfricaRelapsing (hypnozoites); rarely severeSchüffner's dots; larger rings; amoeboid trophozoites
P. ovaleWest Africa, PacificRelapsing (hypnozoites); milderSchüffner's dots; oval-infected RBC; fimbriated
P. malariaeWorldwide (patchy)Chronic; quartan fever (72h); nephrotic syndromeBand forms; rosettes; no hypnozoites
P. knowlesiSE Asia (Borneo)Zoonotic (macaques); can be severe/fatalAll stages in peripheral blood; daily fever (24h cycle)

3. Clinical Features

Uncomplicated Malaria

FeatureDescription
FeverParoxysmal (tertian 48h: vivax/ovale/falciparum; quartan 72h: malariae) or continuous
RigorsCold phase → hot phase → sweating
Headache, myalgia, arthralgiaCommon
Nausea, vomiting, diarrhoeaFrequent
AnaemiaHaemolysis + suppressed erythropoiesis
SplenomegalyCommon (palpable by day 7)
Mild jaundiceHaemolysis + hepatic dysfunction

Severe Malaria (WHO Criteria) — P. falciparum predominantly

CriterionThreshold
Impaired consciousness / cerebral malariaGCS <11 (adults) / Blantyre <3 (children)
Severe anaemiaHb <70 g/L (or <50 in children) + parasites
AcidosisBicarbonate <15 mmol/L or lactate ≥5 mmol/L
HypoglycaemiaBlood glucose <2.2 mmol/L
Renal impairmentCreatinine >265 µmol/L (or >3 mg/dL)
Pulmonary oedema / ARDSRadiological + hypoxaemia
Significant bleeding / DICPlatelets <50, PT prolonged
ShockSBP <80 (adults) / <50 (children)
Parasitaemia>10% (or >250,000/µL in low transmission)
Hyperparasitaemia>20% (>500,000/µL)
Cerebral malaria = unrousable coma + falciparum + no other cause
Blackwater fever = massive intravascular haemolysis + haemoglobinuria + renal failure (falciparum + G6PD deficiency + oxidant drugs)

4. Diagnosis

Gold Standard: Microscopy (Thick + Thin Blood Films)

FilmPurpose
Thick filmScreening (concentrated, 20–30x sensitivity); detect low parasitaemia; count parasitaemia
Thin filmSpecies identification (morphology); quantify % parasitaemia
3 negative films 12–24h apart to exclude malaria (if high suspicion, continue)

Rapid Diagnostic Tests (RDTs)

TargetSensitivitySpecificityNotes
HRP2 (falciparum-specific)High (>95%)HighPersists weeks post-treatment (false +)
pLDH (pan-specific or species-specific)HighHighClears rapidly post-treatment
Aldolase (pan-specific)ModerateHighLess common
RDT + microscopy = ideal; RDT negative + high suspicion → repeat microscopy

Parasitaemia Quantification

MethodFormula
Thin film% parasitised RBCs = (parasitised RBCs / total RBCs) × 100
Thick filmParasites/µL = (parasites counted / WBC counted) × patient WBC count

5. Treatment

Uncomplicated P. falciparum (WHO/UK Guidelines)

RegimenDoseDurationNotes
Artemether-Lumefantrine (AL)20/120mg: 4 tabs BD (weight-based)3 days1st-line UK/WHO; take with fat
Dihydroartemisinin-Piperaquine (DHA-PPQ)40/320mg: 3 tabs OD3 daysAlternative; long half-life (piperaquine)
Artesunate-Amodiaquine (AS-AQ)Weight-based3 daysAfrica preferred; not UK 1st-line
Artesunate-Mefloquine (AS-MQ)Weight-based3 daysSE Asia; neuropsychiatric AE (mefloquine)
ACT = Artemisinin + Partner drug (artemisinin rapidly reduces biomass; partner eliminates remainder)
Take with fatty food (lumefantrine, piperaquine absorption ↑)
Avoid artemisinin monotherapy (resistance)

Uncomplicated Non-Falciparum (P. vivax, P. ovale, P. malariae)

SpeciesAcute TreatmentRadical Cure (Hypnozoites)
P. vivax / P. ovaleChloroquine (if sensitive) OR ACT (if resistant)Primaquine 15mg OD × 14d (G6PD test mandatory!)
P. malariaeChloroquine (usually sensitive)Not needed (no hypnozoites)
P. knowlesiACT (artemether-lumefantrine)Primaquine not needed (no hypnozoites)
Primaquine: G6PD testing MANDATORY (haemolysis risk); 15mg OD × 14d (adults); 0.5mg/kg/d in children
G6PD deficiency: Weekly primaquine 0.75mg/kg × 8w (supervised)

Chloroquine Dose (if sensitive)

DrugDose
Chloroquine base10mg/kg stat → 5mg/kg at 6h, 24h, 48h (total 25mg/kg over 3 days)

Severe Malaria (P. falciparum) — Medical Emergency

DrugDoseRouteNotes
Artesunate2.4mg/kg IV/IM at 0, 12, 24h then dailyIV preferred1st-line WHO/UK; continue 24h after clinical improvement → switch oral ACT × 3d total
Quinine (if artesunate unavailable)20mg/kg loading IV over 4h → 10mg/kg q8hIVCardiotoxicity (QT); monitor ECG, glucose; switch oral when improving
Artesunate superior to quinine (↓ mortality 22–35%, fewer side effects)
Parenteral for min 24h → switch to full oral ACT course

Adjunctive Management of Severe Malaria

ComplicationManagement
Cerebral malariaAirways, anticonvulsants (levetiracetam), mannitol if raised ICP, avoid steroids
Severe anaemiaTransfuse Hb <70 (or <50 children); avoid overload
AcidosisBicarbonate if pH <7.1; ventilatory support
Renal failureFluid balance, RRT if indicated
Hypoglycaemia10% dextrose bolus + infusion; monitor glucose q4–6h
Pulmonary oedemaFluid restriction, diuretics, CPAP/ventilation
DICFFP, platelets, cryoprecipitate

6. Chemoprophylaxis (Travellers)

DrugDoseStart/StopIndications
Atovaquone-Proguanil (Malarone)250/100mg OD1–2d before → 7d after1st-line (well-tolerated, short post-travel)
Doxycycline100mg OD1–2d before → 4w afterCheap; photosensitivity, GI, oesophagitis
Mefloquine250mg weekly2–3w before → 4w afterNeuropsychiatric AE (contraindicated if hx); long half-life
Chloroquine + ProguanilChloroquine weekly + Proguanil daily1w before → 4w afterLimited areas (chloroquine-sensitive only)
No prophylaxis 100% effective — bite avoidance essential (DEET, nets, clothing)
Standby emergency treatment (SBET): Carry AL for self-treatment if medical care >24h away

7. Malaria in Special Populations

Pregnancy

AspectRecommendation
Risk↑ severity (falciparum); ↑ maternal/fetal mortality; placental malaria → LBW, stillbirth
Treatment (uncomplicated)2nd/3rd tri: ACT (AL preferred); 1st tri: Quinine + Clindamycin (artemisinin safety uncertain)
Treatment (severe)Artesunate IV all trimesters (life-saving)
ProphylaxisMefloquine (2nd/3rd tri); Atovaquone-Proguanil (2nd/3rd); Doxycycline CONTRAINDICATED; Chloroquine + Proguanil + Folic acid

Children

AspectRecommendation
Weight-based dosingEssential (artesunate 2.4mg/kg AL weight-based)
Severe malariaArtesunate IV 2.4mg/kg (same as adults); rectal artesunate if IV unavailable
PrimaquineG6PD test mandatory; 0.5mg/kg/d × 14d

HIV Co-infection

AspectRecommendation
Severity↑ parasitaemia, ↑ severe malaria if CD4 low
TreatmentSame ACT (check interactions: efavirenz ↓ lumefantrine; DTG + AL OK)
ProphylaxisAtovaquone-proguanil OK with ART; Mefloquine + EFV = ↓ mefloquine levels

8. UK Imported Malaria Guidelines (Key Points)

  • All fever in returned traveller from endemic area = malaria until proven otherwise
  • 3 blood films 12–24h apart before excluding
  • RDT + microscopy = standard
  • Artesunate IV = 1st-line severe malaria
  • AL (artemether-lumefantrine) = 1st-line uncomplicated falciparum
  • Primaquine 15mg OD × 14d for vivax/ovale (G6PD test!)
  • Notify Public Health (statutory)

9. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Falciparum = cerebral, severe anaemia, acidosis, renal; ring forms only
  • Vivax/Ovale = relapsing (hypnozoites), primaquine 14d (G6PD test!)
  • Severe malaria = artesunate IV 2.4mg/kg (not quinine)
  • Uncomplicated falciparum = AL (artemether-lumefantrine) 3d
  • Chemoprophylaxis: Atovaquone-proguanil 1st-line; doxycycline; mefloquine (neuropsychiatric)
  • Cerebral malaria = GCS <11 + falciparum; artesunate + supportive
  • Blackwater fever = massive haemolysis + Hburia + renal failure (G6PD + falciparum)
  • P. knowlesi = daily fever (24h), SE Asia, can be severe

10. Common Viva Questions

  1. What is the first-line treatment for severe falciparum malaria?
  2. How do you differentiate P. falciparum from P. vivax on blood film?
  3. When do you give primaquine and what is the essential test before giving it?
  4. What is the treatment for uncomplicated falciparum malaria?
  5. Which antimalarials are safe in pregnancy?

11. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

  • Quinine no longer 1st-line for severe malaria — artesunate IV is superior
  • Primaquine = G6PD test mandatory (haemolysis)
  • Artesunate IV > quinine (mortality benefit)
  • Cerebral malaria = treat with artesunate, NOT steroids
  • Chloroquine resistance = widespread (P. falciparum, P. vivax in many areas)
  • RDT HRP2 persists weeks post-treatment = false positive
  • RDT negative ≠ no malaria if high suspicion → microscopy
  • ACT = artemisinin + partner (never use artemisinin alone)

12. Mnemonics

  • Severe malaria criteria: Impaired consciousness, Severe anaemia, Acidosis, Hypoglycaemia, Renal, Pulmonary, Bleeding, Shock, Parasitaemia = ISAHRPBSP
  • Falciparum blood film: Ring forms Only = RO
  • Vivax/Ovale: Hypnozoites → Primaquine 14d
  • Severe malaria Rx: Artesunate IV = AI
  • Primaquine: G6PD test mandatory
  • Pregnancy malaria: ACT (2/3 tri), Quinine (1st tri), Artesunate IV (severe)

13. One-Page Revision Summary

Malaria = Plasmodium spp.

  • Falciparum = ring forms only, cerebral, severe; Artesunate IV severe; AL uncomplicated
  • Vivax/Ovale = relapsing (hypnozoites), Primaquine 14d (G6PD!)
  • Severe = artesunate IV 2.4mg/kg (not quinine)
  • Uncomplicated = AL 3d (take with fat)
  • Prophylaxis: Atovaquone-proguanil 1st-line; doxycycline; mefloquine (neuropsychiatric)
  • Pregnancy: 2/3 tri ACT; severe = artesunate IV; 1st tri quinine+clinda
  • Cerebral = artesunate IV + supportive; no steroids
  • Primaquine = G6PD test mandatory!
  • Chemoprophylaxis: Atovaquone-proguanil 1st-line

14. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • Falciparum vs vivax/ovale blood film differences
  • Severe malaria criteria (WHO)
  • Artesunate IV 1st-line severe; AL 1st-line uncomplicated
  • Primaquine for vivax/ovale (G6PD test!)
  • Pregnancy treatment differences
  • Chemoprophylaxis options

Should Know

  • P. knowlesi features
  • Blackwater fever
  • Antimalarial resistance patterns
  • Supportive care of severe malaria
  • UK imported malaria guidelines

Nice to Know

  • Artemisinin resistance (kelch13 mutations)
  • Malaria vaccine (RTS,S/AS01)
  • Transmission-blocking drugs
  • Host genetics (sickle cell, thalassaemia, G6PD, Duffy)
  • Economic burden and elimination strategies

15. MCQs (10)

  1. First-line treatment for severe falciparum malaria:

B. Artesunate IV 2.4mg/kg

C. Artemether-lumefantrine

D. Chloroquine

  1. Radical cure for P. vivax / P. ovale:

B. Primaquine 15mg OD × 14d (G6PD test mandatory)

D. Mefloquine

  1. Mandatory test before primaquine:

A. HIV

B. G6PD

C. Hepatitis B

D. Creatinine

  1. First-line uncomplicated P. falciparum in UK:

A. Quinine

B. Artemether-lumefantrine (AL)

C. Mefloquine

D. Doxycycline

  1. Cerebral malaria definition:

A. Any coma with malaria

B. GCS <11 (adults) + falciparum + no other cause

C. Seizures + malaria

  1. P. vivax blood film features:

A. Ring forms only

B. Schüffner's dots, larger rings, amoeboid trophozoites

C. Band forms only

D. Rosettes

  1. Primaquine contraindication:

A. Pregnancy

B. G6PD deficiency

C. Renal failure

D. Hepatic failure

  1. Chemoprophylaxis — 1st-line for most travellers:

B. Atovaquone-proguanil

  1. Artesunate vs quinine in severe malaria:

16. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 30-year-old returned from Nigeria 10 days ago, fever 39°C, GCS 10, Hb 65, lactate 5. Blood film: ring forms only, 15% parasitaemia. Immediate management:

A. IV Quinine loading

B. IV Artesunate 2.4mg/kg + ICU supportive care

C. Oral artemether-lumefantrine

D. IM Artemether

  1. A 25-year-old returned from India 3 weeks ago, fever, chills 48h cycle. Blood film: Schüffner's dots, amoeboid trophozoites. Species and treatment:

A. Falciparum — AL

B. Vivax — ACT (or chloroquine if sensitive) + Primaquine 14d

C. Ovale — Chloroquine only

D. Malariae — Chloroquine only

  1. A 40-year-old with P. vivax, G6PD deficient (mild). Radical cure:

A. Primaquine 15mg OD × 14d

B. Primaquine 0.75mg/kg weekly × 8w (supervised)

C. No primaquine needed

C. Tafenoquine single dose

  1. A 28-week pregnant woman, returned from Ghana, uncomplicated P. falciparum. Best treatment:

A. Artemether-lumefantrine

B. Quinine + Clindamycin (1st trimester)

C. Artesunate IV

D. Mefloquine

  1. A 30-year-old at 32 weeks pregnant, severe falciparum malaria (GCS 9). Treatment:

A. Quinine IV

B. Artesunate IV (all trimesters if severe)

C. Artemether-lumefantrine

D. Mefloquine

  1. A traveller to Kenya for 2 weeks. Best chemoprophylaxis:

A. Mefloquine (cheap)

B. Atovaquone-proguanil (well-tolerated, short post-travel)

C. Chloroquine

D. No prophylaxis needed

  1. A 5-year-old child, returned from Uganda, severe malaria, weight 18kg. Artesunate dose:

A. 2.4mg/kg IV

B. 2.4mg/kg IV (same as adult)

C. 1.2mg/kg IV

D. 4.8mg/kg IV

  1. A 35-year-old returned from Papua New Guinea, daily fever 24h cycle, all stages on blood film, severe. Species:

A. Falciparum

B. Knowlesi (daily fever, all stages, SE Asia)

C. Vivax

C. Malariae

  1. A patient on doxycycline prophylaxis for malaria, returns with fever, confirmed P. falciparum. Treatment:

A. Continue doxycycline

B. AL (artemether-lumefantrine) (avoid doxycycline as treatment if used for prophylaxis)

C. Quinine + doxycycline

D. Artesunate IV

  1. P. malariae — characteristic feature:

A. Daily fever

B. Quartan fever (72h cycle)

C. Relapsing (hypnozoites)

D. Cerebral involvement common

  1. Blackwater fever — association:

A. P. vivax + G6PD deficiency

B. P. falciparum + G6PD deficiency + oxidant drugs

C. P. ovale + sulphur drugs

C. P. knowlesi + quinine

17. Flashcards

  • Q: Severe falciparum Rx

A: Artesunate IV 2.4mg/kg

  • Q: Falciparum blood film

A: Ring forms only

  • Q: Vivax/Ovale radical cure

A: Primaquine 15mg OD × 14d (G6PD test!)

  • Q: Primaquine prerequisite

A: G6PD test mandatory

  • Q: Cerebral malaria

A: GCS <11 + falciparum + no other cause

  • Q: Uncomplicated falciparum

A: Artemether-lumefantrine 3d (with fat)

  • Q: Prophylaxis 1st-line

A: Atovaquone-proguanil

  • Q: Pregnancy uncomplicated

A: ACT (2/3 tri); Quinine+clinda (1st tri)

  • Q: Severe pregnancy

A: Artesunate IV all trimesters

  • Q: Cerebral malaria supportive

A: Artesunate + anticonvulsants + mannitol; NO steroids

18. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQ 1: B — Artesunate IV 2.4mg/kg = 1st-line severe malaria.

MCQ 2: B — Falciparum = ring forms only (sequestration).

MCQ 3: B — Primaquine 15mg OD × 14d for hypnozoites.

MCQ 4: B — G6PD test mandatory (haemolysis risk).

MCQ 5: B — AL = 1st-line UK/WHO.

MCQ 6: B — GCS <11 + falciparum = cerebral.

MCQ 7: B — Vivax = Schüffner's dots, amoeboid.

MCQ 8: B — G6PD deficiency = primaquine contraindicated.

MCQ 9: B — Atovaquone-proguanil 1st-line prophylaxis.

MCQ 10: B — Artesunate ↓ mortality 22–35% vs quinine.

SBA 1: B — Severe malaria = IV artesunate + ICU.

SBA 2: B — Vivax features → chloroquine/ACT + primaquine 14d.

SBA 3: B — G6PD deficient = weekly primaquine 8w supervised.

SBA 4: B — 1st trimester = quinine + clindamycin.

SBA 5: B — Severe malaria in pregnancy = artesuate IV all trimesters.

SBA 6: B — Artesunate IV 2.4mg/kg same dose all ages.

SBA 7: B — P. knowlesi = 24h cycle, all stages, SE Asia.

SBA 8: B — Doxycycline prophylaxis failure → AL (different class).

SBA 9: B — Malariae = quartan 72h.

SBA 10: B — Falciparum + G6PD + oxidant = blackwater fever.

Status: Full FCPS/MRCP topic note completed — 2026-06-13

Topic Overview

1. Learning Objectives
2. Plasmodium Species
3. Clinical Features
Uncomplicated Malaria
Severe Malaria (WHO Criteria) — P. falciparum predominantly
4. Diagnosis
Gold Standard: Microscopy (Thick + Thin Blood Films)
Rapid Diagnostic Tests (RDTs)

5. Treatment
Uncomplicated P. falciparum (WHO/UK Guidelines)
Uncomplicated Non-Falciparum (P. vivax, P. ovale, P. malariae)
Chloroquine Dose (if sensitive)

Adjunctive Management of Severe Malaria

topics.title: Mpox And Emerging Viral Infections
topics.html:
[!info] Davidson Ch 11 Alignment: Infectious Disease → Specific Organism Groups → Viruses → Emerging and Re-emerging Infections
FCPS/MRCP Focus: 2022 global outbreak (Clade IIb), clinical features, transmission, vaccination (MVA-BN), IPC, differential from chickenpox/smallpox

1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Describe Mpox virology: Orthopoxvirus, Clade I (Central Africa, severe) vs Clade II (West Africa, milder); Clade IIb = 2022 outbreak
  • [ ] Recognise clinical features: Prodrome (fever, lymphadenopathy) → centrifugal rash (face, extremities, palms/soles, genital/perianal)
  • [ ] Differentiate from chickenpox (centripetal, crops, no lymphadenopathy) and smallpox (eradicated, centrifugal, severe)
  • [ ] Diagnose: PCR (lesion swab - gold standard), antigen, electron microscopy
  • [ ] Manage: Supportive, Tecovirimat (severe/immunocompromised), Vaccination (MVA-BN - pre/post-exposure), IPC
  • [ ] Know Nipah, Hendra (henipaviruses): Bat-borne, encephalitis, respiratory, high mortality

2. 📚 Mpox (Monkeypox)

Virology & Clades

CladeFormer NameRegionSeverityCFR
Clade ICongo BasinCentral AfricaSevere10-15%
Clade IIaWest AfricaWest AfricaMilder<1-3%
Clade IIb2022 Outbreak lineageGlobal (2022-)Mild-moderate<0.1%

Transmission (2022 Outbreak - Clade IIb)

  • Close contact: Sexual networks (MSM predominant), skin-to-skin, mucosal contact
  • Fomites: Contaminated bedding, towels, surfaces
  • Respiratory: Prolonged face-to-face (less efficient)
  • Vertical: Mother to fetus (congenital mpox)
  • Animal: Rodents, primates (endemic areas)

Clinical Features

PhaseTimelineFeatures
Incubation5-21 days (median 7)Asymptomatic
Prodrome1-5 days before rashFever, lymphadenopathy (cervical, inguinal, submandibular - KEY), headache, myalgia, back pain, asthenia
Rash1-4 days after feverCentrifugal (face → extremities → palms/soles), synchronous (same stage), deep-seated, umbilicated, pseudopustular → crusts
Genital/PerianalProminent in 2022 outbreakPainful lesions, proctitis, urethritis, rectal pain, tenesmus
Resolution2-4 weeksCrusts fall off, scarring, hypo/hyperpigmentation

Complications

  • Secondary bacterial infection, pneumonia, encephalitis, corneal scarring, sepsis
  • Immunocompromised (HIV/CD4<200): Severe, disseminated, atypical, prolonged
  • Pregnancy: Fetal loss, preterm, congenital mpox
  • Children: Higher severity historically

3. 🔬 Diagnosis

TestDetails
PCR (lesion swab)Gold standard: Orthopoxvirus generic + Mpox-specific (Clade I/II); lesion roof/fluid/crust
Antigen detectionRapid tests (lower sensitivity); not for definitive diagnosis
Serology (IgM/IgG)Retrospective; cross-reacts with other orthopoxviruses/vaccinia
Electron microscopy"Brick-shaped" virions; reference labs
Viral cultureBSL-3; not routine
[!tip] Exam Pearl: Lymphadenopathy = key differentiator from smallpox/chickenpox. PCR lesion swab = diagnostic standard.

4. 💊 Management

Supportive Care (Most Cases)

  • Analgesia, antipyretics, hydration, wound care (antiseptic, prevent secondary infection)
  • Isolation until all crusts fallen off + fresh skin (typically 2-4 weeks)

Antivirals (Severe/High-Risk)

DrugIndicationRegimen
Tecovirimat (TPOXX)First-line: Severe disease, immunocompromised, children <8, pregnancy, ocular/genital involvement, atypical600 mg PO bd × 14 days (weight-based IV if unable oral)
Cidofovir / BrincidofovirRefractory / tecovirimat unavailableNephrotoxicity risk (cidofovir); brincidofovir better tolerated

Vaccination (MVA-BN / Jynneos / Imvanex)

StrategyScheduleNotes
Pre-exposure (PrEP)2 doses SC, 28 days apartLab workers, healthcare workers, high-risk MSM
Post-exposure (PEP)Single dose within 4 days (up to 14 days)Reduces severity; ideally <4d
Immunocompromised (HIV CD4<200)3 doses (0, 28, 56 days)Reduced immunogenicity
[!warning] Contraindications: Live vaccines (ACAM2000) contraindicated in immunocompromised, pregnancy, eczema. MVA-BN is non-replicating = safe.

Infection Prevention & Control

  • Isolation: Airborne + contact + standard precautions (negative pressure if aerosol-generating)
  • PPE: Gown, gloves, N95/FFP2, eye protection
  • Environmental: EPA-registered disinfectants (QAT, bleach), laundry handling
  • Duration: Until all lesions crusted, crusts separated, fresh skin intact

5. 📋 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
CladesClade I (Central Africa, severe); Clade IIb (2022 global, MSM networks, milder)
Key clinicalLymphadenopathy (vs smallpox/chickenpox), centrifugal rash, palms/soles, genital
DiagnosisPCR lesion swab (roof/fluid/crust)
TreatmentSupportive; Tecovirimat for severe/high-risk
VaccineMVA-BN (Jynneos) - non-replicating, 2 doses PrEP, 1 dose PEP
IPCIsolation until crusts off + fresh skin; airborne/contact precautions

6. 🦠 Other Emerging Viral Infections (High-Yield)

Nipah Virus (Henipavirus)

FeatureDetails
ReservoirPteropus fruit bats (flying foxes)
TransmissionBat → human (date palm sap), pig → human (Malaysia), human-to-human (Bangladesh/India)
ClinicalEncephalitis (fever, headache, drowsiness, confusion, coma), respiratory (cough, ARDS)
Mortality40-75%
DiagnosisRT-PCR (CSF, blood, throat), IgM ELISA, virus isolation (BSL-4)
TreatmentSupportive; Ribavirin (in vitro, limited human data); Monoclonal antibody (m102.4) experimental
OutbreaksMalaysia 1998 (pigs), Bangladesh/India annual (date palm sap), Kerala 2018/2021/2023

Hendra Virus (Henipavirus)

FeatureDetails
ReservoirPteropus bats
TransmissionBat → horse → human (equine respiratory secretions)
ClinicalEncephalitis or severe respiratory (few human cases, high CFR ~60%)
GeographyAustralia (Queensland, NSW)
TreatmentSupportive; m102.4 monoclonal antibody (compassionate)
VaccineEquivac HeV (horse vaccine - One Health)

Other Emerging/Re-emerging

VirusKey Points
Novel CoronavirusesSARS-CoV-2, MERS-CoV; zoonotic, respiratory, pandemic potential
Avian Influenza (H5N1, H7N9)Poultry exposure, severe pneumonia, high mortality, limited human-to-human
Crimean-Congo HFTick-borne (Hyalomma), nosocomral, ribavirin
Rift Valley FeverMosquito-borne, livestock, haemorrhagic/ocular/encephalitic forms
ZikaMosquito/sexual, microcephaly, GBS
ChikungunyaMosquito, severe arthralgia (chronic), no specific Rx
OropoucheMidge/mosquito, Amazon, febrile, neuroinvasive

7. ❓ Viva Questions (FCPS/MRCP)

  1. Differentiate Mpox from chickenpox and smallpox.
  2. What are the clades of Mpox? Which caused the 2022 outbreak?
  3. Describe the rash of Mpox. Where is it distributed?
  4. What is the diagnostic test of choice for Mpox?
  5. When do you use tecovirimat for Mpox?
  6. Describe MVA-BN vaccine schedule for PrEP and PEP.
  7. A patient returns from Kerala with fever, encephalitis, and pig contact. Diagnosis?
  8. What is the reservoir for Nipah virus? How does transmission occur?
  9. Why is ACAM2000 contraindicated in immunocompromised patients?
  10. What IPC precautions for suspected Mpox?

8. 🧠 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Mpox vs ChickenpoxMpox: Lymphadenopathy, centrifugal, synchronous, deep/umbilicated, palms/soles; Chickenpox: No lymphadenopathy, centripetal, crops, superficial, spares palms/soles
Mpox vs SmallpoxSmallpox: Eradicated, no lymphadenopathy, more severe, centrifugal; Mpox: Lymphadenopathy, milder, zoonotic
Clade I vs Clade IIClade I: Central Africa, severe, higher CFR, community spread; Clade II: West Africa, milder, Clade IIb = 2022 sexual network spread
MVA-BN vs ACAM2000MVA-BN: Non-replicating, safe in immunocompromised/HIV/eczema/pregnancy; ACAM2000: Live replicating, contraindicated above

Mnemonic - Mpox: "MPOX" → Monkeypox, Prodrome (fever + lymphadenopathy), Orthopoxvirus (Clade I/II), X (sex - 2022 MSM spread), Centrifugal rash, Rash synchronous/P59; Tecovirimat severe; MVA-BN vaccine

Mnemonic - Nipah: "NIPAH" → Nipah, India/Bangladesh (date palm), Pteropus bats, Ancephalitis + respiratory, High mortality (40-75%), Ribavirin/m102.4 experimental

9. 🗺️ Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Emerging Viral Infections))
    Mpox (Monkeypox)
      Clade I (Central Africa, severe)
      Clade IIa (West Africa)
      Clade IIb (2022 global, MSM)
      Lymphadenopathy (KEY)
      Centrifugal rash, palms/soles
      PCR lesion swab
      Tecovirimat (severe)
      MVA-BN vaccine (non-replicating)
    Henipaviruses
      Nipah
        Pteropus bats
        Date palm sap / pigs
        Encephalitis + respiratory
        Kerala outbreaks
      Hendra
        Pteropus → horses → humans
        Australia
        Equivac HeV (horse vaccine)
    Other
      Novel coronaviruses
      Avian influenza (H5N1, H7N9)
      CCHF (ticks, ribavirin)
      Rift Valley Fever
      Zika (microcephaly)
      Chikungunya (chronic arthralgia)

10. 📄 One-Page Revision Card

MpoxKey Facts
VirusOrthopoxvirus; Clade I (severe), Clade IIb (2022 outbreak)
TransmissionClose contact (sexual networks), fomites, respiratory
ClinicalLymphadenopathy (KEY), centrifugal rash, palms/soles, genital
DiagnosisPCR lesion swab (gold standard)
TreatmentSupportive; Tecovirimat 600mg bd × 14d (severe/high-risk)
VaccineMVA-BN: 2 doses PrEP (28d), 1 dose PEP (<4d ideal)
IPCAirborne + contact; isolate until crusts off + fresh skin
NipahKey Facts
ReservoirPteropus fruit bats
TransmissionDate palm sap, pigs, human-to-human
ClinicalEncephalitis + respiratory; CFR 40-75%
DiagnosisRT-PCR (CSF/blood), IgM ELISA
TreatmentSupportive; m102.4 mAb experimental

11. 📊 Spaced Repetition Tracker

Review IntervalDateScore (1-5)Notes
24 hours
7 days
15 days
30 days
90 days

12. 🧪 Self-Test Scorecard

TopicKnown? (✓/✗)Last Reviewed
